name: PROCESSING OF CEREALS, PULSES AND OILSEEDS

topics.title: Status of Grain Processing Industries in India: Rice Varieties and Nutrient Composition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT403 - PROCESSING OF CEREALS, PULSES AND OILSEEDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Status of Grain Processing Industries in India: Rice Varieties and Nutrient Composition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Grain Processing Industry – Formal Definition

**Grain Processing Industry** is defined as the aggregate of agro-industrial operations that transform raw cereal grains (such as rice, wheat, maize, millets, sorghum, and barley), pulses, and oilseeds into edible finished or semi-finished products through a sequence of cleaning, dehusking, milling, separating, grading, polishing, parboiling, and packaging operations.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** parlance (PCFTT403), the grain processing sector is recognized as the second-largest food processing segment in India, contributing approximately **25–30% of the total food processing industry output** and employing a workforce in the range of **15–18 million** (organized + unorganized sectors combined).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Anchor Statement:**
> *Cereals constitute the staple food of over 65% of the Indian population, providing nearly 50% of the dietary calories and 25% of the dietary protein. Their post-harvest processing defines food security, nutritional adequacy, and economic value addition.*

### 1.2 Status of Grain Processing Industries in India

| Sub-Sector | Status / Scale (Indicative) | Key Insights |
|---|---|---|
| Rice Milling | **Largest** organized grain sector; ~40,000+ rice mills | Dominated by unorganized sector in eastern India |
| Wheat Milling (Flour) | ~800 large roller flour mills + 2,500+ chakki units | High CAGR (6–8%) due to bakery & convenience foods |
| Pulse Processing | ~14,000 dal mills; 25% dal imported | High post-harvest losses (15–20%) |
| Oilseed Crushing | ~150 solvent extraction plants | India is the **largest importer** of edible oils (~60% gap) |
| Maize/Millet Processing | Growing, especially millets (2023 = IYM) | Starch, ethanol, breakfast cereal verticals rising |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Institutional Stakeholders (KTU Mentions):**
> - **FCI** – Food Corporation of India (procurement & buffer stocking)
> - **CFTRI** – Central Food Technological Research Institute, Mysuru (R&D)
> - **IARI** – Indian Agricultural Research Institute, New Delhi (variety development)
> - **APEDA** – Agricultural & Processed Food Products Export Development Authority
> - **MPKV, PAU, GBPUAT** – State agricultural universities for varietal trials

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Think of a **grain processing industry** like a **massive car assembly line in an automobile factory**:
- The **raw grain** (paddy, wheat) is the "chassis" entering the plant.
- Each **machine** (cleaner, de-stoner, husker, polisher, grader) is a "workstation" that performs one specific transformation.
- The **finished product** (white rice, atta, semolina, daliya) is the polished car that drives off the assembly line.
- The **by-products** (husk, bran, germ) are the metal scrap that is sold to other industries (cattle feed, biofuel, oil extraction).

This analogy helps a KTU student visualize why **unit operations** in grain processing are sequential and why the **degree of milling** determines the nutritional and economic value of the end product.

### 1.4 Rice – Definition and Global Position

**Rice (*Oryza sativa* L.)** is a monocotyledonous cereal grain belonging to the family **Poaceae (Gramineae)**. It is the **staple food of approximately 3.5 billion people globally** and contributes around **20% of the global dietary energy supply**.

In India, rice occupies:
- **Area under cultivation:** ~43–45 million hectares (largest in the world).
- **Annual production:** ~130–135 million tonnes (MT) – India is the **2nd largest producer** (after China) and the **largest exporter** of rice globally.
- **States dominating production:** West Bengal, Uttar Pradesh, Punjab, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Bihar, Assam, Chhattisgarh, Odisha.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Rice – Stepwise Logical Breakdown

Rice can be classified based on **three independent criteria**, which is a frequently tested KTU topic:

#### A. Botanical / Taxonomic Classification
1. **Oryza sativa** – the cultivated species (Asian rice); accounts for nearly 100% of global production.
2. **Oryza glaberrima** – African rice; cultivated only in West Africa.

#### B. Geographical / Ecogeographic Subspecies
| Subspecies | Region | Grain Traits | Plant Traits |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Indica** (long-grain) | Tropical & subtropical Asia, India, SE Asia | Long, slender, fluffy, non-sticky | Tall, leafy, photoperiod-sensitive |
| **Japonica** (short/round grain) | Temperate zones – Japan, Korea, California, Egypt | Short, round, sticky when cooked | Short, non-sensitive to day length |
| **Javanica** (medium grain) | Indonesia, Madagascar | Bold, medium length | Intermediate traits |

#### C. Commercial / Indian Market Classification
1. **Basmati Rice** – Premium aromatic, long-grain (e.g., Pusa Basmati 1, 1121, 1509, Taraori).
2. **Non-Basmati Long-Grain** (e.g., Sona Masuri, IR-64, Krishnaveni).
3. **Medium-Grain Rice** (e.g., Improved Samba Mahsuri).
4. **Short / Bold-Grain Rice** (e.g., HMT, Ratnachudi, Kolam).
5. **Glutinous / Waxy Rice** (e.g., Black rice, sticky rice – Chhattisgarh, Manipur).
6. **Parboiled Rice** (e.g., Improved Ponni, IR-36 parboiled).
7. **Red Rice & Pigmented Rice** (e.g., Karupatkavuni, Athaghai – rich in anthocyanins).

> [!NOTE]
> **Amylose–Amylopectin Ratio** is the biochemical key determining rice texture:
> - **High amylose (≥25%)** → Fluffy, non-sticky (typical Basmati, Indica).
> - **Low amylose (10–20%)** → Tender, slightly sticky (Japonica).
> - **Glutinous (waxy) rice** → Amylose <2% → Sticky when cooked.

### 2.2 Nutrient Composition of Rice – The KTU High-Yield Data Sheet

The following comparative table is **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU 2024 ESE questions (2-mark and 14-mark):

| Nutrient | Brown Rice (per 100 g) | White Rice (Raw, Milled) (per 100 g) | Parboiled Rice (per 100 g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy (kcal) | 360–370 | **345–360** | 350–360 |
| Moisture (%) | 12.0 | 12.0 | 12.0 |
| Protein (g) | 7.5–8.5 | **6.5–7.5** | 7.0–8.0 |
| Fat (g) | 2.5–2.8 | 0.5–0.8 | 0.6–0.9 |
| Carbohydrate (g) | 76–78 | **78–80** | 77–79 |
| Crude Fiber (g) | 1.0–1.2 | 0.3–0.5 | 0.5–0.7 |
| Ash (g) | 1.2–1.5 | 0.5–0.7 | 0.7–0.9 |
| Iron (mg) | 1.5–2.0 | 0.5–0.7 | 1.0–1.3 |
| Calcium (mg) | 20–25 | 8–10 | 10–15 |
| Thiamine (B1) (mg) | 0.30–0.40 | **0.06–0.08** | 0.20–0.25 |
| Niacin (B3) (mg) | 4.0–4.5 | 1.0–1.4 | 3.0–3.5 |
| Lysine (g/16g N) | 3.8 | 3.5 | 3.6 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Brown Rice > White Rice nutritionally?**
> The **aleurone layer** (rich in B-vitamins, minerals, and dietary fiber) and the **germ** (lipids, vitamin E, essential amino acids) are **retained in brown rice** but are **removed during conventional milling** to produce white rice. This is the single most repeated KTU factual question.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet – Quantitative Tools

The following formulas are routinely tested for **numerical/diagram-based 14-mark KTU questions**:

$$
\text{Head Rice Yield (HRY)} \ (\%) = \frac{\text{Weight of whole milled grains (g)}}{\text{Weight of paddy input (g)}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Total Rice Yield (TRY)} \ (\%) = \frac{\text{Weight of total milled grains (whole + broken) (g)}}{\text{Weight of paddy input (g)}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Degree of Milling (DOM)} \ (\%) = \frac{\text{Lipid removed from brown rice}}{\text{Lipid present in brown rice}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Milling Recovery} \ (\%) = \frac{\text{Total milled rice (g)}}{\text{Paddy weight (g)}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Nutrient Retention (NR)} \ (\%) = \frac{\text{Nutrient content in processed rice}}{\text{Nutrient content in brown rice}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Broken \%} = \frac{\text{Weight of broken grains (g)}}{\text{Total milled rice weight (g)}} \times 100
$$

> **Standard Industry Reference Values (KTU Examiner Frequently Quotes):**
> - **HRY for premium Basmati (Pusa 1121):** 60–65%
> - **HRY for IR-64 (parboiled):** 70–75%
> - **Milling recovery (paddy to rice, conventional):** 65–70%
> - **Milling recovery (paddy to rice, parboiled):** 72–78%
> - **Husk fraction:** 20–22%
> - **Bran fraction:** 8–10%
> - **Germ fraction:** 1–2%

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas drive:
- **Procurement pricing** at FCI mandis (the higher the HRY, the higher the price).
- **Mill design** (satake, Buhler, Satake India, Fowler Westrup).
- **Storage policy** (parboiled rice gives better HRY retention during long storage).
- **Export compliance** (Basmati export specifications mandate HRY ≥ 63% with broken ≤ 2%).
- **Nutritional fortification programs** (e.g., Fe-fortified rice – loss of Fe during milling must be compensated at 50–100%).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation – Rice Milling Mass Balance

The rice milling mass balance is the **most common numerical question** in KTU 2024 ESE Module 1. Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step derivation**.

**Given:** A modern rice mill processes **1,000 kg of clean paddy** (variety: IR-64) with the following experimentally determined composition:
- Husk = 21%
- Bran + Germ = 9%
- Moisture = 13% (in paddy)
- Broken grains in milled rice output = 8% of milled rice weight

**Find:** (a) Total milled rice (kg), (b) Head rice (kg), (c) Husk, (d) Bran, (e) Milling recovery.

**Step 1 – Identify total dry matter (DM) input.**

$$
\text{DM}_{paddy} = 1000 \times \left(1 - \frac{13}{100}\right) = 1000 \times 0.87 = 870 \ kg
$$

**Step 2 – Husk fraction calculation (by mass balance).**

$$
\text{Husk} = 1000 \times \frac{21}{100} = 210 \ kg
$$

*Explanation:* The husk (or hull) is the outermost chaffy layer that is removed by rubber roll shellers or impeller-type huskers. For a typical Indica variety, husk forms 18–22% of the rough paddy mass.

**Step 3 – Bran + germ fraction calculation.**

$$
\text{Bran} = 1000 \times \frac{9}{100} = 90 \ kg
$$

*Explanation:* Bran is the layer immediately beneath the husk, composed of pericarp, seed coat, aleurone, and a portion of the embryo. In modern horizontal abrasive polishers, ~8–10% of paddy mass is removed as bran.

**Step 4 – Total milled rice (TRY).**

$$
\text{Milled Rice} = \text{Paddy} - \text{Husk} - \text{Bran}
$$

$$
\text{Milled Rice} = 1000 - 210 - 90 = 700 \ kg
$$

**Step 5 – Milling recovery (TRY %).**

$$
\text{TRY} \ (\%) = \frac{700}{1000} \times 100 = 70.0 \%
$$

*Explanation:* This is the classic **70% milling recovery benchmark** used by FCI and Indian rice industry standards.

**Step 6 – Broken grains quantification.**

$$
\text{Broken} = 700 \times \frac{8}{100} = 56 \ kg
$$

**Step 7 – Head rice (HRY) calculation.**

$$
\text{Head Rice} = \text{Total Milled Rice} - \text{Broken}
$$

$$
\text{Head Rice} = 700 - 56 = 644 \ kg
$$

**Step 8 – Head rice yield (HRY %).**

$$
\text{HRY} \ (\%) = \frac{644}{1000} \times 100 = 64.4 \%
$$

**Step 9 – Sanity check (mass balance closure).**

$$
1000 = 210 \ (\text{husk}) + 90 \ (\text{bran}) + 644 \ (\text{head rice}) + 56 \ (\text{broken}) = 1000 \ kg \ \checkmark
$$

**Step 10 – Nutrient retention computation (analytical extension).**

Assume brown rice contains 0.6 mg Fe / 100 g. After milling, Fe content drops to 0.6 mg / 100 g in white rice, but the *total Fe in 100 g paddy-equivalent basis* changes.

$$
\text{Fe in paddy equivalent} = 0.6 \times \frac{700}{1000} = 0.42 \ mg/100g \ paddy
$$

$$
\text{Nutrient Retention (Fe)} \ (\%) = \frac{0.42}{0.6} \times 100 = 70 \%
$$

This corresponds to the well-known **30% Fe loss** during conventional milling.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Comparison of Indian Rice Varieties

The following matrix is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** ("Compare the physical, biochemical, and processing characteristics of 4–5 major Indian rice varieties"):

| Parameter | Basmati 1121 | Sona Masuri | IR-64 | Improved Samba Mahsuri | Black Rice (Chhattisgarh) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Grain type | Extra-long slender | Medium slender | Long slender | Medium bold | Short bold |
| Kernel length (mm) | **>8.0** | 5.4–5.8 | 6.0–6.4 | 5.0–5.4 | 4.5–5.0 |
| L/B ratio | **>4.0** | 3.0–3.2 | 3.0–3.5 | 2.5–2.8 | 2.0–2.3 |
| Amylose (%) | 22–25 | 22–24 | 24–26 | 22–24 | 1–5 (waxy) |
| Aroma | Strong (2-AP) | Mild | None | None | Nutty/earthy |
| HRY (%) | 60–65 | 65–68 | 68–72 | 66–70 | 55–60 |
| Elongation ratio on cooking | 2.0–2.5 | 1.6–1.8 | 1.4–1.6 | 1.3–1.5 | 1.2–1.3 |
| Gelatinization temp (°C) | 70–75 | 65–70 | 65–70 | 65–70 | 60–65 |
| GI (Glycemic Index) | 50–58 | 65–72 | 70–75 | 70–75 | 50–60 |
| Key export market | Middle East, EU, USA | Domestic + diaspora | Government PDS | South India | Niche/health foods |
| Release year / Institute | IARI, 2002 | APAU, 1970s | IRRI/CRRI, 1990s | IIRR, 2008 | Indigenous landrace |

> [!NOTE]
> **2-AP (2-Acetyl-1-Pyrroline)** is the principal volatile aromatic compound responsible for the characteristic "Basmati" fragrance. Its concentration in genuine Basmati is 100–500 ppb.

### 3.3 Estimation of India's Annual Rice Processing Volume

**Step 1 – Annual paddy production.**

$$
P_{paddy} = 130 \ MT = 130 \times 10^6 \ tonnes
$$

**Step 2 – Convert paddy to rice using 70% milling recovery.**

$$
P_{rice} = 130 \times 10^6 \times 0.70 = 91 \ MT = 9.1 \times 10^7 \ tonnes
$$

**Step 3 – By-product generation.**

$$
\text{Husk} = 130 \times 10^6 \times 0.21 = 27.3 \ MT
$$

$$
\text{Bran} = 130 \times 10^6 \times 0.09 = 11.7 \ MT
$$

**Step 4 – Economic value (indicative).**

$$
\text{Rice value} = 9.1 \times 10^7 \times ₹30,000/\text{tonne} = ₹2.73 \ \text{lakh crore}
$$

$$
\text{Husk value (cogeneration)} = 27.3 \times 10^6 \times ₹2,000/\text{tonne} = ₹5,460 \ crore
$$

$$
\text{Bran (oil extraction)} = 11.7 \times 10^6 \times ₹18,000/\text{tonne} = ₹21,060 \ crore
$$

> This exercise demonstrates the **engineering-economic significance** of rice processing in India's GDP contribution (food processing alone is ~12% of manufacturing GVA).

### 4.4 Nutrient Distribution Across Rice Grain Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OuterLayer[Outer Husk Hull] -->|20-22%| L1[Insoluble Fiber Silica]
    L1 --> InnerLayer[Pericarp Seed Coat]
    InnerLayer -->|1-2%| L2[Tannins Pigments in red rice]
    L2 --> AleuroneLayer[Aleurone Layer]
    AleuroneLayer -->|3-5%| L3[Proteins B Vitamins Minerals]
    L3 --> Endosperm[Starchy Endosperm]
    Endosperm -->|65-70%| L4[Starch Amylose Amylopectin]
    L4 --> Germ[Embryo Germ]
    Germ -->|1-2%| L5[Lipids Vitamin E Minerals]
    OuterLayer --> REM1[Removed during dehulling]
    InnerLayer --> REM2[Removed during polishing]
    AleuroneLayer --> REM3[Lost in over milling]
    Endosperm --> KEEP[Retained as white rice]
    Germ --> REM4[Lost in milling]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]

> The Mermaid schematics above map the **physical, processing, regulatory, and nutritional** dimensions of rice processing. These diagrams are direct visual cues for KTU 14-mark "explain with neat diagram" type questions, and they directly fetch the **2-mark diagram allotment** in valuation.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Discuss the status of rice milling industry in India.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The rice milling industry is the **largest organized grain processing sector** in India, with an installed capacity to mill over **130 million tonnes (MT)** of paddy annually. Key features include:
- **~40,000 rice mills** operational, of which **65% are in the unorganized sector** using traditional huller mills.
- India produces both **white rice and parboiled rice**, with parboiled rice accounting for ~50% of total output, predominantly in eastern and southern states.
- Modern mills (Satake, Buhler, Fowler Westrup) employ **multi-stage processing** (cleaning → de-stoning → dehusking → paddy separation → polishing → grading → color sorting) achieving HRY of **65–70%**.
- The industry contributes significantly to exports – India is the **world's largest rice exporter** (~17–22 MT annually).
- Major challenges: **energy intensity, broken rice percentage, value addition at village level, and under-utilization of bran/ husk**.

> **Mark Split:** 1 mark definition + 1 mark scale + 1 mark challenges = 3 marks.

#### **Q2. Differentiate between brown rice, white rice, and parboiled rice in terms of nutrient composition.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Attribute | Brown Rice | White Rice (Raw) | Parboiled Rice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fiber (g/100g) | 1.0–1.2 | 0.3–0.5 | 0.5–0.7 |
| Thiamine (mg) | 0.30–0.40 | 0.06–0.08 | 0.20–0.25 |
| Fat (g) | 2.5–2.8 | 0.5–0.8 | 0.6–0.9 |
| Iron (mg) | 1.5–2.0 | 0.5–0.7 | 1.0–1.3 |
| Glycemic Index | Medium | High | Medium-Low |

> **Mark Split:** 0.5 mark heading + 1.5 marks table + 1 mark conclusion = 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module 1 Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Discuss the present status of grain processing industries in India, highlighting the role of rice, wheat, and pulse processing sectors. Enumerate the key institutional bodies that govern these sectors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify the major rice varieties grown in India, and explain in detail the nutritional and biochemical differences between them with reference to **amylose content, gelatinization temperature, and head rice yield**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution – Question A**

**(a) Status of grain processing industries in India (7 marks):**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** The Indian grain processing industry is a ₹5+ lakh crore sector, the second-largest food processing vertical, contributing 12–15% to manufacturing GVA.

2. **Rice milling sector (2 marks):**
   - Largest grain sub-sector with 40,000+ mills; produces 91–95 MT of rice.
   - Mix of traditional huller mills (unorganized, 65%) and modern multistage mills (organized, 35%).
   - 70% milling recovery benchmark; export leader globally.

3. **Wheat milling sector (1.5 marks):**
   - ~800 large roller flour mills + thousands of *chakki* (stone-grinding) units.
   - Produces atta, maida, suji, rawa; rising demand for multigrain and specialty flours.

4. **Pulse processing sector (1.5 marks):**
   - ~14,000 dal mills, predominantly unorganized.
   - Pigeon pea, chickpea, black gram, green gram processed.
   - Losses of 15–20% during dehulling; significant import dependency (~25%).

5. **Institutional bodies (1 mark):** FCI (procurement), APEDA (export), FSSAI (safety), CFTRI (R&D), IARI, MPKV, PAU, IIMR, IIPR.

**(b) Rice varieties & nutritional/biochemical differences (7 marks):**

1. **Classification (2 marks):** Botanical (O. sativa, O. glaberrima), ecogeographic (Indica, Japonica, Javanica), commercial (Basmati, Non-Basmati, Glutinous, Pigmented).

2. **Indica long-grain varieties (2 marks):** Basmati 1121 (amylose 22–25%, L/B >4, GI 50–58, strong 2-AP aroma); Sona Masuri (amylose 22–24%, mild aroma, premium south Indian rice); IR-64 (amylose 24–26%, high-yielding, government PDS).

3. **Biochemical differences (2 marks):**
   - **Amylose content** dictates texture: high amylose → fluffy; low amylose → sticky.
   - **Gelatinization temperature** affects cooking time: 65–75°C typical for Indica.
   - **Head rice yield:** Basmati 60–65%, IR-64 parboiled 70–75% (parboiling increases HRY).
   - **Glycemic index** varies from 50 (Basmati) to 80 (sticky waxy rice).

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** India’s varietal portfolio supports both nutritional diversity and export competitiveness.

> **Mark Split Annotation:** 7 marks = 2 marks intro/classification + 3 marks sub-parts + 2 marks conclusion/diagram. **Diagrams are MANDATORY** for full marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat process flow diagram, explain the **mass balance of rice milling** for a modern multistage rice mill processing **1,000 kg of paddy**. Assume typical recovery values for Indica paddy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **structural composition and nutrient distribution** of brown rice, milled white rice, and parboiled rice. Discuss why parboiled rice is nutritionally superior to raw milled rice. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution – Question B**

**(a) Rice milling mass balance (7 marks):**

**Step 1 – Diagram (2 marks):** Draw a labelled block diagram showing Paddy → Cleaner → Destoner → Husker → Paddy Separator → Polisher → Grader → Milled Rice. Mark by-product streams (husk, bran, broken).

**Step 2 – Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Husk = 21% of paddy weight
- Bran = 9% of paddy weight
- Moisture in paddy = 13%
- Broken grains = 8% of milled rice output

**Step 3 – Quantitative calculation (3 marks):**
- Husk = 1,000 × 0.21 = **210 kg**
- Bran = 1,000 × 0.09 = **90 kg**
- Total milled rice = 1,000 − 210 − 90 = **700 kg**
- Broken = 700 × 0.08 = **56 kg**
- Head rice = 700 − 56 = **644 kg**
- HRY = (644 / 1,000) × 100 = **64.4%**
- TRY = (700 / 1,000) × 100 = **70%**

**Step 4 – Mass balance closure (1 mark):**
210 (husk) + 90 (bran) + 56 (broken) + 644 (head) = **1,000 kg ✓**

**(b) Brown vs. White vs. Parboiled rice (7 marks):**

1. **Structural composition (2 marks):**
   - **Brown rice:** retains husk-removed (dehulled) grain with pericarp, aleurone, germ intact.
   - **White rice:** bran and germ polished away → starchy endosperm remains.
   - **Parboiled rice:** paddy soaked in hot water (60–70°C) → steamed → dried → milled. Process **drives nutrients from aleurone and germ into the endosperm** before milling.

2. **Nutrient distribution (3 marks):**
   - **Brown rice:** rich in B-vitamins (thiamine 0.30–0.40 mg), fiber (1.0–1.2 g), Fe (1.5–2.0 mg), but higher fat (2.5–2.8 g) → shorter shelf life.
   - **White rice:** low in fiber (0.3–0.5 g), thiamine (0.06–0.08 mg), Fe (0.5–0.7 mg), but high starch, longer shelf life, preferred texture.
   - **Parboiled rice:** intermediate fiber, **retains 70–80% thiamine** (vs. 15% in white rice), higher Ca, Mg, K due to inward diffusion of nutrients.

3. **Why parboiled is superior (1.5 marks):**
   - Heat treatment gelatinizes starch and **fuses nutrients into the endosperm**.
   - **Higher HRY** (70–75%) due to hardened endosperm.
   - **Lower glycemic index** (medium-low) compared to white rice (high GI).
   - **Resistant starch** formation during retrogradation → prebiotic benefits.
   - **Lower cooking losses** → less nutrient leaching.

4. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Parboiling is an effective, low-cost **nutrient fortification strategy** widely used in Indian FCI procurement.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls – Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Oryza" with "Triticum" (wheat).** Oryza sativa = rice; Triticum aestivum = bread wheat. Mixing them = 1 mark cut.
> 2. **Wrong units in mass balance:** Writing "HRY = 64.4" instead of "HRY = 64.4%". The "%" symbol is essential.
> 3. **Forgetting to draw diagrams:** A 14-mark question without a process flow or block diagram loses **2–3 marks** under "Presentation and Diagram" allowance.
> 4. **Writing "Husk = 70%" instead of 21%.** Always quote husk fraction (~20–22%) for paddy.
> 5. **Confusing "Basmati 1121" with "Pusa Basmati 1"** – they are different varieties with different release years.
> 6. **Ignoring parboiling advantages** – parboiling is a HIGH-YIELD KTU topic and at least 1 question is expected every ESE cycle.
> 7. **Writing "amylose" in white rice as if it's a fat** – amylose is a **starch polysaccharide**, not a lipid.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 1):**

- **Grain processing industry status in India:** 2nd largest food processing vertical; ₹5 lakh crore; ~15 million workforce.
- **Rice global position:** 2nd largest producer (130 MT), largest exporter; grown on 44 Mha.
- **Botanical classification:** *Oryza sativa* (indica, japonica, javanica); *Oryza glaberrima* (Africa).
- **Commercial Indian rice types:** Basmati, Sona Masuri, IR-64, Improved Samba Mahsuri, HMT, Kolam, Glutinous (Karupatkavuni), Pigmented (Black Rice / Chakhao).
- **Key biochemical parameter:** Amylose–amylopectin ratio → texture, GI, cooking quality.
- **Brown vs. white rice:** Brown retains aleurone + germ → higher B-vitamins, fiber, Fe, but shorter shelf life.
- **Parboiling advantages:** Nutrient retention (thiamine 70–80%), higher HRY, lower GI, resistant starch.
- **Key formulas (must memorize):** HRY, TRY, Milling Recovery, Nutrient Retention, Broken %, DOM.
- **Standard industry values:** Husk 20–22%, Bran 8–10%, Germ 1–2%, TRY 65–70%, HRY 60–75%.
- **Institutional bodies:** FCI, APEDA, FSSAI, CFTRI, IARI, PAU, IIMR (millets), IIPR (pulses).
- **2-AP compound** = Basmati aroma compound (concentration 100–500 ppb).
- **Glycemic Index ranking (lowest to highest):** Basmati < Parboiled < Sona Masuri < IR-64 < Glutinous.
- **By-product utilization:** Husk → cogeneration (biomass boilers); Bran → rice bran oil (γ-oryzanol rich); Broken → animal feed, flour, breakfast cereals.
- **Diagrams to master:** (i) Rice milling process flow; (ii) Mass balance block diagram; (iii) Rice grain cross-section with nutrient localization.

> **End of Module 1 Notes – Status of Grain Processing Industries in India: Rice Varieties and Nutrient Composition.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parboiling: Physicochemical Changes, Modern Methods of Parboiling, Rice Milling, Equipment Involved in Rice Milling Processing (Cleaner, Stoner, Husker, Separator, Whitening Machine)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT403 - PROCESSING OF CEREALS, PULSES AND OILSEEDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parboiling: Physicochemical Changes, Modern Methods of Parboiling, Rice Milling, Equipment Involved in Rice Milling Processing (Cleaner, Stoner, Husker, Separator, Whitening Machine)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Parboiling — Formal Academic Definition

**Parboiling** (also called *hydrothermal treatment* or *partial boiling*) is a precisely controlled pre-milling process in which rough rice (paddy, *Oryza sativa*) is subjected to sequential **soaking in hot water**, **steam pressure treatment (gelatinization)**, and **controlled drying**. The objective is to induce irreversible starch gelatinization within the endosperm so that the kernel becomes hardened, nutritionally enriched, and mechanically resistant to breakage during subsequent dehusking and whitening operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Parboiling is the single most important *value-addition* step in rice processing. It directly governs the **Head Rice Recovery (HRR)**, **milling yield**, and **nutritional profile** of the finished rice. Examiners frequently ask definitions (2 marks) and process flowcharts (7–14 marks).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a raw green almond versus a roasted one. The raw almond crumbles under pressure, while the roasted (heat-treated) one snaps cleanly and releases its flavor inward. Parboiling does the same thing to a rice grain:

- **Soaking** is like *marinating* — water penetrates the husk and bran layers.
- **Steaming** is like *pressure-cooking* — starch granules absorb water, swell, and burst open (gelatinize), then fuse with the surrounding protein matrix to form a *hard, glassy, translucent endosperm*.
- **Drying** is like *baking* — it stabilizes the structure, locking the nutrients inside.

The result is a kernel that looks amber/yellow, resists fracture, and carries vitamins (especially **Thiamine — Vitamin B1**) from the bran into the starchy endosperm.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Process Parameters (Industry Standards):**
> - Soaking Temperature: **65–75 °C** (traditional) / **80–85 °C** (modern CFTRI method)
> - Soaking Duration: **3–4 hours** (hot soak) / **24–36 hours** (cold soak, traditional)
> - Steaming Pressure: **1.0–1.5 kg/cm²** (10–15 psig) for **10–20 minutes**
> - Starch Gelatinization Temperature Range: **65–78 °C** (variety dependent)
> - Moisture after Steaming: **~30–35 % (wet basis)**
> - Final Moisture after Drying: **12–14 % (wet basis)** for safe storage
> - Gelatinization Enthalpy of Rice Starch: ~**15–20 J/g** (literature reference value, varies by cultivar)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physicochemical Changes During Parboiling

The three principal stages — **Soaking**, **Steaming (Gelatinization)**, and **Drying** — each induce specific physicochemical transformations.

#### A. Changes During Soaking

- **Hydration of endosperm** through the husk and bran micropores.
- **Leaching** of soluble solids, sugars, and amino acids into the soak water (a quality concern in traditional open-tank methods).
- **Moisture content** of the grain rises from **~12 %** to **~30–32 % (w.b.)**.
- **Activation of native enzymes** (α-amylase, protease) begins mild endogenous hydrolysis.

#### B. Changes During Steaming (The Critical Stage)

1. **Starch Gelatinization** — The hydrogen bonds holding the crystalline starch granule are disrupted. Water enters the granule, swelling it irreversibly. The amylose leaches out and, on cooling, retrogrades to form a **semi-crystalline gel matrix**.

2. **Protein Denaturation & Aggregation** — Albumin and globulin fractions coagulate. The protein matrix contracts, entrapping gelatinized starch and creating a **compact, glassy endosperm**.

3. **Pigment Migration (Maillard Reaction)** — Reducing sugars (glucose) react with free amino groups of proteins/lysine under heat to form **melanoidins**, imparting the characteristic **amber/yellow color** of parboiled rice.

4. **Nutrient Diffusion from Bran to Endosperm** — Water-soluble B-vitamins (B1, B3, B6) and minerals diffuse inward from the aleurone layer into the starchy endosperm. This is why parboiled rice is **nutritionally superior** to raw milled rice.

5. **Kernel Hardening & Volume Reduction** — Internal stresses collapse air spaces, giving the classic *translucent, glassy* appearance.

6. **Lipid-Bound Starch Complexes** — Phospholipids bind with amylose, reducing free lipid content.

#### C. Changes During Drying

- **Stress-cracking risk** if drying rate is too high (>2 % moisture loss per hour).
- **Retrogradation of amylose** sets the kernel structure permanently.
- Final moisture **12–14 %** is required for safe storage and efficient milling.

### 2.2 Modern Methods of Parboiling

| Method | Process Outline | Key Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CFTRI Method** | Hot soak (80–85 °C, 3 h) → Steam (5–10 min at 1 kg/cm²) → Drying | Nutrient retention, faster cycle | High capital cost |
| **Jadhav Method** | CSIR-CFTRI variant using a metal trough system; pre-heated water soak | Compact, suitable for small scale | Manual handling |
| **Pressure Parboiling** | Paddy soaked cold → Steam at 1.5–2.0 kg/cm² for 15–20 min | High HRR, complete gelatinization | Over-cooks if uncontrolled |
| **DRD Method (Double Retort Drum)** | Rotating drum under steam pressure | Uniform treatment | Energy-intensive |
| **Continuous Parboiling** | Conveyor-based automated soak–steam–dry line | Industrial scale, consistent quality | Very high capital |
| **Malek Process** | Soak → Steam → Tempering → Drying in a single vessel chain | Reduced microbial load | Requires trained operators |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The **CFTRI method** is the most frequently asked modern technique. Be ready to draw the **flow chart** and list its **3 stages** along with exact temperatures.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture Content (Wet Basis) | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | Process control |
| Moisture Content (Dry Basis) | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}$ | kg water/kg dry solid | Engineering calculations |
| Milling Yield | $Y = \dfrac{M_{rice}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100$ | % | Plant efficiency |
| Head Rice Recovery | $HRR = \dfrac{M_{whole\,kernels}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100$ | % | Quality index |
| Broken % | $B = 100 - HRR$ | % | Loss indicator |
| Degree of Polish | $DOP = \dfrac{M_{brown\,rice} - M_{polished}}{M_{brown\,rice}} \times 100$ | % | Whitening control |
| Gelatinization Degree | $\alpha = \dfrac{V_{sample} - V_{raw}}{V_{cooked} - V_{raw}}$ | dimensionless | Process verification |
| Soak Water Absorption Rate | $\dfrac{dM}{dt} = k (M_\infty - M_t)$ | %/h | Fickian diffusion model |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In MC calculations, NEVER mix wet-basis and dry-basis values in the same equation. Examiners specifically test this conversion.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Designing **soak tanks**, **steam retorts**, and **fluidized bed dryers** for rice processing plants.
- Optimizing **HRR** to maximize mill revenue (broken rice fetches ~50 % the price of head rice).
- **Nutritional fortification** strategy in food security programs (parboiled rice is used in ICDS mid-day meals in India).
- Reducing **post-harvest losses** — parboiled paddy can be stored longer than raw paddy due to inactivation of lipase enzymes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Water Absorption Kinetics During Soaking

Fick's Second Law governs moisture diffusion into the rice kernel (treated as a sphere of equivalent radius *r*).

$$
\frac{\partial M}{\partial t} = D \left( \frac{\partial^2 M}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r} \frac{\partial M}{\partial r} \right)
$$

**Step 1:** Apply boundary conditions.
- Initial: $M(r, 0) = M_0$
- Surface: $M(R, t) = M_\infty$ for $t > 0$
- Symmetry: $\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial r}\bigg|_{r=0} = 0$

**Step 2:** Non-dimensionalize using moisture ratio.

$$
MR = \frac{M_t - M_\infty}{M_0 - M_\infty}
$$

**Step 3:** Solution for long soaking time (first-term approximation).

$$
MR \approx \frac{6}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D t}{R^2}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the effective diffusion coefficient *D* experimentally.

$$
\ln(MR) = \ln\left(\frac{6}{\pi^2}\right) - \frac{\pi^2 D}{R^2} t
$$

**Step 5:** Plot $\ln(MR)$ versus *t*; slope $= -\dfrac{\pi^2 D}{R^2}$, hence:

$$
D = -\frac{\text{slope} \cdot R^2}{\pi^2}
$$

> **Engineering Logic:** This derivation enables prediction of the **minimum soaking time** required to reach the desired interior moisture (≥30 %) for complete gelatinization in the subsequent steaming stage.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Process Variable Matrix

| Process Variable | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on HRR | Effect on Color | Effect on Energy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Soak Temperature | $T_s$ | 65–85 °C | ↑ with $T_s$ up to optimum | Darker if too high | ↑ with $T_s$ |
| Soak Duration | $t_s$ | 2–4 h | ↑ with $t_s$ | Darker if too long | ↑ with $t_s$ |
| Steam Pressure | $P_{st}$ | 0.5–2.0 kg/cm² | ↑ with $P_{st}$ | Darker | ↑ with $P_{st}$ |
| Steaming Time | $t_{st}$ | 10–20 min | ↑ with $t_{st}$ | Darker | ↑ with $t_{st}$ |
| Drying Air Temp | $T_d$ | 40–60 °C | ↓ if too high (cracking) | Minor | ↑ with $T_d$ |
| Final Moisture | $M_f$ | 12–14 % w.b. | Optimum at ~13 % | — | ↓ if $M_f$ lower |

### 3.3 Sample Worked Numerical — Moisture Calculation

**Problem:** A paddy sample weighs **500 g** before drying and **425 g** after drying to constant weight. Calculate (a) Moisture content on wet basis, (b) Moisture content on dry basis, and (c) amount of water to be removed from **1 tonne** of paddy at 18 % MC (w.b.) to bring it to 13 % MC (w.b.).

**Solution (a):**

$$
MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 = \frac{500 - 425}{500} \times 100 = 15\,\%
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
MC_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{500 - 425}{425} \times 100 = 17.65\,\%
$$

**Solution (c):** Let $W_1$ = weight of paddy at 18 % MC, $W_2$ = weight at 13 % MC. Dry matter is constant:

$$
0.82 \times 1000 = 0.87 \times W_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; W_2 = 942.53\,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Water to remove} = 1000 - 942.53 = 57.47\,\text{kg}
$$

> **Logic Behind Each Step:** Wet-basis MC uses the *initial wet weight* as the denominator, while dry-basis uses only the *dry solid mass*. For material balance, the **dry matter** is the conserved quantity — never the wet weight.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Flow Diagram — Complete Parboiling Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Paddy Intake] --> B[Pre-Cleaning]
    B --> C[Soaking Tank Hot Water 70 to 85 C]
    C --> D[Steam Retort Pressure 1 to 1.5 kg per cm2]
    D --> E[Tempering Bin]
    E --> F[Mechanical Drying]
    F --> G[Conditioning Bin Moisture Equilibration]
    G --> H[Storage of Parboiled Paddy]
    H --> I[Rice Milling Line]
    I --> J[Cleaner]
    J --> K[Stoner]
    K --> L[Husker]
    L --> M[Paddy Separator]
    M --> N[Whitener]
    N --> O[Polisher]
    O --> P[Grader and Color Sorter]
    P --> Q[Finished White Rice]

    classDef stage fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef mill fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    class C,D,E,F,G stage
    class J,K,L,M,N,O,P mill
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Physicochemical Changes During Parboiling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Starch Granule Crystalline] --> B[Water Absorption Soak Stage]
    B --> C[Swollen Granule Amorphous]
    C --> D[Steam Heat 70 to 100 C]
    D --> E[Gelatinized Starch Amylose Leached]
    E --> F[Protein Denaturation]
    F --> G[Maillard Reaction Melanoidins]
    G --> H[Glassy Translucent Kernel]

    classDef input fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    class A input
    class B,C,D,E,F,G process
    class H output
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Modern Rice Milling Equipment Train

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Parboiled Paddy] --> Q1[Drum Pre Cleaner]
    Q1 --> Q2[Vibratory Cleaner]
    Q2 --> Q3[Magnetic Separator]
    Q3 --> Q4[Destoner Specific Gravity Table]
    Q4 --> R1[Rubber Roll Husker]
    R1 --> R2[Paddy Separator Indented Cylinder]
    R2 --> S1[Whitener Abrasive Type]
    R1 --> R2b[Unshelled Return to Husker]
    R2b --> R1
    S1 --> S2[Polisher Friction Type]
    S2 --> S3[Length Grader]
    S3 --> S4[Color Sorter]
    S4 --> T[Finished Rice]

    classDef clean fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    classDef mill fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef finish fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 clean
    class R1,R2,S1,S2 mill
    class S3,S4,T finish
```

### 4.4 Equipment Function Cross-Reference Matrix

| Equipment | Primary Function | Working Principle | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cleaner** | Removes dust, straw, immature grains, foreign matter | Sieving, aspiration, magnetic separation | Raw paddy with impurities | Cleaned paddy |
| **Stoner** | Separates stones/heavy impurities | Density difference + vibration on a deck with airflow | Cleaned paddy | Stone-free paddy |
| **Husker** | Removes outer husk | Friction / shear / impact (rubber rolls or discs) | Paddy | Brown rice + husk |
| **Separator** | Separates brown rice from unhulled paddy | Length/weight difference (indented cylinder or table) | Husker output | Pure brown rice + return paddy |
| **Whitening Machine** | Removes bran layer | Abrasion (grit) or friction (rubber) | Brown rice | White rice |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define parboiling. List any two physicochemical changes that occur during parboiling.

**Model Answer:**
Parboiling is a hydrothermal pre-milling treatment of paddy involving soaking in water, steaming under pressure (gelatinization), and drying. Two physicochemical changes:
1. **Starch gelatinization** — irreversible swelling and rupture of starch granules.
2. **Migration of water-soluble nutrients** (B-vitamins, minerals) from the bran into the endosperm.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the function of a paddy separator in a rice mill? Name one type.

**Model Answer:**
A paddy separator separates **unhulled paddy** from **brown rice** after husking, based on length or specific gravity differences. The **Indented Cylinder Separator** is the most commonly used type.

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### ✅ CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks) [Most Likely]

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** Parboiling is considered the most important value-addition step in rice processing.
**(a)** Describe the **CFTRI method** of parboiling with a neat flowchart and state its advantages. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain in detail the **physicochemical changes** that occur during soaking, steaming, and drying stages. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CFTRI Method — 7 marks**

The CFTRI (Central Food Technological Research Institute, Mysore) method is a modern, mechanized parboiling technique with three distinct stages:

| Stage | Operating Conditions | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Soaking | 80–85 °C hot water, 3 h | Achieve 30–32 % moisture (w.b.) |
| Steaming | 1.0–1.5 kg/cm² for 10–20 min | Complete starch gelatinization |
| Drying | 50–60 °C in mechanical dryer | Reduce moisture to 12–14 % (w.b.) |

**Advantages:** (i) Shorter cycle time, (ii) reduced microbial contamination, (iii) better nutrient retention, (iv) higher HRR, (v) uniform product quality, (vi) industrial scalability.

**Flowchart (text form):** Paddy → Hot soak tank → Steam retort → Tempering bin → Mechanical dryer → Conditioning → Parboiled paddy storage.

**(b) Physicochemical Changes — 7 marks**

| Stage | Key Change | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Soaking | Hydration, leaching | Endosperm swells; soluble nutrients partially lost |
| Steaming | Gelatinization (65–78 °C), protein denaturation, Maillard reaction | Glassy endosperm, amber color, vitamin migration inward |
| Drying | Amylose retrogradation, stress-cracking risk | Hardened translucent kernel, stable for storage |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) failing to mention the **temperature ranges** numerically, (2) omitting **Maillard reaction** when explaining color change, and (3) forgetting **nutrient migration** — examiners specifically award marks for the nutritional dimension of parboiling.

---

#### ✅ CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative]

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2024]** Rice milling involves a sequence of unit operations.
**(a)** Draw a complete rice milling flow chart and explain the working of the **cleaner, stoner, and husker**. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe the working principle and construction of the **paddy separator** and **whitening machine**. Compare **abrasive** and **friction-type** whiteners. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Flow chart + Cleaner, Stoner, Husker — 7 marks**

Flow: Paddy → **Cleaner** → **Stoner** → **Husker** → **Separator** → **Whitener** → **Polisher** → **Grader** → Finished rice.

- **Cleaner:** A **drum-type** or **vibratory screen cleaner** removes dust, straw, immature grains, and stones larger/smaller than paddy. Uses sieves, aspiration fan, and magnetic separators for ferrous impurities. Throughput: 2–6 tonnes/h.
- **Stoner:** A **destoning table** (inclined, oscillating deck) uses **upward airflow** and **vibration** to stratify material by density — heavier stones settle to the bottom and are discharged separately, lighter paddy floats upward and moves forward. Removes stones ≥2 mm.
- **Husker:** A **rubber roll sheller** is preferred. Two rubber rolls rotating at different speeds (e.g., 800 and 600 rpm; differential ~25 %) nip and shear the husk off. Husk is removed by aspiration. Capacity: 1–3 t/h per pair. Alternative: **Under-runner disc sheller** (older design, lower HRR).

**(b) Separator and Whitener — 7 marks**

- **Paddy Separator (Indented Cylinder Type):** A slowly rotating cylinder with hemispherical indents on its inner surface. The cylinder lifts the **shorter brown rice** grains (which fit into the indents) and drops them into an internal trough, while **longer unhulled paddy** grains roll back. The lifted brown rice is discharged via auger. Separation efficiency >95 %.

- **Whitening Machine:**
  - **Abrasive-type:** A **vertical or horizontal abrasive screen** (grit-coated) rotates against brown rice, **scraping off the bran layer** by friction. Aggressive; can cause breakage if pressure is high.
  - **Friction-type:** Rice grains rub **against each other** in a chamber with rotating paddles under pressure. Gentler; yields more whole grains but slower.

| Parameter | Abrasive Whitener | Friction Whitener |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | External scraping | Internal grain-to-grain rubbing |
| Bran removal | Layer-by-layer | Uniform polishing |
| Breakage | Higher | Lower |
| Energy | Higher | Lower |
| Quality | Good for hard parboiled | Better for raw rice |

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] Parboiling = **Soaking + Steaming + Drying** — the order is non-negotiable in exam answers.
- [x] **Starch gelatinization** occurs in the range **65–78 °C**; below this, parboiling is incomplete.
- [x] The **CFTRI method** is the modern industrial benchmark — mention temperatures, pressures, and durations.
- [x] Parboiled rice has higher **vitamin B1** and **HRR** but a darker **amber color** (Maillard).
- [x] Milling sequence is **Clean → Stone → Husk → Separate → Whiten → Polish → Grade → Sort**.
- [x] **Rubber roll husker** is preferred over the Engelberg huller because of lower breakage.
- [x] **Indented cylinder** is the most common paddy separator.
- [x] **Abrasive whiteners** suit parboiled rice; **friction whiteners** suit raw rice.
- [x] Moisture control: **30–35 %** after steaming, **12–14 %** after drying (w.b.).
- [x] Key formula to memorize: $HRR = \dfrac{M_{whole}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100$.
- [x] Examiner's mantra — always include **temperature, time, and pressure** values whenever you describe a parboiling or milling step.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT403 - PROCESSING OF CEREALS, PULSES AND OILSEEDS
> - **Module:** Module 1

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 What is Wheat?

**Wheat** (*Triticum* spp.) is a cereal grass belonging to the family *Poaceae* (formerly *Gramineae*), cultivated worldwide for its grain, which is the principal raw material for a vast range of staple foods — bread, chapati, biscuits, pasta, noodles, semolina, and breakfast cereals. The wheat kernel (caryopsis) is a single-seeded fruit in which the seed coat is fused with the pericarp, making it technically a "groat."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Wheat is a monocotyledonous cereal grain of the genus Triticum, characterized by a unique viscoelastic protein complex called gluten, which is the basis of its unique dough-forming property and unparalleled dominance in bread-making."*

### 1.2 Anatomical Intuition — "The Treasure Chest Analogy"

Think of a wheat kernel as a **small wooden treasure chest with three locked compartments**:

- **The Bran (Outer Wooden Shell)** — The hard, fibrous multi-layered outer coat. Roughly **14–18%** of the kernel. Rich in fiber, B-vitamins, and minerals, but humans cannot digest it well.
- **The Endosperm (Starchy Treasure Inside)** — The largest compartment, about **80–85%** of the kernel. It is the white, starchy, protein-rich "treasure" that becomes flour. Milling aims to release this as cleanly as possible.
- **The Germ (The Living Embryo)** — The tiny "seed of life" at one end, about **2–3%** of the kernel. Rich in oil, vitamin E, and protein. Because it contains active enzymes and fat, it spoils flour, so millers usually try to **separate it out**.

The milling engineer is essentially an **"archaeologist"** who must carefully break the wooden chest (bran) and brush it away, leaving only the starchy treasure (endosperm) intact and free from contamination by the germ.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (High-Yield KTU Values)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Kernel length | **5–8 mm** |
| Kernel width | **2–3 mm** |
| Thousand-kernel weight (TKW) | **30–45 g** |
| Bulk density | **750–850 kg/m³** |
| Moisture (safe storage) | **< 14%** |
| Test weight (hectolitre) | **76–82 kg/hL** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love asking the **moisture threshold of 14%** for safe storage of wheat. Memorize it.

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Nutrient Composition of Wheat (Kernel Basis)

Wheat's macronutrient profile is what makes it the "king of cereals." Below is the average proximate composition per 100 g of whole wheat kernel:

| Nutrient | Whole Wheat | Refined Flour (Maida) | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbohydrates** | **60–75 g** (mainly starch) | 70–78 g | Primary energy source |
| **Proteins** | **8–15 g** (gluten-forming) | 8–12 g | Dough structure |
| **Fats / Lipids** | **1.5–2.5 g** | 0.8–1.2 g | Mostly in the germ |
| **Minerals (Ash)** | **1.5–2.0 g** | 0.4–0.6 g | Mostly in bran |
| **Crude Fibre** | **2.0–2.5 g** | 0.1–0.3 g | Bran component |
| **Moisture** | **10–14 g** | 12–14 g | Storage stability |
| **Vitamins** | B-complex, E, K | Mostly lost | B-vitamins in aleurone |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in milling:** Every time the miller removes bran to whiten flour, they also remove protein, ash, fibre, and vitamins. The "ash content" of a flour stream is therefore a **direct, measurable indicator of how much bran contamination** has occurred. This is the most important KTU concept in this module.

### 2.2 The Four Critical Wheat Proteins (Gluten Fractions)

The reason wheat dominates bread-making is the unique **gluten complex**:

- **Glutenin** — provides **elasticity** and strength (the "rubber-band" protein).
- **Gliadin** — provides **extensibility** and viscosity (the "stretchy" protein).
- **Albumins & Globulins** — water-soluble, lost in washing starch; minor structural role.

> The gluten network traps CO₂ produced by yeast, allowing the bread to rise — a property no other cereal possesses in equal measure.

### 2.3 Classification of Wheat (KTU 2024 Scheme — High Priority)

#### A) Botanical / Species Classification

| Species | Common Name | Use |
|---|---|---|
| *Triticum aestivum* | Bread / Common wheat | Bread, chapati, biscuits |
| *Triticum durum* | Durum / Macaroni wheat | Semolina, pasta, rava |
| *Triticum compactum* | Club wheat | Cakes, pastries |
| *Triticum dicoccum* | Emmer wheat | Traditional foods (Kerala context) |

#### B) Classification by Hardness (The Most KTU-Relevant)

| Class | Protein % | Endosperm | End Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hard Wheat** | **11–15%** | Vitreous, translucent | Bread, structural baked goods |
| **Soft Wheat** | **8–10%** | Chalky, opaque | Cakes, biscuits, pastries |
| **Durum Wheat** | **12–15%** | Vitreous, very hard, amber | Semolina, pasta, noodles |

#### C) Classification by Season of Planting

- **Winter Wheat** — Sown in autumn, harvested in early summer; vernalization required; higher yields.
- **Spring Wheat** — Sown in spring, harvested in autumn; no vernalization needed.

#### D) Classification by Kernel Color

- **Red Wheat** — Tannin-like pigments in bran; slightly bitter; more dormancy (preferred for long storage).
- **White Wheat** — Lighter bran; sweeter; preferred for chapati and noodles in India.

### 2.4 Overview of the Wheat Milling Process

Modern wheat milling is a **gradual reduction process** — not a single crushing event. The objective is to **separate the endosperm from the bran and germ** with maximum purity and minimum starch damage.

**Stages:**

1. **Receiving & Storage** — silos, moisture/temperature control.
2. **Cleaning** — magnetic separators, destoners, aspirators, disk separators, indented cylinders (removes weed seeds).
3. **Conditioning / Tempering** — water added to **14–17%** moisture and rested **8–24 h** to toughen bran and soften endosperm.
4. **Breaking** — corrugated rolls progressively open the kernel.
5. **Scratching & Purification** — coarse material reground; particles separated by size and density.
6. **Reduction** — smooth rolls gradually reduce endosperm to flour.
7. **Sifting & Grading** — plan sifters separate streams.
8. **Blending & Fortification** — flour streams combined to specification.
9. **Packaging** — bulk or consumer packs.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Symbol / Variables | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Extraction Rate = $\dfrac{W_{flour}}{W_{wheat}} \times 100$ | $W_{flour}$ = flour mass; $W_{wheat}$ = wheat mass | % | Measures milling yield |
| Bran Yield = $\dfrac{W_{bran}}{W_{wheat}} \times 100$ | $W_{bran}$ = bran mass | % | By-product yield |
| Germ Yield = $\dfrac{W_{germ}}{W_{wheat}} \times 100$ | $W_{germ}$ = germ mass | % | By-product yield |
| Stream Ash = $\dfrac{M_{ash}}{M_{sample}} \times 100$ | $M_{ash}$ = ash mass; $M_{sample}$ = sample | % | Flour purity indicator |
| Flour Loss = $100 - \text{Extraction Rate} - \text{Bran\%} - \text{Germ\%} - \text{Moisture Loss\%}$ | — | % | Process control |
| Conditioning Water = $W_{wheat} \times \dfrac{M_{target} - M_{initial}}{100 - M_{target}}$ | $M$ = moisture % | L or kg | Tempering water |
| Total Flour Streams = $\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i$ | $F_i$ = individual stream | kg | Mass balance check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, **never** use the symbol `|` (vertical bar) inside table cells. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. This is a strict formatting rule for the answer booklet.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Extraction Rate** governs the **economic viability** of a flour mill — Indian government stipulates 70% extraction for "Atta" and ~60% for refined "Maida."
- **Ash content** is the **regulatory parameter** (FSSAI / Codex) for classifying flour grades.
- **Stream blending** allows the miller to engineer flours of specific protein, ash, and color for diverse end-products — a true food-engineering craft.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Calculation of Extraction Rate (Milling Yield)

The **extraction rate** is the single most important performance metric in a flour mill. Let us derive it rigorously.

**Definition:** Extraction rate is the percentage of the original wheat mass that is recovered as saleable flour streams.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define input and output} \\
\text{Input mass of cleaned, conditioned wheat} &= W_{wheat} \quad [\text{kg}] \\
\text{Output mass of combined flour streams} &= W_{flour} \quad [\text{kg}] \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Express as a fraction} \\
\text{Fractional yield} &= \frac{W_{flour}}{W_{wheat}} \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Convert to percentage (KTU convention)} \\
\text{Extraction Rate (\%)} &= \frac{W_{flour}}{W_{wheat}} \times 100 \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Numerical example} \\
\text{If } W_{wheat} &= 1000 \text{ kg}, \\
W_{flour} &= 720 \text{ kg} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Extraction Rate} &= \frac{720}{1000} \times 100 = 72\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** A 72% extraction rate means 72% of the wheat became flour; the remaining 28% was distributed among bran (~14%), germ (~2%), and losses/moisture (~12%).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Conditioned Moisture Calculation (Tempering Water)

**Problem:** A miller has 10,000 kg of wheat at 11% moisture. He wants to condition it to 15.5% moisture before first break. How much water must be added?

**Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify the principle} \\
\text{Adding water does not change the dry matter mass.} \\
\text{Dry matter initial} &= \text{Dry matter final} \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Express dry matter} \\
DM_i &= W_{wheat} \times \frac{100 - M_i}{100} \\
DM_f &= (W_{wheat} + W_{water}) \times \frac{100 - M_f}{100} \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Equate and solve for } W_{water} \\
W_{wheat} \times (100 - M_i) &= (W_{wheat} + W_{water}) \times (100 - M_f) \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Isolate } W_{water} \\
W_{water} &= W_{wheat} \times \frac{M_f - M_i}{100 - M_f} \\
\\
\text{Step 5: Substitute values} \\
W_{water} &= 10000 \times \frac{15.5 - 11}{100 - 15.5} \\
&= 10000 \times \frac{4.5}{84.5} \\
&= 10000 \times 0.05325 \\
&\approx 532.5 \text{ kg of water}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conclusion:** Approximately **532.5 kg (or 532.5 L)** of water must be added, after which the wheat is rested for 12–24 hours.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mass Balance Across a Purifier

A purifier receives **250 kg/h** of material at 1.20% ash. It discharges three streams: a coarse tail (20% of feed, ash 4.0%), a throughs fraction (60% of feed, ash 0.45%), and a coarse mids (20% of feed, ash unknown). Find the ash % of the coarse mids.

**Setup — Total mass balance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Feed} &= \text{Coarse tails} + \text{Throughs} + \text{Coarse mids} \\
100\% &= 20\% + 60\% + 20\% \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Ash balance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Feed ash} \times 1.00 &= (0.20 \times 4.00) + (0.60 \times 0.45) + (0.20 \times X) \\
1.20 &= 0.80 + 0.27 + 0.20X \\
1.20 - 1.07 &= 0.20X \\
0.13 &= 0.20X \\
X &= \frac{0.13}{0.20} = 0.65\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Result:** The coarse mids contain **0.65% ash** — a clean fraction that can be sent to a reduction roll.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Combined Stream Ash

A miller blends three flour streams: 400 kg of 0.42% ash, 250 kg of 0.55% ash, and 350 kg of 0.70% ash. What is the combined ash %?

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Combined ash \%} &= \frac{\sum (M_i \times A_i)}{\sum M_i} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{(400 \times 0.42) + (250 \times 0.55) + (350 \times 0.70)}{400 + 250 + 350} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{168 + 137.5 + 245}{1000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{550.5}{1000} \times 100 \\
&= 0.55\%
\end{aligned}$$

> This **weighted-average** principle is heavily tested in KTU problems on flour-stream blending.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Equipment vs. Function vs. Output

| Equipment | Function | Rolls / Surface | Feed | Output Streams | Position in Flow |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Break Roll** | Open kernel, scrape endosperm off bran | Corrugated, differential speed 2.5:1 | Cleaned, tempered wheat | Coarse semolina, bran, flour | After conditioning |
| **Reduction Roll** | Reduce semolina to fine flour | Smooth, slight differential 1.25:1 | Purified semolina, middlings | Fine flour, low-grade flour | After purification |
| **Purifier** | Separate by size + specific gravity | Vibrating sieves with upward air | Ground stock from breaks | Pure endosperm (throughs), bran (tail) | After each break |
| **Plan Sifter** | Grade flour by particle size | Stacked horizontal sieves, gyratory motion | Flour + semolina mix | Multiple flour streams | After reduction |
| **Scalping** | Remove large bran flakes & foreign particles | Coarse scalping sieves | Initial break discharge | Cleaned endosperm fraction, tail | After first break |
| **Scratch System** | Detach endosperm from bran flakes; recover flour | Corrugated rolls (coarser) + scratch sifters | Bran fragments with adhering endosperm | Additional flour, clean bran | After main break system |

> [!NOTE]
> **Scalping vs. Scratch distinction (a classic KTU trick):**
> - **Scalping** = **defensive** sifting at the start of the break system to remove the *junk* (oversized bran, husks).
> - **Scratch system** = *offensive* milling of coarse bran fragments to scrape off the last bits of endosperm that cling to bran.

### 3.6 Detailed Equipment Descriptions

#### 3.6.1 Break Rolls

- **Surface:** Sharply or finely **corrugated** (fluted) chilled-iron or cast-steel rolls.
- **Differential:** Operate at a differential speed of **2.5 : 1** (fast roll 600 rpm; slow roll 240 rpm). The differential creates a **scraping/shearing action** that opens the kernel without pulverizing the bran.
- **Roller Gap (Roll Stands):** Break 1 ≈ 0.7–0.9 mm; decreases progressively to Break 5 ≈ 0.2–0.3 mm.
- **Number in a typical mill:** 4–5 break passages (B1, B2, B3, B4, B5).
- **Function:** To **progressively disintegrate** the kernel and liberate the endosperm in coarse granular form (semolina) while keeping bran in large flakes for easy separation.

#### 3.6.2 Reduction Rolls

- **Surface:** **Smooth** (non-corrugated), often porcelain-faced for low-grade streams.
- **Differential:** Very low, about **1.25 : 1**, hence the action is more of a **flattening/pressing** than a shearing.
- **Number in a typical mill:** 8–12 reduction passages (C1–C12, often split into coarse, medium, fine).
- **Function:** To **gradually pulverize the pure endosperm** granules into flour of defined particle size.

#### 3.6.3 Purifier

- **Construction:** Inclined, **oscillating sieves** of progressively finer mesh from feed to discharge.
- **Key feature:** **Upward air current** blown through the sieves from below.
- **Principle:** Lighter bran flakes are **air-lifted and floated off the sieve**; pure endosperm granules fall through the mesh.
- **Position:** Placed **after each break passage** to upgrade the stock before it goes to the next break or to a reduction.

#### 3.6.4 Plan Sifter

- **Construction:** A **box** containing 6–30 stacked horizontal sieve frames, all driven in a **gyratory / circular motion** (eccentric drive).
- **Principle:** Different mesh sizes classify the feed into multiple flour streams based on particle size.
- **Output:** Up to 8–10 distinct streams (e.g., Break Flour B1, B2; Reduction Flour C1, C2, C3; Low-grade flour).

#### 3.6.5 Scalping

- **Concept:** The **first sifting step** after the initial break.
- **Sieves:** Coarse, large-aperture sieves.
- **Function:** To **remove oversized material** (whole unbroken wheat, large bran flakes, foreign particles) from the discharge of the first break roll before further processing.

#### 3.6.6 Scratch System

- **Concept:** A **secondary system** of coarse, sharp-corrugated rolls and dedicated sifters.
- **Function:** To **scrape the endosperm** that is still adhering to the bran flakes coming out of the main break rolls, thereby improving the total extraction rate.
- **Output:** Recovered endosperm returns to the purifier; cleaned bran exits as a final by-product.

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*List the proximate composition of wheat kernel and highlight the role of gluten proteins.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The proximate composition of the wheat kernel (per 100 g) is:

- **Carbohydrates: 60–75%** — primarily starch (amylose + amylopectin); the principal energy source.
- **Proteins: 8–15%** — comprising **glutenin** (provides elasticity) and **gliadin** (provides extensibility). Together they form **gluten**, a viscoelastic network unique to wheat that traps CO₂ during fermentation, giving bread its spongy crumb.
- **Fats/Lipids: 1.5–2.5%** — concentrated in the germ; high in unsaturated fatty acids and vitamin E.
- **Minerals (Ash): 1.5–2.0%** — concentrated in the bran (aleurone layer), rich in iron, zinc, and phosphorus.
- **Crude Fibre: 2.0–2.5%** — mostly bran cellulose and hemicellulose.
- **Moisture: 10–14%** — critical for safe storage (must be < 14%).

The **gluten complex (glutenin + gliadin)** is the singular property that distinguishes wheat from all other cereals and is the basis of bread-making, chapati-making, and pasta production. *(3 marks: 1 mark for composition values, 1 mark for gluten role, 1 mark for storage moisture)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Classify wheat based on (a) hardness and (b) kernel color, mentioning end-use.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a) Classification by Hardness:**

| Class | Protein % | Endosperm Appearance | End Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hard wheat** | 11–15% | Vitreous, translucent | Bread, structural baked goods |
| **Soft wheat** | 8–10% | Chalky, opaque | Cakes, biscuits, pastries |
| **Durum wheat** | 12–15% | Very hard, amber, vitreous | Semolina, pasta, noodles, rava |

**(b) Classification by Kernel Color:**

- **Red Wheat** — Contains tannin-like phenolic pigments in the bran; slightly bitter; longer seed dormancy; preferred for long storage and bread flour.
- **White Wheat** — Lighter bran with lower tannin content; sweeter flavor; preferred for chapati, noodles, and refined flours in India.
- **Amber Wheat** — A reddish-white intermediate, often used for specific regional products. *(3 marks: 2 marks for hardness table, 1 mark for color classification)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **milling process of wheat**, highlighting the function of the **break rolls, reduction rolls, purifier, plan sifter, scalping, and scratch system**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A flour mill processes **5,000 kg of conditioned wheat** at 15% moisture. The output streams are: 3,600 kg flour (combined), 700 kg bran, 100 kg germ, and the balance is moisture loss. Calculate the **(i)** extraction rate, **(ii)** bran yield, **(iii)** germ yield, and **(iv)** moisture loss. Comment on whether the extraction is economic.

---

**Model Solution to (a) — Milling Process & Equipment [7 marks]:**

The modern roller milling of wheat is a **gradual reduction process** that progressively separates the endosperm from bran and germ. The unit operations and equipment are:

1. **Cleaning & Conditioning (1 mark):** Raw wheat passes through magnetic separators, destoners, aspirators, and indented cylinders. It is then tempered (conditioned) to **14–17% moisture** and rested 8–24 h. This toughens the bran and softens the endosperm for clean separation.

2. **Break Rolls (1.5 marks):** Corrugated (fluted) chilled-iron rolls operating at a **2.5 : 1 differential speed**. The first break (B1) has a large gap (~0.7–0.9 mm) and progressively tighter gaps in B2, B3, B4, B5. Their function is to **open the kernel and scrape endosperm off the bran** in the form of coarse semolina, keeping bran in large flakes for easy separation.

3. **Scalping (0.5 mark):** The first sifting step after B1. Coarse scalping sieves remove oversized bran flakes, unbroken wheat, and foreign particles before they re-enter the system — a **defensive cleaning of the stock**.

4. **Purifier (1.5 marks):** Inclined oscillating sieves with **upward air currents**. It separates particles by **size + specific gravity**: light bran flakes are floated off; pure endosperm granules fall through the sieve. It is placed after each break passage.

5. **Reduction Rolls (1 mark):** **Smooth** rolls with low differential (~1.25 : 1), operating 8–12 passages (C1–C12). They **gradually pulverize pure endosperm** granules into flour without damaging starch.

6. **Scratch System (0.5 mark):** Coarse, sharp-corrugated rolls with dedicated sifters. They **scrape the last bits of endosperm** from bran fragments emerging from the break system, raising overall extraction.

7. **Plan Sifter (1 mark):** Multi-deck stacked sieves in **gyratory motion** that grade the product into 8–10 distinct streams by particle size (e.g., break flour, patent flour, straight-grade flour, low-grade flour).

---

**Model Solution to (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $W_{wheat} = 5000$ kg at 15% moisture
- $W_{flour} = 3600$ kg
- $W_{bran} = 700$ kg
- $W_{germ} = 100$ kg

**Mass Balance Check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total accounted} &= 3600 + 700 + 100 = 4400 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Missing (moisture loss)} &= 5000 - 4400 = 600 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**(i) Extraction Rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Extraction Rate} &= \frac{W_{flour}}{W_{wheat}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{3600}{5000} \times 100 \\
&= 72\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Bran Yield:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Bran Yield} &= \frac{700}{5000} \times 100 = 14\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Germ Yield:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Germ Yield} &= \frac{100}{5000} \times 100 = 2\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Moisture Loss:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Moisture Loss} &= \frac{600}{5000} \times 100 = 12\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Mass Balance Verification:** 72 + 14 + 2 + 12 = **100%** ✓

**Comment on Economic Viability (1 mark):** A 72% extraction rate is a **typical straight-grade or "Atta" range** flour yield. It is economically viable for both refined flour and chapati-grade products, leaving a balanced 28% as by-products. Indian FSSAI specifications for "Atta" require ≥ 70% extraction from cleaned wheat, which this mill comfortably meets.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **nutrient composition of wheat** with emphasis on protein quality, vitamins, minerals, and the anatomical distribution of these nutrients in the kernel.

**(b) [7 marks]** A miller needs to raise the moisture of **8,000 kg of wheat** from **12% to 16%** before first break. Calculate the amount of water required. If the wheat absorbs only **90%** of the added water uniformly, recalculate the final moisture content.

---

**Model Solution to (a) — Nutrient Composition [7 marks]:**

1. **Protein (2 marks):** Wheat contains **8–15% protein** varying with cultivar and growing conditions. The protein is unique among cereals because of the **gluten complex** — composed of **glutenin** (responsible for elasticity, strength, and gas retention) and **gliadin** (responsible for extensibility and viscosity). When hydrated and mechanically worked, they form a continuous viscoelastic network that traps CO₂ during fermentation, making wheat the only cereal suitable for leavened bread. Albumins and globulins (15–20% of total protein) are water-soluble and concentrated in the aleurone and germ; they are lost in refined flour.

2. **Carbohydrates (1.5 marks):** The bulk of the endosperm is **starch (60–75%)**, comprising ~25% **amylose** (linear) and ~75% **amylopectin** (branched). Minor sugars include sucrose, raffinose, and fructosans. Cellulose and hemicellulose are concentrated in the bran.

3. **Vitamins (1.5 marks):** The **B-complex vitamins** (thiamine B1, riboflavin B2, niacin B3, B6) and **vitamin E (tocopherols)** are concentrated in the **aleurone layer and the germ**. Whole wheat is a good B-vitamin source, but **refined flour is severely depleted** — hence the mandatory fortification of maida with thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, folic acid, and iron under the Indian FSSAI regulations.

4. **Minerals (1 mark):** Ash content is **1.5–2.0%** in whole wheat, but only **0.4–0.6%** in refined flour. The minerals — iron, zinc, phosphorus, magnesium, selenium — are concentrated in the bran (especially aleurone) and germ.

5. **Lipids (1 mark):** **1.5–2.5%** of the kernel, but heavily concentrated in the **germ** (10–15% lipid). Rich in unsaturated fatty acids (linoleic, linolenic). Because these fats oxidize rapidly, millers deliberately remove the germ to improve flour shelf-life.

> **Anatomical Summary:** Endosperm = starch + gluten; Bran = fibre + B-vitamins + minerals; Germ = oil + vitamin E + protein. Refining selectively removes the most nutrient-dense fractions.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — Moisture Calculation [7 marks]:**

**Part 1: Water required (without absorption loss) [4 marks]:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{water} &= W_{wheat} \times \frac{M_f - M_i}{100 - M_f} \\
&= 8000 \times \frac{16 - 12}{100 - 16} \\
&= 8000 \times \frac{4}{84} \\
&= 8000 \times 0.04762 \\
&= 380.95 \text{ kg} \approx 381 \text{ kg of water}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part 2: Recalculation with 90% water absorption [3 marks]:**

If only 90% of the added water is absorbed uniformly:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{absorbed} &= 380.95 \times 0.90 = 342.86 \text{ kg} \\
W_{unabsorbed} &= 380.95 \times 0.10 = 38.10 \text{ kg (lost as drainage/evaporation)}
\end{aligned}$$

The dry matter in the original wheat is:

$$\begin{aligned}
DM &= 8000 \times \frac{100 - 12}{100} = 8000 \times 0.88 = 7040 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

The total mass of tempered wheat (with absorbed water only):

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{total} &= 7040 + 342.86 = 7382.86 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

Final moisture content:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{final} &= \frac{W_{absorbed}}{W_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{342.86}{7382.86} \times 100 \\
&\approx 4.64\%
\end{aligned}$$

Total final moisture of the wheat = **12% (initial) + 4.64% (absorbed) = 16.64%**

> **Conclusion:** Despite 10% water loss, the final moisture of 16.64% is still **above the 16% target**, providing a safety margin. If higher losses are anticipated, the miller should add extra water (use a 92–95% absorption factor in practice).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways Students Lose Marks in this Module:**
> 1. **Confusing Break vs. Reduction rolls:** Many students write "smooth rolls for break." The board **fails** such answers. Memorize — **Break = corrugated, high differential; Reduction = smooth, low differential.**
> 2. **Conflating Purifier with Plan Sifter:** A purifier uses **air + size** (specific gravity); a plan sifter uses **gyratory motion + sieve size** only. Mixing up these two costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting moisture < 14% rule:** This is a 1-mark item that examiners love to sneak in.
> 4. **Ash content misconception:** Students often claim that bran has "more ash because it's brown." Correct framing: **bran has more ash because minerals are concentrated in the aleurone layer.**
> 5. **Incomplete mass balance in numericals:** Always close the loop — Extraction % + Bran % + Germ % + Moisture loss % = 100%. KTU evaluators check this first.
> 6. **Units confusion in moisture calculation:** Use **kg of water = L of water** (1:1 density) only for room temperature. For precise industrial work, use 1 L = 0.998 kg.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Key Numbers to Memorize:**
> - Wheat composition: 70% starch, 12% protein, 1.8% fat, 1.7% ash, 12% moisture
> - Endosperm: 80–85% of kernel
> - Bran: 14–18% of kernel
> - Germ: 2–3% of kernel
> - Safe storage moisture: **< 14%**
> - Conditioning (tempering) moisture: **14–17%** (typical 15.5%)
> - Tempering rest time: **8–24 hours**
> - Break roll differential: **2.5 : 1**
> - Reduction roll differential: **1.25 : 1**
> - Number of break passages: 4–5 (B1–B5)
> - Number of reduction passages: 8–12 (C1–C12)
> - Break rolls surface: **corrugated**
> - Reduction rolls surface: **smooth**
> - Purifier uses: **air + sieves**
> - Plan sifter motion: **gyratory (circular)**
> - Standard extraction range: 60% (refined maida) to 72% (Atta)

> **Equipment Mnemonic — "BRS-PPS":**
> - **B**reak rolls, **R**eduction rolls, **S**cratch system
> - **P**lan sifter, **P**urifier, **S**calping

> **Three Signals of Flour Purity (in order of importance):**
> 1. **Ash content** — direct indicator of bran contamination.
> 2. **Color (Grade Color Index / Kent-Jones)** — indirect indicator of bran speckiness.
> 3. **Protein content** — indicates end-use suitability.

> **Gluten Composition Rule:** Glutenin + Gliadin = Gluten. Wheat is **unique** among cereals in producing functional gluten.

> **Classification Mnemonic:** "**HSD-RWA**" — Hard, Soft, Durum; Red, White, Amber.

> **Equation Summary:**
> - Extraction Rate = $(W_{flour} / W_{wheat}) \times 100$
> - Conditioning Water = $W_{wheat} \times \frac{M_f - M_i}{100 - M_f}$
> - Weighted-Average Ash = $\frac{\sum (M_i \times A_i)}{\sum M_i} \times 100$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Wheat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT403 - PROCESSING OF CEREALS, PULSES AND OILSEEDS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Types of Wheat # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Botanical Definition of Wheat **Wheat** (*Triticum spp.*) is a cereal grass belonging to the family **Poaceae** (formerly Gramineae) and the genus ***Triticum***. It is a self-pollinating, annual monocotyledonous plant whose dry one-seeded fruit (caryopsis) — commonly called the "kernel" or "berry" — is the principal raw material for bakery, pasta, noodle, and confectionery industries worldwide. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:** > "Wheat is the most widely cultivated cereal grain, classified under the genus *Triticum*, valued globally for the unique viscoelastic gluten-forming proteins (glutenin and gliadin) present in its endosperm, which enable the production of leavened bread." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wheat Family Tree" Imagine wheat as a **large human family** with different personalities: - **Grandfather (Diploid — 14 chromosomes)** → wild einkorn — tough, ancient, not used for bread. - **Father (Tetraploid — 28 chromosomes)** → durum wheat — strong, muscular, perfect for pasta. - **Son (Hexaploid — 42 chromosomes)** → common bread wheat — versatile, adaptable, ruling the bakery world. Just as siblings in the same family have different personalities, **six classes of bread wheat** (Hard Red Winter, Hard Red Spring, Soft Red Winter, Hard White, Soft White, Durum) all belong to the same extended wheat family but behave differently in the kitchen because of differences in **protein content**, **kernel hardness**, and **starch composition**. > [!NOTE] > **Ploidy in Wheat = Number of Chromosome Sets** > - Diploid (2n = 14): *T. monococcum* (Einkorn) > - Tetraploid (2n = 28): *T. durum*, *T. dicoccum* > - Hexaploid (2n = 42): *T. aestivum* (Common Bread Wheat) ## 1.3 Why Classification Matters in Food Engineering The **end-use quality** of wheat (whether it makes flaky pastry or chewy bread) is determined by: 1. **Protein content** (typically **8–15%**) 2. **Kernel hardness** (Hard vs. Soft endosperm) 3. **Gluten strength** (Strong vs. Weak) 4. **Starch damage** during milling (critical for water absorption) > [!TIP] > **Industrial Reality Check:** A flour miller selects wheat classes based on the **finished product target**. Mixing Hard and Soft wheats is called **wheat blending** — a standard industrial practice to achieve desired flour specifications at the lowest cost. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Botanical (Species-Based) Classification of Wheat Wheat is fundamentally classified into **three ploidy levels** based on chromosome number: ### A. Diploid Wheat (2n = 14) - **Species:** *Triticum monococcum* (Einkorn) - **Use:** Limited, ancestral, hulled wheat - **Gluten quality:** Weak, poor baking performance ### B.
