Tetraploid Wheat (2n = 28) - **Species:** *Triticum durum* (Macaroni/Durum wheat) - **Use:** Pasta, semolina, couscous, bulgur - **Gluten quality:** Strong but not elastic (good for pasta, not for pan bread) ### C. Hexaploid Wheat (2n = 42) - **Species:** *Triticum aestivum* (Common/Bread wheat) - **Subspecies:** *T. compactum* (Club wheat), *T. spelta* (Spelt) - **Use:** Breads, cakes, biscuits, noodles, cookies - **Gluten quality:** Balanced — viscoelastic — ideal for leavened bread ## 2.2 Commercial / End-Use Classification (KTU High-Yield — Most Important for Exams) The **U.S. Wheat Classes** system (followed worldwide including by Indian industries) classifies wheat into **six commercial classes** based on **kernel hardness, color, and growing season**: ### Class 1 — Hard Red Winter (HRW) - **Protein:** **10–13%** - **Gluten:** Medium-strong, balanced elasticity - **End-use:** Pan breads, hamburger buns, hard rolls - **Growing season:** Winter (planted fall, harvested summer) ### Class 2 — Hard Red Spring (HRS) - **Protein:** **12–15%** (highest among bread wheats) - **Gluten:** Very strong, high water absorption - **End-use:** Premium hearth breads, bagels, pizza crust - **Growing season:** Spring ### Class 3 — Soft Red Winter (SRW) - **Protein:** **8.5–10.5%** - **Gluten:** Weak, extensible - **End-use:** Cakes, cookies, crackers, pastries - **Growing season:** Winter ### Class 4 — Hard White (HW) - **Protein:** **10–12%** - **Gluten:** Medium-strong - **End-use:** Asian noodles, tortillas, pan breads - **Specialty:** Lower bran content → milder flavor, whiter flour ### Class 5 — Soft White (SW) - **Protein:** **8.5–10.5%** - **Gluten:** Weak - **End-use:** Cookies, cakes, noodles, wafers - **Specialty:** Very white flour, low ash ### Class 6 — Durum - **Protein:** **12–15%** - **Gluten:** Strong but non-elastic (high yellow pigment — xanthophyll) - **End-use:** Semolina → pasta, macaroni, spaghetti - **Specialty:** Largest kernel, hardest endosperm ## 2.3 Seasonal Classification | Parameter | Winter Wheat | Spring Wheat | |---|---|---| | Sowing | Autumn (Oct–Nov) | Spring (Feb–Mar) | | Harvest | Early summer | Late summer | | Vernalization | Required | Not required | | Yield | Higher | Lower | | Protein | Moderate | High | | Use | HRW, SRW | HRS | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vernalization:** A cold-treatment period (typically **4–10 °C for 30–60 days**) required by winter wheat to switch from vegetative to reproductive phase. Without this, the plant will not produce grain. ## 2.4 Indian Varieties (KTU Kerala Context) | Variety | Type | Best For | |---|---|---| | HD-2967 | Bread wheat | Chapati, bread | | Lok-1 | Durum | Pasta | | Sharbati | Durum | Premium atta | | PBW-343 | Bread wheat | Bread | | WH-1105 | Bread wheat | Bread, biscuits | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet | Concept / Parameter | Value / Range | Industrial Significance | |---|---|---| | Hexaploid chromosome number | **2n = 42** | Common bread wheat | | Tetraploid chromosome number | **2n = 28** | Durum wheat | | Diploid chromosome number | **2n = 14** | Einkorn (wild) | | Bread wheat protein range | **11–14%** | Strong gluten formation | | Cake/cookie wheat protein | **8–10%** | Tender crumb | | Durum protein | **12–15%** | Pasta quality | | Optimal milling moisture | **14–15%** | Conditioning target | | Hard wheat starch damage | High (~7–10%) | High water absorption | | Soft wheat starch damage | Low (~2–4%) | Low water absorption | | Test weight (standard) | **≥ 76 kg/hL** | Grade 1 milling wheat | | Thousand kernel weight | **30–50 g** | Milling yield indicator | > [!NOTE] > **Test Weight (Bushel Weight):** Mass of grain per unit volume, measured in **kg/hL (kilograms per hectolitre)**. Higher test weight → higher milling yield → better grade. KTU examiners love this as a 2-mark definition. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Identify Wheat Type from Specifications When a flour mill receives a wheat consignment, classification is done by analyzing **three sequential parameters**. Below is the engineering decision logic KTU examiners love: ### Step 1: Kernel Hardness Test (PSI — Particle Size Index) The **Single Kernel Characterization System (SKCS)** measures the force required to crush a kernel. The derived parameter is the **Particle Size Index (PSI)**. $$ \text{PSI} = \frac{\text{Weight of flour passing through 94 $\mu$m sieve}}{\text{Total weight of sample}} \times 100 $$ **Interpretation:** - PSI > 50 → **Soft wheat** (fragments easily → fine particles) - PSI 30–50 → **Medium hard** - PSI < 30 → **Hard wheat** (resistant → coarse particles) ### Step 2: Protein Quantification (Kjeldahl Method) The **crude protein content** is determined by the Kjeldahl nitrogen analysis: $$ \text{Protein \%} = \text{N \%} \times F $$ Where: - $F$ = **Conversion factor = 5.7** for wheat (since wheat protein contains ~17.54% N) - For general food: $F = 6.25$ **Step-by-step numerical example (typical KTU problem):** A wheat sample analyzed by Kjeldahl shows **2.4% nitrogen**. Calculate the protein content. **Step (a):** Identify the conversion factor for wheat. $$ F_{\text{wheat}} = 5.7 $$ **Step (b):** Substitute into the protein formula. $$ \text{Protein \%} = N \% \times F_{\text{wheat}} $$ **Step (c):** Plug in the values. $$ \text{Protein \%} = 2.4 \times 5.7 $$ **Step (d):** Final arithmetic evaluation. $$ \text{Protein \%} = 13.68\% $$ **Conclusion:** With **13.68% protein**, this wheat falls in the **Hard Red Spring (HRS)** category, suitable for **premium bread manufacturing**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often use **6.25** instead of **5.7** for wheat. Always use **5.7 for wheat**, **6.25 for general food**, and **6.38 for dairy**. Examiners specifically deduct marks for this. ### Step 3: Gluten Wash Test (Wet Gluten %) $$ \text{Wet Gluten \%} = \frac{\text{Weight of washed gluten mass}}{\text{Weight of flour sample}} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Dry Gluten \%} = \text{Wet Gluten \%} \times 0.33\ (\text{approx.}) $$ | Wheat Type | Wet Gluten % | |---|---| | Soft (cakes/biscuits) | 18–25% | | Medium (all-purpose) | 26–32% | | Strong (bread) | 33–40%+ | | Durum (pasta) | 30–38% | ## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Table — Industrial Decision Parameters | Parameter | HRW | HRS | SRW | Durum | Soft White | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Protein % | 10–13 | 12–15 | 8.5–10.5 | 12–15 | 8.5–10.5 | | Kernel Hardness | Hard | Very Hard | Soft | Very Hard | Soft | | Gluten Strength | Medium | Very Strong | Weak | Strong (non-elastic) | Weak | | Starch Damage | High | High | Low | Medium | Low | | Water Absorption (Flour) | 60–65% | 64–70% | 50–55% | 60–65% | 50–55% | | End Product | Pan bread | Bagels, hearth bread | Cookies, cakes | Pasta, semolina | Asian noodles | | Color Pigment | Red bran | Red bran | Red bran | Amber (xanthophyll) | White bran | ## 3.3 The Ploidy Equation — Why 42 Chromosomes? Wheat evolved through **two natural hybridization events**: $$ \text{Einkorn (AA, 2n=14)} \times \text{Goatgrass (BB, 2n=14)} \rightarrow \text{Emmer (AABB, 2n=28)} $$ $$ \text{Emmer (AABB, 2n=28)} \times \text{Goatgrass (DD, 2n=14)} \rightarrow \text{Bread Wheat (AABBDD, 2n=42)} $$ **Step-by-step chromosome count:** $$ \text{Total chromosomes in hexaploid} = 6 \times 7 = 42 $$ **Step-by-step chromosome count for tetraploid durum:** $$ \text{Total chromosomes} = 4 \times 7 = 28 $$ This is why **bread wheat has 3 sets of 7 chromosomes × 2 homologous pairs = 42 chromosomes**. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Wheat Classification Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[WHEAT - Genus Triticum] --> B{Ploidy Level} B -->|Diploid 2n=14| C[T. monococcum
Einkorn
Wild Hulled] B -->|Tetraploid 2n=28| D[T. durum
Macaroni Wheat] B -->|Hexaploid 2n=42| E[T. aestivum
Common Bread Wheat] E --> F{End-Use Classification} F --> G1[HRW
Bread] F --> G2[HRS
Premium Bread] F --> G3[SRW
Cakes and Cookies] F --> G4[Hard White
Noodles and Tortillas] F --> G5[Soft White
Confectionery] D --> H[Semolina and Pasta] C --> I[Animal Feed and Heritage Foods] G1 --> J1[Milling and Baking Industry] G2 --> J1 G3 --> J2[Confectionery Industry] G4 --> J3[Noodle Industry] G5 --> J2 H --> J4[Pasta and Couscous Industry] ``` ## 4.2 Wheat Kernel Anatomy (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Wheat Kernel Caryopsis] --> B[1 Bran Layer 13 percent] A --> C[2 Endosperm 84 percent] A --> D[3 Germ Embryo 3 percent] B --> B1[Pericarp Outer] B --> B2[Testa Pigment Layer] B --> B3[Aleurone Protein and Mineral Rich] C --> C1[Starch Granules 70 percent of kernel] C --> C2[Gluten Proteins Glutenin and Gliadin] C --> C3[Cell Wall Pentosans] D --> D1[Scutellum Nutrient Transfer] D --> D2[Embryonic Axis Future Plant] ``` ## 4.3 Industrial Flow — From Wheat Class to Finished Product ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Wheat Consignment] --> B[CLEANING
Removal of Stones and Foreign Matter] B --> C[TEMPERING
Moisture 14 to 16 percent
Duration 18 to 24 hours] C --> D{Selection Based on Type} D -->|Hard Wheat HRW HRS| E[Roller Milling
Multiple Break and Reduction Passes] D -->|Soft Wheat SRW SW| F[Stone Milling or Impact Detacher] D -->|Durum| G[Semolina Mill
Purifier and Sizing] E --> H[Strong Flour
High Protein 12 to 14 percent] F --> I[Weak Flour
Low Protein 8 to 10 percent] G --> J[Semolina
Coarse Granulation 200 to 400 microns] H --> K[1 Bread Manufacturing] H --> L[2 Bun and Pizza Base] I --> M[3 Cakes and Cookies] J --> N[4 Pasta and Macaroni] J --> O[5 Couscous and Bulgur] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Node Rule Compliance:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`) and all labels use clean uppercase text without markdown formatting characters to ensure rendering safety across all KTU classroom browsers and PDF exports. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]** **Classify wheat on the basis of botanical classification. Give two examples for each class.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Wheat is botanically classified into **three ploidy levels** based on chromosome number: 1. **Diploid Wheat (2n = 14):** *Triticum monococcum* (Einkorn). Limited use, mostly heritage. 2. **Tetraploid Wheat (2n = 28):** *Triticum durum* (Durum/Macaroni wheat) and *T. dicoccum* (Emmer). Used for pasta and semolina. 3. **Hexaploid Wheat (2n = 42):** *Triticum aestivum* (Common bread wheat) and *T. compactum* (Club wheat). Used for bread, cakes, biscuits. ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]** **Differentiate between Hard Red Spring (HRS) and Soft Red Winter (SRW) wheat.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | HRS | SRW | |---|---|---| | Protein content | High (12–15%) | Low (8.5–10.5%) | | Kernel hardness | Very Hard | Soft | | Gluten strength | Very strong | Weak | | End use | Premium bread, bagels | Cakes, cookies, crackers | | Starch damage | High | Low | --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern) ### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **commercial classification of wheat** with reference to kernel hardness, protein content, gluten quality, and end-use applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **anatomy of a wheat kernel** with a neat diagram. Mention the industrial significance of each anatomical part. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** ### **Part (a) — Commercial Classification of Wheat (7 marks)** The commercial (or end-use) classification of wheat divides all wheat into **six classes** recognized internationally. Classification is based on three physical characteristics: **kernel hardness**, **bran color**, and **growing season**. **Class 1 — Hard Red Winter (HRW):** Protein 10–13%, medium-strong gluten, used for pan breads, hamburger buns, and hard rolls. Sown in autumn, requires vernalization. **Class 2 — Hard Red Spring (HRS):** Protein 12–15%, the highest among bread wheats; very strong gluten, high water absorption. Used for premium hearth breads, bagels, and pizza crust. **Class 3 — Soft Red Winter (SRW):** Protein 8.5–10.5%, weak gluten, low water absorption. Used for cakes, cookies, crackers, and pastries. **Class 4 — Hard White (HW):** Protein 10–12%, medium-strong gluten, lower ash content. Used for Asian noodles, tortillas, and pan breads. The white bran produces milder-flavored flour. **Class 5 — Soft White (SW):** Protein 8.5–10.5%, weak gluten, very low ash. Used for cookies, cakes, wafers, and white salted noodles. **Class 6 — Durum:** Protein 12–15%, hardest kernel, amber-colored endosperm (xanthophyll pigment), strong but non-elastic gluten. Used exclusively for **semolina, pasta, macaroni, spaghetti, and couscous**. Durum is tetraploid (2n = 28), unlike the other five hexaploid classes. > [!TIP] > **Industrial Significance:** Wheat blending is a common practice — millers mix 60% HRW + 40% SRW to produce **all-purpose flour** at lower cost than pure HRW. ### **Part (b) — Wheat Kernel Anatomy (7 marks)** The wheat kernel (caryopsis) is a one-seeded fruit with three distinct anatomical regions: **1. Bran (13% of kernel weight):** Outer protective layers comprising the pericarp (outermost), testa (pigment layer giving red/white color), and aleurone (rich in proteins, B-vitamins, and minerals). Industrially, bran is a **by-product** sold as cattle feed or used in **whole-wheat atta**. **2. Endosperm (84% of kernel weight):** Starchy inner tissue containing **70–75% starch granules** and **8–15% gluten-forming proteins** (glutenin + gliadin). This is the **flour-producing portion** of the kernel and the basis of all refined wheat products. **3. Germ (3% of kernel weight):** The embryo, consisting of the scutellum (nutrient transfer organ) and embryonic axis (future plant). Rich in **lipids (10%) and vitamin E**. Industrially, the germ is **separated during milling** to prevent rancidity in stored flour. **Industrial Significance of Anatomy:** - **Milling yield** depends on endosperm separation efficiency. - **Bran removal rate** determines whether flour is *whole wheat* (100% extraction) or *refined* (~72% extraction). - **Germ separation** improves flour shelf-life. --- ### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Explain the **botanical classification of wheat** in detail. How does ploidy level influence end-use quality? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A wheat sample analyzed by Kjeldahl method shows **2.1% nitrogen**. Calculate the protein content using the appropriate factor. Identify the **commercial class** and **end-use application** of this wheat. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** ### **Part (a) — Botanical Classification of Wheat (7 marks)** Wheat belongs to the family **Poaceae**, genus ***Triticum***. Botanically, wheat is classified based on **ploidy (chromosome number)** into three groups: **1. Diploid Wheat (2n = 14):** - Species: *Triticum monococcum* (Einkorn) - Genome composition: **AA** - Characteristics: Hulled kernels, low yield, weak gluten. Not commercially significant; grown only as heritage crop in parts of Europe. **2. Tetraploid Wheat (2n = 28):** - Species: *Triticum durum* (the only commercially important tetraploid) - Genome composition: **AABB** - Characteristics: Very hard kernels, high protein (12–15%), amber-yellow endosperm due to xanthophyll pigment. Gluten is strong but **lacks elasticity** — ideal for pasta but unsuitable for leavened bread. **3. Hexaploid Wheat (2n = 42):** - Species: *Triticum aestivum* (Common bread wheat — accounts for ~95% of global wheat trade) - Genome composition: **AABBDD** - Characteristics: Viscoelastic gluten, balanced protein (8–15%), versatile end-use. Subtypes include *T. compactum* (club wheat) and *T. spelta* (spelt). **Influence of Ploidy on End-Use:** - **Higher ploidy** → more genetic diversity → wider protein polymorphism → better gluten quality. - **Hexaploid bread wheat** has the D-genome contribution from *Aegilops tauschii* (goatgrass), which provides the **glutenin subunits (Glu-D1)** responsible for dough elasticity. - **Tetraploid durum** lacks the D-genome → produces tenacious but non-elastic gluten → pasta-grade. ### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)** **Given:** - Nitrogen content of wheat sample = 2.1% (from Kjeldahl analysis) **Step 1: Identify the correct conversion factor.** For wheat: $$ F_{\text{wheat}} = 5.7 $$ **Step 2: Apply the Kjeldahl protein formula.** $$ \text{Protein \%} = \text{N \%} \times F_{\text{wheat}} $$ **Step 3: Substitute the given values.** $$ \text{Protein \%} = 2.1 \times 5.7 $$ **Step 4: Final calculation.** $$ \text{Protein \%} = 11.97\% \approx 12\% $$ **Step 5: Identify the commercial class.** A protein content of **~12%** places this wheat in the **Hard Red Spring (HRS)** or **Hard White (HW)** category, which falls under the **strong-gluten bread wheat** class. **Step 6: Identify the end-use application.** Such strong-gluten, high-protein wheat is suitable for: - **Premium pan breads and hearth breads** - **Bagels and pizza crust** - **Buns and rolls requiring high water absorption** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. Using **6.25** instead of **5.7** for wheat → loses **1 mark**. > 2. Forgetting to **state units** (% or g/100g) → loses **0.5 mark**. > 3. Skipping the **commercial class identification** step → loses **1 mark**. > 4. Writing only "bread" without specifying "pan bread / hearth bread / bagels" → loses **0.5 mark** for lack of industrial specificity. > 5. Confusing **wet gluten %** with **protein %** in tabular answers → loses **1 mark**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Types of Wheat** ### **Must-Memorize Definitions** - ☐ **Caryopsis** — dry, one-seeded fruit of wheat (the kernel itself) - ☐ **Ploidy** — number of chromosome sets (2n) - ☐ **Vernalization** — cold treatment required by winter wheat - ☐ **Test weight** — kg/hL (≥ 76 for Grade 1) - ☐ **Particle Size Index (PSI)** — kernel hardness indicator - ☐ **Wet vs Dry Gluten** — dry gluten ≈ wet gluten × 0.33 ### **Must-Memorize Numbers** - ☐ Bread wheat chromosome number: **2n = 42** - ☐ Durum wheat chromosome number: **2n = 28** - ☐ Kjeldahl factor for wheat: **5.7** - ☐ Kjeldahl factor for dairy: **6.38** - ☐ Kjeldahl factor for general food: **6.25** - ☐ Strong gluten protein threshold: **> 12%** - ☐ Weak gluten protein threshold: **< 10%** - ☐ Optimal tempering moisture: **14–16%** ### **Must-Memorize Wheat Classes** - ☐ **HRW** — Bread, 10–13% protein, winter sown - ☐ **HRS** — Premium bread, 12–15% protein, spring sown - ☐ **SRW** — Cakes/cookies, 8.5–10.5% protein, winter sown - ☐ **HW** — Noodles/tortillas, 10–12% protein, white bran - ☐ **SW** — Confectionery, 8.5–10.5% protein, white bran - ☐ **Durum** — Pasta/semolina, 12–15% protein, **tetraploid** ### **Must-Draw Diagrams (for 14-mark answers)** - ☐ Wheat kernel anatomy with % composition (Bran 13%, Endosperm 84%, Germ 3%) - ☐ Wheat classification flowchart (Ploidy-based + Commercial) - ☐ Industrial milling flow (Cleaning → Tempering → Milling → Products) ### **Golden Rule for KTU 2024** > "Whenever asked about wheat types, **always mention all three**: (1) Botanical (ploidy), (2) Commercial (end-use class), (3) Seasonal (winter vs spring). Examiners reward comprehensive answers." ---

topics.title: Barley
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT403 - PROCESSING OF CEREALS, PULSES AND OILSEEDS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Barley # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Barley ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Barley** (*Hordeum vulgare* L.) is a monocotyledonous annual cereal grass belonging to the family **Poaceae** (Gramineae) and the tribe **Triticeae**. It is one of the founder crops of Old World agriculture (domesticated ~10,000 years ago in the Fertile Crescent) and ranks **fourth in global cereal production** after maize, rice, and wheat. Botanically, barley is a **self-pollinating, diploid (2n = 14)** species whose inflorescence is a spike composed of spikelets arranged in a distinctive triplet pattern along the rachis, producing either **two-rowed** or **six-rowed** ear morphology. In the context of **KTU PCFTT403 (Processing of Cereals, Pulses and Oilseeds)**, barley is studied as a multi-purpose cereal used for: 1. **Malting** → brewing, distilling, malt-based beverages 2. **Food** → pearled barley, barley flour, flakes, grits, β-glucan extracts 3. **Animal feed** → whole or rolled grain for ruminants and swine 4. **Industrial** → bioethanol, starch, biodegradable films > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 2):** > Barley is grouped under *"Minor Cereals and Pseudocereals"* or *"Specialty Cereals"*. The expected learning outcomes (COs) emphasize kernel morphology, proximate composition, malting-grade vs. feed-grade classification, primary processing (dehulling/pearling), malting biochemistry, and value-added product development. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine barley as **"wheat's rugged, adaptable cousin"** 🌾: - Where wheat wears a **smooth, naked coat**, barley is dressed in a **tight, inedible husk** that clings stubbornly to the grain — like a jacket zipped onto the kernel. - When you soak barley and let it germinate, you trigger a **biological sugar factory** inside the seed. Enzymes wake up, starch breaks down into sugars, and the grain turns sweet. This is essentially what brewers exploit to make beer. - The **β-glucan** fiber inside barley is like a viscous gel that traps cholesterol and slows digestion — making barley a functional food (similar to oats). - Think of the barley kernel as a **layered onion**: outer husk → bran (fiber-rich) → aleurone (enzyme factory) → starchy endosperm (energy store) → germ (embryo). > [!NOTE] > **Fun Fact:** Ancient Roman gladiators were called *hordearii* ("barley-eaters") because barley was their staple diet. Today, barley is the soul of whisky and beer — a single seed links Roman arenas to modern breweries! ## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value / Range | |---|---| | Chromosome number (2n) | **14** (7 pairs) | | Thousand-kernel weight (TKW) | **35–55 g** | | Test weight (hectolitre weight) | **62–68 kg/hL** | | Protein content | **8–13%** (malt-grade: 9.5–11.5%) | | Starch content | **55–65%** | | β-glucan content | **3–11%** (waxy: up to 12%) | | Kernel dimensions (length × width) | **7–12 mm × 2–3 mm** | | Optimal germination temperature | **15–20 °C** | | Germinative capacity (malt-grade) | **≥ 95%** | | Moisture for safe storage | **≤ 12%** (wet basis) | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Botanical Classification and Spike Morphology Barley spikes bear spikelets in **triplets** at each rachis node. This generates three distinct inflorescence architectures: 1. **Two-rowed barley** (*H. vulgare* convar. *distichon*) — Only the central spikelet of each triplet is fertile; laterals are sterile. Produces **plumper, more uniform kernels** → preferred for **malting**. 2. **Six-rowed barley** (*H. vulgare* convar. *hexastichon*) — All three spikelets per triplet are fertile. Produces **smaller, less uniform kernels** with higher protein → preferred for **animal feed**. 3. **Irregular barley** — Intermediate fertility; rarely cultivated commercially. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Six-rowed barleys have higher enzymatic (diastatic) power and protein, but lower starch content. Two-rowed barleys have plumper kernels, lower protein, and higher extract yield in malting. ## 2.2 Kernel Anatomy (The "Layered Onion" of Barley) The barley caryopsis is enveloped by **lemma and palea** (the hull), which remain attached at harvest — a critical distinguishing feature from wheat. | Layer (Outer → Inner) | Composition | Functional Role | |---|---|---| | **Husk** (lemma + palea) | Cellulose, hemicellulose, silica, lignin (35–45% of hull) | Inedible; must be removed (dehulling/pearling) for food use | | **Pericarp** (fruit coat) | Cellulose, lignin | Mechanical protection | | **Testa** (seed coat) | Polyphenols, tannins | Pigmentation; semi-permeable barrier | | **Aleurone layer** (2–3 cell thick) | Proteins (30%), lipids, minerals, B-vitamins, enzymes | **Enzymatic factory** during malting — releases α- and β-amylase | | **Starchy endosperm** (80% of kernel) | Starch granules (large + small), matrix proteins (hordein, glutelin) | Energy reserve; the substrate malted into sugar | | **Embryo (germ)** | Lipids (~25%), proteins, vitamins, enzymes | Viability center; produces gibberellic acid (GA₃) to signal aleurone | ## 2.3 Proximate and Chemical Composition The composition of barley is highly **cultivar- and environment-dependent**. The table below summarizes typical values per 100 g of dry, dehulled grain: | Component | Content (g/100 g db) | Notes for KTU | |---|---|---| | **Starch** | 55–65 | Main carbohydrate; amylose 22–30% (waxy: 3–8%) | | **Protein** | 8–13 | Hordeins (prolamins) dominate; limits foam stability in brewing | | **β-glucan** (soluble fiber) | 3–11 | Health claim: 3 g/day lowers cholesterol (FDA, EFSA) | | **Arabinoxylan** | 4–7 | Pentosan; affects wort viscosity | | **Lipids** | 2–3 | Mostly in germ; PUFA-rich (linoleic, oleic) | | **Minerals** (ash) | 2–3 | High in P, Se, Mg | | **Vitamins** | trace | B-complex (B₁, B₂, B₃, B₆), E, folate | | **Polyphenols** | 0.1–0.3 | Ferulic, p-coumaric acids; antioxidant | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the following formulas — they are routinely tested in numerical problems of the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE). | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $TKW = \dfrac{W_{1000}}{1000}$ | Thousand-kernel weight | g | | 2 | $TW = \dfrac{m_{grain}}{V_{hectolitre}}$ | Test weight (hectolitre weight) | kg/hL | | 3 | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | % | | 4 | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry\,matter}} \times 100$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | % | | 5 | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}} \times 100$ | Conversion wb ↔ db | % | | 6 | $W_{dry} = W_{wet}\left(1 - \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{100}\right)$ | Weight after drying | kg | | 7 | $Y_{extract} = \dfrac{m_{solubles}}{m_{malt}} \times 100$ | Malt extract yield (brewing) | % | | 8 | $DP = \dfrac{\text{g maltose}}{100\,\text{g malt}\cdot\text{h}}$ | Diastatic power (malt quality) | °L | | 9 | $ME = 0.0185\,P + 0.19$ | Malt extract from protein (empirical) | % | | 10 | $N_{rows} = 2 \times f_{fertile}$ | Spikelet fertility → row number | integer | | 11 | $Germ\% = \dfrac{n_{germinated}}{n_{total}} \times 100$ | Germinative capacity | % | | 12 | $W_{pearled} = W_{raw}\,(1 - P_{abrasion})$ | Yield after pearling | g or % | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Malting industry** uses **two-rowed, low-protein (< 11.5%)** barley to maximize extract yield in brewing. - **Functional food industry** exploits **β-glucan-rich waxy barley** for heart-health claims. - **Animal nutrition** relies on **six-rowed, high-protein** cultivars as energy–protein concentrates. - **Storage engineers** design silos using **equilibrium moisture content (EMC) curves** to predict safe storage life. - **Food process engineers** apply **pearling** (abrasive dehulling) at 8–14% moisture to balance yield vs. whiteness. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between Wet-Basis and Dry-Basis Moisture Content > **Why this matters:** Almost every KTU numerical problem on grain processing involves moisture — because water drives **microbial spoilage, milling yield, and shelf life**. ### 3.1.1 Given Let a grain sample of total mass $m_{total}$ contain $m_{water}$ of water and $m_{dry}$ of dry matter. $$m_{total} = m_{water} + m_{dry}$$ ### 3.1.2 Derive **Step 1 — Define wet basis:** $$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{total}} \times 100 = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{water} + m_{dry}} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Define dry basis:** $$MC_{db} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry}} \times 100$$ **Step 3 — Express $m_{water}$ and $m_{total}$ in terms of $m_{dry}$ and $MC_{db}$:** From Step 2: $$m_{water} = \dfrac{MC_{db}}{100} \cdot m_{dry}$$ Then: $$m_{total} = m_{water} + m_{dry} = m_{dry}\left(1 + \dfrac{MC_{db}}{100}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the wet-basis equation:** $$\begin{aligned} MC_{wb} &= \dfrac{\dfrac{MC_{db}}{100} \cdot m_{dry}}{m_{dry}\left(1 + \dfrac{MC_{db}}{100}\right)} \times 100 \\ MC_{wb} &= \dfrac{MC_{db}}{100 + MC_{db}} \times 100 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Rearrange to express dry basis from wet basis:** $$\boxed{MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}} \times 100}$$ ### 3.1.3 Worked Example A barley lot has $MC_{wb} = 12\%$. Compute $MC_{db}$: $$\begin{aligned} MC_{db} &= \dfrac{12}{100 - 12} \times 100 \\ MC_{db} &= \dfrac{12}{88} \times 100 \\ MC_{db} &= 13.64\,\% \end{aligned}$$ > **Interpretation:** The dry matter contains 13.64% water equivalent. This is the form used in **drying-rate calculations** and **mass balances** in food engineering. ## 3.2 Derivation: Pearling Yield vs. Weight Loss **Step 1 — Define pearling loss** ($P_L$) as the fraction of raw grain removed by abrasive friction: $$P_L = \dfrac{m_{raw} - m_{pearled}}{m_{raw}} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Solve for pearled yield** ($Y_P$): $$Y_P = 100 - P_L = \dfrac{m_{pearled}}{m_{raw}} \times 100$$ **Step 3 — Typical KTU numerical:** > A barley lot weighing 500 kg is pearled. After removing the husk + bran, the pearled barley weighs 410 kg. Find the pearling loss and verify it is within the industry standard (15–25%). $$\begin{aligned} P_L &= \dfrac{500 - 410}{500} \times 100 = 18\,\% \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** A loss of 18% is **acceptable** and lies in the standard range. Such a yield corresponds to a **medium-grade pearled barley** (Type II), where ~6% husk, ~5% pericarp/testa, and ~7% aleurone/outer endosperm are removed. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Malting Biochemical Pathway (Germination → Kilning) | Stage | Trigger | Enzyme Activated | Substrate | Product | Engineering Parameter | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Steeping** (0–48 h) | Water + O₂ uptake | Respiration enzymes | Endosperm starch | CO₂ + H₂O + heat | Moisture → **42–46%** | | **Germination** (Day 2–5) | Gibberellic acid (GA₃) from embryo → aleurone | α-amylase, β-amylase, limit dextrinase, β-glucanase, peptidase | Starch, β-glucan, protein | Maltose, glucose, dextrins, peptides, amino acids | Temperature **14–18 °C**; humidity **> 95% RH** | | **Kilning** (Drying + Curing) | Heat ramp | Enzyme inactivation (controlled) | — | Maillard products, color, flavor | 50 °C → 65 °C (drying) → **80–85 °C** (curing) | > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Insight:** The ratio of **soluble protein to total protein** (Kolbach Index) of malt should be **38–45%**. Higher indices indicate excessive proteolysis → poor foam in beer. ## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Germinative Capacity (Malt-Grade Barley Test) The KTU lab syllabus often asks for the **germination test**: **Step 1:** Count 100 intact barley kernels (in duplicates). **Step 2:** Place on moist filter paper in a Petri dish; cover. **Step 3:** Incubate at **20 °C** for **72 hours**. **Step 4:** Count kernels showing visible **coleorhiza + plumule** emergence. $$\begin{aligned} Germ\% = \dfrac{n_{sprouted}}{100} \times 100 \end{aligned}$$ **Acceptance criterion:** $\geq 95\%$ (malt-grade); $\geq 90\%$ (feed-grade). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Barley Processing Value Chain This **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps the complete industrial value chain for barley — from raw grain intake to finished products. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Barley Intake
Sampling and Grading] --> B[Pre-Cleaning
Removal of dust, stones, foreign matter] B --> C[Storage Silos
Moisture less than 12 percent
Aeration and Fumigation] C --> D{End Use Routing} D --> E1[Malting Stream] D --> E2[Food Processing Stream] D --> E3[Feed Stream] E1 --> F1[Steeping
Water immersion 36 to 48 hours
Moisture 42 to 46 percent] F1 --> F2[Germination Floor or Saladin Box
4 to 5 days at 15 to 18 degrees C] F2 --> F3[Kilning
50 degrees C drying to 85 degrees C curing] F3 --> F4[Deculming and Polishing] F4 --> F5[Malt Products
Brewing Distilling and Malt Extracts] E2 --> G1[Dehulling and Pearling
8 to 14 percent moisture
Abrasive friction 15 to 25 percent loss] G2[Milling Roller Mill] --> G3[Sieving and Grading] G1 --> G2 G2 --> G3 G3 --> G4[Food Products
Pearled Barley Flour Flakes and Grits] E3 --> H1[Cracking Rolling and Flaking] H1 --> H2[Animal Feed Pellets and Mash] style A fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style F1 fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,color:#000 style F2 fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,color:#000 style F3 fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,color:#000 style G1 fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#333,color:#000 style G2 fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#333,color:#000 style G3 fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#333,color:#000 style H1 fill:#ffadad,stroke:#333,color:#000 style H2 fill:#ffadad,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Barley Kernel Anatomy (Longitudinal Section) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Husk
Lemma and Palea
Cellulose and Silica] --> B[Pericarp
Fruit Coat
Cellulose and Lignin] B --> C[Testa
Seed Coat
Polyphenols] C --> D[Aleurone Layer
2 to 3 cells thick
Enzyme Factory] D --> E[Starchy Endosperm
80 percent of kernel
Starch Granules in Protein Matrix] E --> F[Embryo Germ
Lipid rich
GA3 Production Center] E -.-> G[Subaleurone Endosperm
High Protein Boundary Zone] style A fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#000,color:#000 style B fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C fill:#ffc6ff,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D fill:#caffbf,stroke:#000,color:#000 style E fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#000,color:#000 style F fill:#ffadad,stroke:#000,color:#000 style G fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Malting Process Flow (Steep → Germinate → Kiln) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Barley Lot] --> B[Steeping Tank
Water 14 to 18 degrees C
Aeration cycles] B --> C[Germination Chamber
4 to 5 days
15 to 18 degrees C 95 percent RH] C --> D[Kiln
Free Drying 50 degrees C
Curing 80 to 85 degrees C] D --> E[Deculming] E --> F[Rootlet Removal] F --> G[Finished Malt] style A fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style B fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,color:#000 style D fill:#ffadad,stroke:#333,color:#000 style G fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Matrix: Two-Rowed vs. Six-Rowed Barley | Feature | Two-Rowed | Six-Rowed | |---|---|---| | Spikelet fertility | Central only | All three | | Kernel size | Plumper, uniform | Smaller, mixed | | Protein | Lower (9–11%) | Higher (11–13%) | | Starch | Higher | Lower | | Diastatic power | Lower | Higher | | Extract yield | Higher | Lower | | Preferred use | Malting | Feed | | Example cultivar | 'Barke', 'Conrad' | 'Lacey', 'Stark' | --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **List and briefly explain the three morphological types of barley spikes. Which one is preferred for malting and why?** **Model Answer:** Barley spikes are classified by the fertility pattern of the three spikelets at each rachis node: 1. **Two-rowed barley** — Only the central spikelet is fertile; the two lateral spikelets are sterile. Produces large, plump, uniform kernels. **Preferred for malting** because of high extract yield, low protein, and uniform germination. 2. **Six-rowed barley** — All three spikelets per triplet are fertile. Produces smaller, less uniform kernels with higher protein content. Preferred for **animal feed** due to higher diastatic power and energy density. 3. **Irregular barley** — Fertile spikelets vary along the spike; agronomically insignificant. **Why two-rowed is malt-preferred:** Higher starch content (60–65%), plump kernels with high thousand-kernel weight (TKW 45–55 g), thinner husk proportion, and uniform germination (>96%). --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is β-glucan? Mention its health significance and the typical range in barley.** **Model Answer:** **β-glucan** is a **soluble, viscous, non-starch polysaccharide** composed of β-(1→3) and β-(1→4) linked D-glucose units. In barley, it is concentrated in the **subaleurone and endosperm cell walls** and constitutes **3–11%** of the dry grain (waxy cultivars can exceed 12%). **Health significance:** - **Cholesterol reduction:** 3 g/day intake lowers LDL-cholesterol by ~5–7% (FDA and EFSA health claim). - **Glycemic control:** Increases viscosity of gut contents, slowing glucose absorption → beneficial for **Type-2 diabetes**. - **Prebiotic effect:** Fermented by gut microbiota → production of **short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs)**. - **Satiety:** Viscous gel prolongs gastric emptying → weight management. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]` #### (a) **Describe the anatomy of a barley kernel with a neat labelled diagram. How does it differ from wheat?** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A barley caryopsis is a one-seeded fruit (caryopsis) where the pericarp is fused with the seed coat. From **outside to inside**, the layers are: 1. **Husk (Lemma + Palea)** — Two protective bracts that remain attached to the kernel at maturity. Composed of cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, and silica. Inedible; removed by **dehulling/pearling**. 2. **Pericarp** — Outer fruit wall; cellulose and lignin-rich. 3. **Testa (Seed Coat)** — Pigmented (yellow, blue, or purple); semi-permeable. 4. **Aleurone Layer** — 2–3 cell thick; rich in proteins (30%), lipids, B-vitamins, and enzymes. Activated by **gibberellic acid (GA₃)** during malting to produce hydrolytic enzymes. 5. **Starchy Endosperm** — ~80% of the kernel; packed with **large (Type-A) and small (Type-B) starch granules** embedded in a **hordein (prolamin) protein matrix**. 6. **Embryo (Germ)** — Rich in lipids (~25%), located at the dorsal end; produces GA₃ during germination. **Differences from wheat:** | Feature | Barley | Wheat | |---|---|---| | Husk at harvest | **Adherent** (lemma + palea) | Free-threshing (naked) | | Protein of starchy endosperm | Hordein (prolamin) | Gliadin + Glutenin | | Endosperm texture | Often **mealy/soft** | Vitreous (hard) or mealy | | Embryo position | Basal (dorsal) | Basal | | Embryo size | Larger | Smaller | | Main use | Malt > Feed | Bread, pasta, chapati | --- #### (b) **Explain the unit operations involved in the malting of barley. Discuss the biochemical changes during germination.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Malting is a controlled, three-stage process: **Stage 1 — Steeping (0–48 h)** - Barley is immersed in water at **14–18 °C** with alternating wet and air-rest cycles. - Moisture is raised from **12% → 42–46%**. - Respiration begins; CO₂ and metabolic heat are liberated. **Stage 2 — Germination (Day 2–5)** - The grain is spread on a germination floor (or in a **Saladin box** or **drum malter**) at **15–18 °C** and **>95% RH**. - The embryo secretes **gibberellic acid (GA₃)**, which diffuses to the aleurone. - The aleurone synthesizes and releases: - **α-amylase** → hydrolyses α-1,4 glycosidic bonds of starch → dextrins + maltose - **β-amylase** → produces maltose from starch chain ends - **Limit dextrinase** → breaks α-1,6 branch points - **β-glucanase** → reduces wort viscosity by hydrolysing β-glucans - **Peptidases** → solubilize proteins into free amino nitrogen (FAN) for yeast nutrition - The **acrospire** (coleoptile) grows to ¾ of kernel length — the visual indicator of malting completion. - Starch endosperm is progressively **modified** (transformed from vitreous to mealy, friable texture). **Stage 3 — Kilning (Drying + Curing, 24–48 h)** - Free-drying at **50 °C** → moisture drops to ~12% (prevents over-modification). - Curing at **80–85 °C** → develops **color and flavor** via **Maillard reactions** and melanoidin formation. - Enzymes are partially inactivated — retaining a controlled diastatic power for mashing. > **Engineering parameters monitored:** Moisture, temperature, CO₂, acrospire length, and **modification homogeneity** (friability test). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Set]` #### (a) **Discuss the proximate composition of barley. Explain how the protein and β-glucan content influence its end-use applications.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The proximate composition of barley (per 100 g db) is: | Component | Content | Functional Note | |---|---|---| | Starch | 55–65 g | Energy source; substrate for brewing | | Protein | 8–13 g | Hordein-rich; limits beer foam | | β-glucan | 3–11 g | Soluble fiber; health claims | | Lipids | 2–3 g | Mostly in germ; PUFA-rich | | Arabinoxylan | 4–7 g | Pentosans; wort viscosity | | Minerals (ash) | 2–3 g | P, Mg, Se, Zn | | Moisture (safe storage) | ≤ 12% wb | — | **Influence of protein content on end-use:** - **Low protein (9–11%)** → Higher malt extract yield; cleaner beer; preferred for **malting/brewing**. - **High protein (12–14%)** → Higher diastatic power; greater enzymatic activity; preferred for **feed** and **distilling** (more FAN). - **Excess protein (>14%)** → Causes **haze in beer**, poor foam stability, and excessive amino nitrogen → bacterial contamination risk. **Influence of β-glucan content on end-use:** - **Low β-glucan (<4%)** → Filtered wort easily; good for **brewing**. - **High β-glucan (>7%)** → Viscosity problems in brewing; **preferred for functional food** (cardiovascular health, glycemic control, satiety). - **Waxy cultivars** (β-glucan 8–12%) are selectively bred for **food-grade** barley. --- #### (b) **Describe the primary and secondary processing of barley for food use. List the major value-added products.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Primary Processing of Food-Grade Barley:** 1. **Pre-cleaning** — Removal of dust, stones, weed seeds, broken kernels (aspirator, destoner, magnetic separator). 2. **Grading** — Separation by size (slot/sieve grader); removes thin and broken kernels. 3. **Dehulling and Pearling** — **Abrasive friction** (e.g., Satake, Buhler pearlers) removes 8–25% of kernel mass: - **Type I (Pot barley):** ~8% removal — husk only. - **Type II (Medium pearled):** ~15% removal — husk + some bran. - **Type III (Pearled barley, fully polished):** ~22–25% removal — husk + bran + aleurone. 4. **Sorting** — Optical sorters remove discoloured kernels. **Secondary Processing:** 1. **Milling** — Roller mills produce **barley flour** and **grits**. 2. **Flaking** — Steam-cooked, rolled into **barley flakes** (porridge). 3. **Extrusion** — High-pressure extrusion → **puffed barley snacks**. 4. **Malting** (covered in Q. A above) → malt flour, malt extract, malted milk. 5. **Fractionation** — Wet milling isolates **β-glucan concentrates** (>30% β-glucan) for nutraceuticals. **Major Value-Added Products:** | Category | Products | |---|---| | **Whole-grain** | Pearled barley, grits, flakes | | **Flour-based** | Barley flour, multigrain atta, cookies, biscuits, bread (with wheat blend) | | **Beverage** | Malt, beer, whisky, barley tea (*mugicha*, *boricha*) | | **Functional** | β-glucan concentrate, soluble fiber supplement | | **Convenience** | Instant porridge, ready-to-eat flakes, expanded snacks | | **Industrial** | Starch, ethanol, biodegradable films | --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing barley spike types:** Students often write "two-rowed has 2 rows of grains" but forget to mention the **lateral spikelet sterility** mechanism. **Always mention the spikelet triplet arrangement.** > 2. **Mixing up moisture bases:** Numerical questions frequently test wb ↔ db conversion. Use the **correct formula** for the basis asked. > 3. **Missing the "Why":** In malting, merely listing "α-amylase is produced" is insufficient. Mention the **gibberellic acid signalling pathway** from embryo → aleurone → enzyme release. > 4. **Ignoring engineering parameters:** Examiners reward answers that include **specific numerical values** (e.g., "moisture 42–46%", "temperature 15–18 °C"). > 5. **Husk-vs-glume confusion:** Barley has a **husk** (lemma + palea fused with the kernel) — not a *glume*. Wheat has glumes that fall off at threshing. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ Barley is a **Poaceae** cereal, **2n = 14**, fourth in global cereal production. - ✅ **Two-rowed** barley (central spikelet only fertile) → **malting**. **Six-rowed** → **feed**. - ✅ Barley carries an **adherent husk** (lemma + palea) — must be removed for food use. - ✅ **Aleurone layer** is the **enzyme factory** triggered by **gibberellic acid (GA₃)** during malting. - ✅ Composition: **Starch 55–65%**, **Protein 8–13%**, **β-glucan 3–11%**, **Lipids 2–3%**, **Ash 2–3%**. - ✅ **β-glucan** is the key **functional food** component — FDA health claim at **3 g/day**. - ✅ Malting stages: **Steeping (12→46% moisture)** → **Germination (4–5 days, 15–18 °C)** → **Kilning (50→85 °C)**. - ✅ **Hordein** is the dominant prolamin protein in barley (analogous to gliadin in wheat). - ✅ **Pearling loss** = **15–25%** of raw kernel mass (industry standard). - ✅ Safe storage moisture: **≤ 12% (wb)** to prevent mould and insect attack. - ✅ Test weight of good malt barley: **62–68 kg/hL**; TKW = **35–55 g**. - ✅ Germinative capacity for malt-grade: **≥ 95%**. - ✅ Diastatic power (DP) is higher in **six-rowed** barley → better for **distilling**. - ✅ Waxy cultivars (low amylose) have **elevated β-glucan** → used for **functional food**. > **🎯 Final Tip for KTU 2024:** When answering barley questions in the ESE, always **integrate botany + composition + processing + product** in a single answer. A 14-mark question expects **all four dimensions**, not just one. Quote numerical values — they signal mastery and fetch full marks.

topics.title: Nutrient Composition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT403 - PROCESSING OF CEREALS, PULSES AND OILSEEDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Nutrient Composition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nutrient Composition** is the systematic quantitative and qualitative profile of macronutrients (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids), micronutrients (vitamins, minerals), and bioactive/anti-nutritional factors present in a given food matrix, expressed per 100 g of edible portion on a fresh or dry weight basis. For cereals, pulses, and oilseeds, the nutrient composition dictates their functional behavior, storage stability, processing requirements, and ultimate nutritional contribution to the human diet.

In the context of **PCFTT403**, nutrient composition forms the **fundamental basis** for understanding:
- Why specific processing techniques (milling, dehusking, refining, roasting, fermentation, extrusion) are employed.
- The fate of nutrients during unit operations.
- The rationale for fortification and value-addition strategies.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Building Block" Concept

Think of cereals, pulses, and oilseeds as **three different apartments in the same building** (your diet):

| Apartment | Primary Inhabitants | Real-World Food Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cereal Block** | Carbohydrates (energy providers) | Rice, Wheat, Maize |
| **Pulse Block** | Proteins (body builders) | Chana Dal, Toor Dal, Urad |
| **Oilseed Block** | Lipids (energy reservoirs) | Soybean, Groundnut, Mustard |

Just as each apartment has its own water, electricity, and furniture (minerals, vitamins, fiber), each food grain has its own nutrient fingerprint. The "furniture arrangement" — that is, the **bioavailability** and **interaction** of these nutrients — is what food processing tries to manage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric (KTU Convention):** All nutritional values in this module are expressed per **100 g of edible portion** on a **dry weight basis (db)** unless stated otherwise. Moisture content is reported separately.

### 1.3 Classification of Nutrients in Plant-Based Foods

- **Macronutrients** (Required in grams/day):
  - Carbohydrates (CHO)
  - Proteins
  - Lipids (Fats/Oils)
- **Micronutrients** (Required in mg/µg/day):
  - Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)
  - Water-soluble vitamins (B-complex, C)
  - Macro-minerals (Ca, P, K, Mg, Na)
  - Micro-minerals / Trace elements (Fe, Zn, Cu, Se, Mn, I)
- **Bioactive / Anti-nutritional Factors (ANF)**:
  - Protease inhibitors, lectins, phytic acid, tannins, oxalates, saponins, glucosinolates, gossypol, erucic acid, cyanogenic glycosides.

### 1.4 Moisture Content — The Hidden Variable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Moisture is **not a nutrient** but is the single most important parameter governing shelf-life, processing efficiency, and nutrient concentration (db vs. wb). Most biochemical and microbial deteriorations are **moisture-driven**. The standard KTU reference for safe storage moisture is **< 14% (wb)** for cereals and **< 10% (wb)** for oilseeds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Nutrient Composition of CEREALS (e.g., Rice, Wheat, Maize, Sorghum, Millets, Oats, Barley)

#### 2.1.1 Macronutrient Profile

- **Carbohydrates (70–80%)**: Predominantly **starch** (amylose + amylopectin), located in the **endosperm**. Minor sugars: sucrose, glucose, fructose, maltose. Dietary fiber (cellulose, hemicellulose, β-glucans — especially in oats and barley) resides in the **bran**.
- **Proteins (8–12%)**: Mainly **glutelins** (oryzenin in rice, glutenin in wheat) and **prolamins** (gliadin in wheat, zein in maize, kafirin in sorghum). Cereals are **lysine-deficient** but **methionine-adequate** — the classic "lysine is the limiting amino acid."
- **Lipids (2–6%)**: Concentrated in the **germ** (embryo) and **aleurone layer**. Rich in **polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFA)** — linoleic (ω-6) and α-linolenic (ω-3). Wheat germ oil is a commercial source of natural vitamin E.

#### 2.1.2 Micronutrient Profile

- **Vitamins**: B-complex — thiamine (B₁), niacin (B₃), pyridoxine (B₆), and folate. These are concentrated in the **bran and germ**, so refining (white rice, maida) causes **70–90% loss** of B-vitamins.
- **Minerals**: Iron, zinc, magnesium, phosphorus, selenium. However, bioavailability is poor due to complexation with **phytic acid (phytate)** in the aleurone layer.

#### 2.1.3 Why the Endosperm Dominates

The endosperm is the **caloric reservoir** of the plant — it must fuel the seedling's growth. Nature has therefore packed it with **dense, compact starch granules** (10–25 µm diameter), surrounded by a protein matrix (gluten in wheat, oryzenin in rice) that forms a continuous viscoelastic network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Hook for Examiners:** "Cereals are energy foods; their nutrient density of carbohydrate makes them substrates for fermentation, malting, brewing, and starch-based industries."

### 2.2 Nutrient Composition of PULSES (Legumes) (e.g., Chickpea, Lentil, Pigeon Pea, Mung Bean, Black Gram, Cowpea, Soybean)

#### 2.2.1 Macronutrient Profile

- **Proteins (20–30%)**: Nearly **2–3× higher** than cereals. Predominantly **globulins** (legumin, vicilin) and **albumins**. Pulses are **lysine-rich** but **methionine- and cystine-deficient** (sulfur-containing AAs are limiting).
- **Carbohydrates (50–60%)**: Mostly **starch** (the second most abundant after cereals), plus **dietary fiber** (soluble + insoluble), and oligosaccharides (raffinose, stachyose, verbascose) that cause flatulence.
- **Lipids (1–6%, except soybean ~20%)**: Low in most pulses; rich in **linoleic acid** and **phospholipids** (lecithin).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cereal–Pulse Complementarity Principle:** Cereals are lysine-poor + methionine-rich. Pulses are lysine-rich + methionine-poor. **A 70:30 cereal–pulse combination** (e.g., khichdi, idli, dosa batter) yields a **complete protein** with a Chemical Score > 90. This is the biochemical basis of the Indian "dal–chawal" diet and is a **guaranteed KTU question.**

#### 2.2.2 Micronutrient Profile

- **Vitamins**: Folate (B₉) is exceptionally high; thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, and B₆ are present in moderate amounts. Vitamin C and provitamin A carotenoids are minimal.
- **Minerals**: Excellent source of **iron, zinc, potassium, magnesium, and phosphorus**. Calcium is significant in certain pulses (e.g., Bengal gram).

#### 2.2.3 Anti-Nutritional Factors (ANF) in Pulses

| ANF | Primary Effect | Major Sources |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Trypsin/Chymotrypsin Inhibitors** | Reduce protein digestibility | Soybean, Kidney bean |
| **Lectins (Phytohemagglutinin)** | Agglutinate RBCs, GI toxicity | Raw kidney beans |
| **Phytic Acid (Phytate)** | Chelates Fe²⁺, Zn²⁺, Ca²⁺ | All pulses; highest in soy |
| **Tannins (Polyphenols)** | Bind proteins, reduce digestibility | Red/Brown lentils, Black gram |
| **Oligosaccharides (Raffinose family)** | Flatulence via colonic fermentation | All pulses |
| **Saponins** | Bitter taste, hemolytic | Chickpea, Soybean |
| **Oxalates** | Calcium binding, kidney stone risk | Cowpea, Horse gram |

### 2.3 Nutrient Composition of OILSEEDS (e.g., Soybean, Groundnut, Mustard, Sunflower, Sesame, Safflower, Cottonseed, Linseed, Coconut)

#### 2.3.1 Macronutrient Profile

- **Lipids (20–55%)**: The defining feature. Rich in **triglycerides** with diverse fatty acid profiles:
  - **Linoleic acid (ω-6)**: Safflower, Sunflower, Corn, Soybean
  - **α-Linolenic acid (ω-3)**: Linseed, Perilla
  - **Oleic acid (ω-9, MUFA)**: Olive, Groundnut, Canola, Sesame
  - **Erucic acid (C22:1)**: Traditional Mustard (rapeseed) — anti-nutritional; reduced in Canola varieties to < 2%)
  - **Lauric acid (C12:0)**: Coconut
- **Proteins (18–40%)**: Soybean is the richest plant protein source (40%). Defatted oilseed meals (soy meal, groundnut cake) are the world's largest protein by-product stream.
- **Carbohydrates (10–35%)**: Mostly structural polysaccharides (cellulose, hemicellulose) and oligosaccharides; starch is minimal.

#### 2.3.2 Micronutrient Profile

- **Vitamin E (Tocopherols + Tocotrienols)**: Abundant in wheat germ, sunflower, and palm oils — natural antioxidants that protect PUFA from oxidation.
- **Sterols / Phytosterols**: β-sitosterol, campesterol, stigmasterol — cholesterol-lowering bioactives.
- **Minerals**: Calcium (sesame is exceptional — ~1000 mg/100 g), magnesium, phosphorus, potassium, iron.

#### 2.3.3 ANF in Oilseeds

| ANF | Effect | Source |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gossypol** | Hepatotoxic, antifertility | Cottonseed |
| **Glucosinolates** | Goitrogenic, bitter | Mustard / Rapeseed |
| **Erucic acid** | Myocardial lipidosis | Traditional mustard |
| **Cyanogenic glycosides** | Release HCN | Linseed, Cassava |
| **Phytic acid** | Mineral chelation | Soybean, Sesame |
| **Oxalates** | Calcium binding | Sesame |

### 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are KTU-favorite computation tools for Part-A (3-mark) and Part-B (14-mark) problems.**

#### Table 1: Proximate Analysis (Weende Method) — Formulas

| Parameter | Formula | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Moisture (Oven Drying) | $\%M = \dfrac{(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1} \times 100$ | % wb |
| Crude Protein (Kjeldahl) | $\%CP = \dfrac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 14.007 \times 6.25}{W \times 1000} \times 100$ | % db |
| Crude Fat (Soxhlet) | $\%CF = \dfrac{W_{ext}}{W_s} \times 100$ | % db |
| Crude Fiber | $\%CFib = \dfrac{W_r - W_a}}{W_s} \times 100$ | % db |
| Ash (550 °C) | $\%Ash = \dfrac{W_a}{W_s} \times 100$ | % db |
| Nitrogen-Free Extract (NFE) | $\%NFE = 100 - (\%M + \%CP + \%CF + \%CFib + \%Ash)$ | % db |
| Total Carbohydrate | $\%TC = \%NFE + \%CFib$ | % db |
| Energy (Atwater) | $E = 4 \times P + 9 \times F + 4 \times C$ | kcal/100 g |

Where $W_1$ = initial weight; $W_2$ = oven-dried weight; $V_s, V_b$ = sample and blank titration volumes; $N$ = Normality of HCl; 6.25 = N-to-protein factor (general); 14.007 = atomic weight of N; 4, 9, 4 = Atwater factors (kcal/g) for protein, fat, and carbohydrate respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Alert (KTU Examiners):** The factor 6.25 is general; specific conversion factors are **5.70 (rice)**, **5.83 (soybean)**, **5.95 (groundnut)**, and **6.38 (wheat flour)**. Using the wrong factor = **-2 marks guaranteed loss.**

#### Table 2: Protein Quality Evaluation

| Index | Formula | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Chemical Score (CS)** | $CS = \dfrac{\text{mg AA per g test protein}}{\text{mg AA per g reference protein}} \times 100$ | Identifies limiting AA |
| **Amino Acid Score (AAS)** | Same as CS (modern nomenclature) | WHO/FAO/UNU (1991) standard |
| **Protein Efficiency Ratio (PER)** | $PER = \dfrac{\text{Weight gain (g)}}{\text{Protein intake (g)}}$ | Rat bioassay (legacy) |
| **Biological Value (BV)** | $BV = \dfrac{N_{retained}}{N_{absorbed}} \times 100$ | N utilization |
| **Net Protein Utilization (NPU)** | $NPU = \dfrac{N_{retained}}{N_{intake}} \times 100$ | Combines digestibility + BV |
| **PDCAAS** | $PDCAAS = AAS \times \text{TD}$ (truncated to 1.0) | Modern gold standard |
| **Limiting AA** | The AA with the lowest CS = "first limiting" | e.g., Lysine in cereals, Methionine in pulses |

#### Table 3: Moisture–Dry Weight Conversions

| Conversion | Formula |
| :--- | :--- |
| Wet basis (wb) to Dry basis (db) | $X_{db} = \dfrac{X_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}$ |
| Dry basis to Wet basis | $X_{wb} = X_{db} \times (1 - M_{wb})$ |
| Moisture content db | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These equations drive every FSSAI nutritional label, every ICAR crop-quality report, and every NIN (National Institute of Nutrition) database entry in India.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Kjeldahl Protein Determination

**Problem (KTU-Style, 7 marks):** A 1.250 g sample of soybean flour (moisture = 8.2% wb) was digested and distilled. The liberated NH₃ was trapped in 50.0 mL of 0.1 N H₂SO₄, and the excess acid was back-titrated with 0.1 N NaOH using methyl red. A blank required 50.0 mL NaOH; the sample required 32.6 mL NaOH. Calculate the % crude protein on both wb and db. (Use N-factor = 5.83 for soybean.)

#### Step 1: Determine the volume of acid that reacted with ammonia
Volume of acid consumed by NH₃ from sample = Volume required for blank − Volume required for sample

$$
V_{acid} = 50.0 - 32.6 = 17.4 \text{ mL}
$$

> *Logic:* The blank uses all 50 mL of acid (no NH₃ present). The sample uses less NaOH because some acid was already neutralized by NH₃. The difference = volume of acid that reacted with sample's nitrogen.

#### Step 2: Apply the Kjeldahl formula
Normality of H₂SO₄ = 0.1 N. The acid reacts with NH₃ in 1:1 stoichiometry. Equivalent weight of N = 14.007.

$$
\text{Mass of N (g)} = V_{acid} \times N \times 14.007 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\text{Mass of N} = 17.4 \times 0.1 \times 14.007 \times 10^{-3} = 0.02437 \text{ g}
$$

> *Logic:* Each mL of 0.1 N acid contains 0.1 × 14.007/1000 = 0.0014007 g of N as NH₃. Multiplied by 17.4 mL = 0.02437 g N.

#### Step 3: Convert N to crude protein (wb)

$$
\%N_{wb} = \dfrac{0.02437}{1.250} \times 100 = 1.9496\% \text{ wb}
$$

$$
\%CP_{wb} = 1.9496 \times 5.83 = 11.37\% \text{ wb}
$$

> *Logic:* Divide nitrogen mass by sample mass to get %N, then multiply by species-specific N-factor to convert to crude protein.

#### Step 4: Convert to dry weight basis
Using the moisture conversion formula:

$$
\%CP_{db} = \dfrac{\%CP_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}} = \dfrac{11.37}{1 - 0.082} = \dfrac{11.37}{0.918} = 12.39\% \text{ db}
$$

> *Logic:* Wet basis reports protein per 100 g of sample as-is; dry basis reports per 100 g of dry matter. Since water is removed, all other components appear more concentrated on db.

**Final Answer:** %CP = **11.37% (wb) ; 12.39% (db)** — a typical value for defatted soybean flour.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Proximate Composition Analysis (14-Mark Synthesis)

**Problem:** Analyze the following data for a defatted groundnut cake: Moisture = 9.0% (wb); Ash = 5.2% (db); Crude Protein = 48.5% (db); Crude Fat (residual) = 1.8% (db); Crude Fiber = 4.6% (db). Calculate (a) %NFE, (b) Total Carbohydrate, and (c) Energy per 100 g on db.

#### Part (a): NFE Calculation

The Weende system uses 100% summation of all proximate fractions (on db):

$$
\%NFE = 100 - (\%M_{db} + \%CP + \%CF + \%CFib + \%Ash)
$$

First convert moisture to db:

$$
M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}} = \dfrac{0.09}{0.91} = 0.0989 = 9.89\% \text{ db}
$$

Now:

$$
\%NFE = 100 - (9.89 + 48.5 + 1.8 + 4.6 + 5.2)
$$

$$
\%NFE = 100 - 69.99 = 30.01\% \text{ db}
$$

> *Logic:* NFE is the "balance" — it captures all unmeasured CHO (mostly starch, sugars, soluble fiber). NFE + Crude Fiber = Total Carbohydrate.

#### Part (b): Total Carbohydrate

$$
\%TC = \%NFE + \%CFib = 30.01 + 4.6 = 34.61\% \text{ db}
$$

#### Part (c): Energy (Atwater Factors, per 100 g db)

$$
E = (4 \times P) + (9 \times F) + (4 \times C)
$$

$$
E = (4 \times 48.5) + (9 \times 1.8) + (4 \times 34.61)
$$

$$
E = 194.0 + 16.2 + 138.44
$$

$$
E = 348.64 \text{ kcal}/100 \text{ g db}
$$

Convert to wb:

$$
E_{wb} = E_{db} \times (1 - 0.09) = 348.64 \times 0.91 = 317.26 \text{ kcal}/100 \text{ g wb}
$$

**Final Answers:** %NFE = **30.01% db**; Total CHO = **34.61% db**; Energy = **348.64 kcal/100 g db (or 317.26 kcal/100 g wb)**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Amino Acid Score (AAS) for Cereal–Pulse Blend

**Problem:** A 60:40 (w/w) blend of rice (Protein = 7.0% db; Lysine = 3.5 g/16 g N) and chana dal (Protein = 21.0% db; Lysine = 7.2 g/16 g N) is prepared. The FAO reference protein requires Lysine = 5.5 g/16 g N. Calculate the AAS of the blend.

#### Step 1: Weighted Lysine Content
Each grain contributes protein and lysine in proportion to its share in the blend. Per 100 g blend:
- Rice protein = 60 × 0.070 = 4.20 g
- Chana protein = 40 × 0.210 = 8.40 g
- Total protein = 12.60 g

Weighted Lysine per 16 g N in blend:
- Lysine from rice = 4.20 × 3.5/16 = 0.919 g
- Lysine from chana = 8.40 × 7.2/16 = 3.780 g
- Total Lysine in blend = 4.699 g

Convert to per 16 g N:

$$
\text{Lysine (g/16 g N)} = \dfrac{4.699 \times 16}{12.60} = 5.967 \text{ g/16 g N}
$$

> *Logic:* The AAS is always expressed per 16 g of N (the protein reference standard), not per g of protein. This is the convention of FAO/WHO (1991).

#### Step 2: Compute AAS

$$
AAS = \dfrac{\text{Lysine in test protein}}{\text{Lysine in reference}} \times 100
$$

$$
AAS = \dfrac{5.967}{5.5} \times 100 = 108.5\%
$$

> *Logic:* Since AAS > 100, the blend is **not limited by lysine** — both cereals' and pulses' amino acid gaps are mutually filled. This is the **quantitative proof** of the cereal–pulse complementarity principle.

**Final Answer:** AAS (Lysine) = **108.5%** — blend is a **complete-quality protein source**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Quick-Reference Comparative Table

| Component | Cereals (Rice/Wheat) | Pulses (Chana/Lentil) | Oilseeds (Soy/Gnut) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Major Macronutrient** | Starch (70–80%) | Protein (20–30%) | Lipid (20–50%) |
| **Limiting Amino Acid** | Lysine | Methionine (+Cystine) | Variable (Soy: low Met; Gnut: balanced) |
| **Major Storage Protein** | Glutelin / Prolamin | Globulin (Legumin, Vicilin) | Globulin (Glycinin, β-conglycinin) |
| **Dominant ANF** | Phytic acid | Trypsin inhibitor, Lectins | Gossypol / Glucosinolate / Erucic acid |
| **Primary Vitamin** | B-complex (B₁, B₃) | Folate (B₉) | Vitamin E (Tocopherol) |
| **Major Mineral** | Fe, Zn (low bioavail.) | Fe, K, Mg | Ca (sesame), P, Mg |
| **Typical Moisture (storage)** | 12–14% wb | 9–12% wb | 7–9% wb |
| **Pericarp Dominant Fiber** | Cellulose, Hemicellulose | Cellulose, Hemicellulose, Pectin | Lignocellulose (hull) |
| **Functional Use** | Starch, Malt, Bioethanol | Dal, Besan, Tofu, Tempeh | Cooking oil, Protein meal |
| **Key Processing Step** | Milling (debranning) | Dehusking + Soaking | Crushing + Solvent extraction |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Nutrient Distribution in a Cereal Kernel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Whole Cereal Kernel - 100g] --> B[Pericarp Bran - 5 to 8%]
    A --> C[Endosperm - 80 to 85%]
    A --> D[Germ Embryo - 2 to 3%]
    A --> E[Aleurone Layer - 6 to 7%]

    B --> B1[Cellulose Hemicellulose Lignin]
    B --> B2[Vitamin B6 Folate]
    B --> B3[Phytic Acid bound P Fe Zn]

    C --> C1[Starch Amylose Amylopectin - 70 to 80%]
    C --> C2[Storage Protein Glutelin Prolamin - 8 to 12%]
    C --> C3[Simple Sugars Sucrose Glucose]

    D --> D1[Lipids PUFA - 15 to 20% of total oil]
    D --> D2[Vitamin E Tocopherol]
    D --> D3[Enzymes Lipase Lipoxygenase]

    E --> E1[Minerals Fe Zn Mg]
    E --> E2[Thiamine Niacin B6]
    E --> E3[High Protein Bodies]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **endosperm** is the carbohydrate-rich bulk; the **germ** is the lipid-vitamin-E-rich minority; the **bran + aleurone** house the minerals, B-vitamins, and most of the anti-nutrients. **Refining (white rice, maida) removes 25–30% of grain mass** but **~70% of micronutrients and 100% of germ lipids.**
