## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term *Special Dietary Foods*. Give two examples of products prepared from cereals, pulses, and oilseeds.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Definition (2 marks):* Special dietary foods are foods specially processed or formulated to fulfil specific dietary requirements arising from physiological, nutritional, or therapeutic needs of particular consumer groups. They are designed to deliver targeted health benefits beyond basic nutrition (FSSAI definition).

*Examples (1 mark):*
- **From Cereals:** Fortified breakfast cereal, gluten-free rice pasta.
- **From Pulses:** Soy protein isolate, weaning food (Bengal gram + wheat blend).
- **From Oilseeds:** Texturized soy protein (TVP), fortified sunflower oil with Vitamin A.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is **PDCAAS**? Why is it considered superior to the older PER method?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Definition (2 marks):* PDCAAS stands for *Protein Digestibility Corrected Amino Acid Score*. It evaluates protein quality on three grounds: (i) essential amino acid composition relative to FAO reference, (ii) limiting amino acid, and (iii) true digestibility. Maximum score is capped at **1.0 (or 100%)**.

*Superiority over PER (1 mark):* PER relies on animal (rat) growth studies, which are slow, expensive, and ethically constrained. PDCAAS uses both chemical scoring and human digestibility, giving a faster, reproducible, and more relevant evaluation.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 3 Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Two alternative questions are given per slot. The student answers **ONE** of the two.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the *manufacturing process of weaning food* with reference to raw material selection, processing steps, and quality parameters. Add a neat flow diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Explain *Extrusion Cooking* as a unit operation. Discuss the role of barrel temperature, screw speed, feed moisture, and SME in determining final product characteristics.

---

**Model Answer — (a) Weaning Food Manufacturing [7 Marks]:**

1. **Raw Material Selection (1 mark):** Cereals (rice, wheat) chosen for energy density; pulses (soy, green gram) chosen for lysine-rich protein complementation. Oilseed proteins (defatted soy flour) are added to lift protein quality.

2. **Formulation (1 mark):** Typically **70:30 cereal-to-pulse ratio**; fortified with **vitamin A, iron, calcium, folic acid** to meet ICMR RDA for infants aged 6–24 months.

3. **Processing Steps (3 marks):**
   - Cleaning and dehulling to remove anti-nutrients.
   - Roasting at **120–140°C** for 10–15 min OR malting to reduce paste viscosity and improve digestibility.
   - Twin-screw extrusion at **130–150°C** with **18–22% moisture** to gelatinize starch and denature anti-trypsin factors.
   - Drying to <4% moisture, milling to 200 mesh powder.
   - Vitamin-mineral premix addition.
   - Nitrogen-flushed airtight packaging.

4. **Quality Parameters (1 mark):** Viscosity < 3000 cP at 25% solids; protein ≥ 15%; moisture < 4%; PDCAAS ≥ 0.8; negative for coliforms, salmonella.

5. **Flow Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for a clean visual schematic.

---

**Model Answer — (b) Extrusion Cooking [7 Marks]:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Extrusion is a continuous, high-temperature short-time (HTST) process in which moistened, expansible food material is plasticized and forced through a die to give shape, texture, and structure.

2. **Types of Extruders (1 mark):** Single-screw (low-shear, for puffed snacks), twin-screw (high-shear, for texturized protein, weaning food), cold extruder (pasta, dough).

3. **Process Variables (3 marks):**
   - **Barrel Temperature (120–180°C):** Controls starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, and product expansion.
   - **Screw Speed (100–400 RPM):** Influences shear rate and SME. Higher RPM = higher SME, more molecular degradation.
   - **Feed Moisture (12–25%):** Low moisture → high expansion (puffed snack); high moisture (>50%) → fibrous, dense texture (TVP).
   - **SME (30–200 Wh/kg):** Master variable governing starch conversion, density, and crispness.

4. **Final Product Characteristics (1 mark):** Puff ratio, water solubility index (WSI), water absorption index (WAI), color, hardness, and shelf stability.

5. **Engineering Significance (1 mark):** Single-step integration of mixing-cooking-shaping reduces energy and labor cost by **40–60%** compared to conventional baking-frying routes.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the *production of Soy Protein Isolate (SPI)* with a process flow diagram. State the isoelectric precipitation principle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the concept of *Functional Foods*. Give suitable examples from oilseeds and cereals. Explain the role of β-glucan and isoflavones.

---

**Model Answer — (a) Soy Protein Isolate [7 Marks]:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** SPI is a highly refined soy protein with **≥ 90% protein (dry basis)**, obtained by removing non-protein constituents (fiber, sugars, ash) from defatted soy flakes.

2. **Raw Material (1 mark):** Defatted soy flakes/flour obtained after hexane extraction of soybeans (oil content < 1%).

3. **Process Steps (3 marks):**
   - **Alkaline Extraction:** Dispersing flakes in water at **1:15 (w/v)** ratio, pH adjusted to **9.0–10.0** with NaOH, stirred at **40–50°C for 30 min** to solubilize proteins.
   - **Centrifugation:** Removes insoluble fiber and residual fat.
   - **Acid Precipitation:** Filtrate acidified to **pH 4.5** (isoelectric point of glycinin/β-conglycinin) using HCl. Proteins flocculate.
   - **Washing and Recovery:** Curd washed, centrifuged, neutralized to pH 7.0.
   - **Pasteurization:** 70°C for 30 min.
   - **Spray Drying:** Inlet 180°C, outlet 90°C → fine powder.

4. **Isoelectric Precipitation Principle (1 mark):** At pH = pI, the **net charge on protein = 0**, eliminating electrostatic repulsion. Proteins aggregate and precipitate, allowing maximum recovery. For soy, pI = **4.5**.

5. **Flow Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.

---

**Model Answer — (b) Functional Foods [7 Marks]:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Functional foods are foods that, by virtue of physiologically active components, deliver specific health benefits beyond basic nutrition (Codex/IFIC definition).

2. **Cereal-Based Examples (2 marks):**
   - **Oats:** Rich in **β-D-glucan** (5.5–8% w/w), a soluble fiber that lowers serum LDL cholesterol by **5–7%** per 3 g/day intake (FDA claim).
   - **Whole Wheat:** Contains arabinoxylan and alkylresorcinols (anti-oxidant).
   - **Brown Rice:** γ-Oryzanol — anti-inflammatory, hypocholesterolemic.

3. **Oilseed-Based Examples (2 marks):**
   - **Soy:** Isoflavones (genistein, daidzein, glycitein) act as **phytoestrogens** — beneficial for menopausal symptoms, bone density, and cardiovascular risk.
   - **Flaxseed:** Lignans (secoisolariciresinol diglucoside — SDG) and α-linolenic acid (ALA, omega-3).
   - **Sunflower/Sesame:** Vitamin E (tocopherols), phytosterols (β-sitosterol).

4. **Role of Bioactives (2 marks):**
   - **β-Glucan:** Forms a viscous gel in the gut, slowing glucose absorption and binding bile acids → forces cholesterol conversion to bile, lowering LDL.
   - **Isoflavones:** Bind to estrogen receptors (ER-β), modulating hormone-related diseases; also antioxidant via free radical scavenging.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Fortification with Enrichment** — Fortification = *addition above* native level; Enrichment = *restoration* of lost nutrients. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for interchange.
> 2. **Always cite specific process parameters** (temperature, pH, moisture %) in extrusion/protein-isolate answers. Vague answers ("heat is applied") lose up to **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **For PDCAAS calculations, do not forget digestibility weighting** by composition ratio — most students compute PDCAAS using single-component digestibility, costing **2 marks**.
> 4. **Use the correct conversion factor (F)** in protein calculation: **5.7 for wheat, 6.25 for soy/milk, 5.95 for rice**. Wrong F is a frequent **1-mark deduction**.
> 5. **Mermaid diagrams in the exam** (when asked) must use clean, labeled blocks. Use arrows for flow direction, and label unit operations clearly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Special dietary foods** are formulated for specific nutritional/therapeutic needs, not general consumption.
- The three pillars of the raw material base are **Cereals, Pulses, and Oilseeds** — with soybean uniquely straddling *pulse* and *oilseed* classifications.
- **Protein Complementarity** is the foundation of weaning food formulation: cereals (rich in Met) + pulses (rich in Lys) → high biological value.
- **Extrusion cooking** is the *most important* unit operation; key variables are temperature (120–180°C), moisture (12–25%), screw speed (100–400 RPM), and SME (30–200 Wh/kg).
- **PDCAAS** is the modern FAO standard (max = 1.0); **PER** is the older rat-growth-based method.
- **Soy Protein Isolate (SPI)** contains **≥ 90% protein** and is produced via alkaline extraction (pH 9–10) and acid precipitation at **pH 4.5** (isoelectric point).
- **TVP** is produced by high-moisture extrusion of defatted soy flour; mimics meat texture.
- **Fortification** adds nutrients beyond native levels; **Enrichment** restores processing losses.
- **Functional food bioactives to memorize:** β-Glucan (oats — cholesterol), Isoflavones (soy — phytoestrogen), Lignans (flax — antioxidant), γ-Oryzanol (rice — anti-inflammatory), Phytosterols (sunflower — LDL-lowering).
- **Weaning food quality:** Viscosity < 3000 cP, PDCAAS ≥ 0.8, protein ≥ 15%, moisture < 4%, fortification with iron + vitamin A.
- **Puff Ratio** = $D_{extrudate} / D_{die}$; higher puff = lower density snack.
- **Anti-nutritional factors** (trypsin inhibitor, phytic acid, lectins) are destroyed by **roasting, malting, fermentation, or extrusion**.
- **Conversion factors for protein estimation:** Wheat = 5.7, Rice = 5.95, Soy/Milk = 6.25, Pulses = 6.25.
- **FSSAI** regulates fortification levels in India; **Codex Alimentarius** is the international reference for special dietary foods.
- **Glycemic Index (GI):** Low GI foods (<55) include oats, lentils, barley, soybean; High GI (>70) include white rice, white bread, instant mashed potato.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Milling of Oilseeds: Mechanical Expression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT403 - PROCESSING OF CEREALS, PULSES AND OILSEEDS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Milling of Oilseeds: Mechanical Expression # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Mechanical Expression** (also called **oil expelling** or **mechanical pressing**) is a unit operation in oilseed processing in which **oil is physically separated from the solid matrix of conditioned oilseed material by the application of external mechanical pressure**, without the use of chemical solvents. The process relies on the rupture of cellular and oleosin-bound oil bodies, followed by the drainage of liquid oil through the porous compressed cake structure. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PCFTT403 – Processing of Cereals, Pulses and Oilseeds*, mechanical expression is classified under the **primary oil recovery stage** of an oilseed milling operation, sitting between the **pre-treatment** (cleaning → dehulling → cracking → flaking → cooking) and the **secondary recovery / solvent extraction** stage. Mathematically, the goal of mechanical expression is to maximize the parameter called **Expression Efficiency (η)** while keeping the **residual oil in cake (ROC)** at acceptable industrial limits: $$\eta_{\text{exp}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Oil recovered by expression}}{\text{Total oil present in the feedstock}} \;\times\; 100\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Two principal classes of mechanical expression must be studied: > 1. **Batch Hydraulic Pressing** (e.g., box press, plate press, cage press) > 2. **Continuous Screw Pressing** (also called **expeller pressing**) > Modern industrial practice also recognizes **Cold-Pressing** as a sub-class of mechanical expression where the press cake temperature is held below **60 °C** to preserve bioactive compounds. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of mechanical expression as **squeezing a tube of toothpaste**: - The **tube body** is the cooked, flaked oilseed — it has been conditioned so its cells are already broken and the oil is free to move. - **Your hand** is the mechanical press. When you squeeze, the **paste (oil + some solids)** flows out the nozzle, while the **empty cap region (the cake)** remains behind. - The harder and more uniform the squeeze, the more "paste" you recover and the drier the "cap" you leave behind. In the real process, a **screw press** acts like a giant rotating screw inside a barrel that gradually **squeezes and shears** the material as it moves from a wider feed end to a narrow discharge end, with oil escaping through slotted bars. A **hydraulic press** does the same job in a **discrete batch** by applying a single enormous static force to a cake of material wrapped in cloth. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Summary:** Mechanical expression = *squeeze the oil out using pressure*. No chemicals, just brute force (in the engineering sense) applied to properly prepared seeds. --- ## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics Used in This Topic | Metric | Typical Industrial Value | Notes | |---|---|---| | Expression Efficiency ($\eta$) | **65 – 85 %** | Single-pass screw press | | Residual Oil in Cake (ROC) | **5 – 15 %** w/w | Depends on seed type & pre-treatment | | Cold-Press Cake Temperature | **< 60 °C** | Preserves tocopherols, phospholipids | | Hydraulic Press Pressure | **14 – 28 MPa** | Up to 2000+ psi | | Screw Press Compression Ratio | **1 : 6 to 1 : 25** | Volume of feed to discharge zone | | Press Cake Moisture | **3 – 6 %** w/w | After cooking/conditioning | > [!TIP] > These numbers appear directly in KTU numericals — memorize the typical ranges, not just the formula. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Background — Why Pressure Alone Is Not Enough Mechanical expression succeeds only when **three simultaneous conditions** are met inside the press: 1. **Cellular Disruption** — The seed's oleosin-stabilized oil bodies must be ruptured. This is achieved by the upstream **cooking/conditioning** step (moisture adjustment to **8–10 %** and heating to **80–110 °C**), which denatures proteins and collapses cell walls. 2. **Phase Separation (Filtration through the cake)** — The expressed oil must find a continuous drainage path. This is the **Darcy-type flow of oil through a compressible porous cake**. 3. **Pressure Gradient Generation** — A mechanical force gradient must push the oil *outward* (perpendicular to the press axis) faster than the cake matrix compacts. If the seed is **under-cooked**, cell walls are intact and oil yield collapses. If it is **over-cooked**, proteins denature too aggressively, the cake becomes impermeable, and the press "chokes" (an industrial term for barrel blockage). > [!IMPORTANT] > The famous **Beverloo–Sherman capillary flow law** (used in powder compaction) and the **Terzaghi consolidation model** (from soil mechanics) are the two theoretical pillars frequently borrowed in KTU exam questions to *explain* why expression efficiency plateaus beyond a certain pressure. --- ## 2.2 Pre-treatment Sequence That Conditions Feed for Expression Mechanical expression is the **last** unit operation in a chain. Its efficiency depends on upstream quality: - **Step 1 — Cleaning:** removal of stones, husks, foreign seeds. - **Step 2 — Dehulling:** removal of fibrous hull (critical for soybean, sunflower). - **Step 3 — Cracking / Grinding:** size reduction to **2–4 mm grits** to break cotyledon structure. - **Step 4 — Flaking:** rolling to **0.25 – 0.40 mm** flakes to rupture oil cells mechanically. - **Step 5 — Cooking / Conditioning:** moisture adjustment to **8–10 %** and temperature **80–110 °C** for **15 – 30 min** in a stack cooker. - **Step 6 — Mechanical Expression** ← *current topic* - **Step 7 — Cake Breaking & Solvent Extraction** (for residual oil < 1 %). > [!NOTE] > Cooking causes **protein coagulation**, which **decreases oil's affinity for the solid matrix** — this is the single biggest reason pre-treatment is non-negotiable. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following markdown table is the **high-yield reference** for Part A and Part B numericals: | # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{oil,out}}}{W_{\text{oil,in}}} \times 100$ | Expression Efficiency | % | | 2 | $\eta_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{O_{\text{seed}} - O_{\text{cake}}}{O_{\text{seed}}} \times 100$ | Efficiency via oil balance | % | | 3 | $O_{\text{cake}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{oil,cake}}}{W_{\text{cake}}} \times 100$ | Residual oil content of cake | % w/w | | 4 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Hydraulic Pressure (Pascal's law) | Pa (N/m²) | | 5 | $CR = \dfrac{V_{\text{feed}}}{V_{\text{discharge}}}$ | Screw Press Compression Ratio | dimensionless | | 6 | $P_{\text{screw}} \approx \mu \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2 N^2 D^3}{60^2}$ | Empirical screw pressure rise (shaft torque model) | Pa | | 7 | $Q = \rho \cdot v_{\text{axial}} \cdot A_{\text{barrel}}$ | Volumetric Throughput | m³/s or kg/h | | 8 | $\Delta P_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{128 \mu Q L}{\pi D^4}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille oil flow in cake capillaries | Pa | | 9 | $F_{\text{press}} = P_{\text{ram}} \times A_{\text{ram}}$ | Total hydraulic force | N | | 10 | $W_{\text{work}} = \int P \cdot dV$ | Work of compression in batch press | J | > [!WARNING] > When substituting in any table-based numerical, **always convert pressure to consistent SI units** (1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa, 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa). KTU examiners explicitly test unit-conversion discipline. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Mechanical expression is the workhorse of the **edible oil industry** for two practical reasons: 1. **Solvent-Free Product** — Cold-pressed oils qualify as *natural* under FSSAI / EU regulations and command a **2×–5× premium** over solvent-extracted oils. 2. **Lower Capital Cost for Small Operations** — A 5 TPD screw press line costs a fraction of an extraction plant, making it the default for **khadi / cottage-scale** oil units in Kerala and Karnataka. The **cake** left behind (containing **15 – 35 % protein** and 5 – 15 % residual oil) is a high-value byproduct used as **cattle feed, fertilizer, or protein isolate feedstock**, so the design goal is *maximize oil recovery **and** cake quality simultaneously*. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expression Efficiency From Mass Balance The expression efficiency is the heart of nearly every KTU numerical. Let us derive it from a first-principles mass balance. ### Statement Given an oilseed charge of mass $M_{\text{seed}}$ containing $O_{\text{seed}}$ percent oil, and a press cake of mass $M_{\text{cake}}$ containing $O_{\text{cake}}$ percent residual oil, prove that: $$\eta_{\text{exp}} \;=\; \frac{O_{\text{seed}} - O_{\text{cake}} \cdot (1 - m_{\text{evap}})}{O_{\text{seed}}} \times 100$$ where $m_{\text{evap}}$ is the fractional moisture lost during cooking. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Total oil entering the press:** $$W_{\text{oil,in}} = \frac{O_{\text{seed}}}{100} \cdot M_{\text{seed}}$$ This is the mass of oil originally locked inside the seed cells that survive cooking. **Step 2 — Oil retained in the cake after expression:** $$W_{\text{oil,cake}} = \frac{O_{\text{cake}}}{100} \cdot M_{\text{cake}}$$ This oil remains trapped in the compressed cake because capillary forces and the solid matrix physically block further drainage. **Step 3 — Mass balance on the oil phase:** By conservation of mass, the oil that *left* the system is the difference between what entered and what stayed in the cake: $$W_{\text{oil,out}} = W_{\text{oil,in}} - W_{\text{oil,cake}}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the definition of efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{exp}} = \frac{W_{\text{oil,out}}}{W_{\text{oil,in}}} \times 100$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the expressions from Step 1 and Step 2:** $$\eta_{\text{exp}} = \frac{\dfrac{O_{\text{seed}}}{100} M_{\text{seed}} - \dfrac{O_{\text{cake}}}{100} M_{\text{cake}}}{\dfrac{O_{\text{seed}}}{100} M_{\text{seed}}} \times 100$$ **Step 6 — Cancel the common factor $(1/100) M_{\text{seed}}$ in numerator and denominator:** $$\eta_{\text{exp}} = \frac{O_{\text{seed}} - O_{\text{cake}} \cdot \dfrac{M_{\text{cake}}}{M_{\text{seed}}}}{O_{\text{seed}}} \times 100$$ **Step 7 — Account for moisture lost in cooking (the *dry basis* correction):** The cake mass $M_{\text{cake}}$ is less than $M_{\text{seed}}$ partly because of moisture evaporation during cooking. Introducing the mass-loss fraction $m_{\text{evap}}$: $$\frac{M_{\text{cake}}}{M_{\text{seed}}} \approx (1 - m_{\text{evap}})$$ **Step 8 — Final compact form:** $$\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{exp}} = \frac{O_{\text{seed}} - O_{\text{cake}} \cdot (1 - m_{\text{evap}})}{O_{\text{seed}}} \times 100\;}$$ This is the textbook formula KTU examiners expect. If $m_{\text{evap}} = 0$ (wet basis, ignoring cooking losses), it reduces to the simpler form in Section 2.3, equation (2). --- ### Worked Numerical — Standard KTU Type **Problem:** A screw press processes **1000 kg of conditioned copra** per hour. The copra contains **65 % oil** (w/w). After pressing, the cake contains **8 % oil** (w/w) and weighs **340 kg/h**. Moisture loss in cooking is **3 %**. Calculate: 1. Expression efficiency. 2. Oil recovered per hour. 3. Residual oil lost to cake per hour. **Solution Walkthrough — All Steps Shown:** **Step 1 — Oil input rate:** $$W_{\text{oil,in}} = 0.65 \times 1000 = 650 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Step 2 — Oil retained in cake:** $$W_{\text{oil,cake}} = 0.08 \times 340 = 27.2 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Step 3 — Oil recovered (output):** $$W_{\text{oil,out}} = 650 - 27.2 = 622.8 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Step 4 — Mass-loss fraction:** $$m_{\text{evap}} = 0.03$$ $$1 - m_{\text{evap}} = 0.97$$ **Step 5 — Efficiency using the dry-basis-corrected formula:** $$\eta_{\text{exp}} = \frac{65 - 8 \times 0.97}{65} \times 100$$ $$\eta_{\text{exp}} = \frac{65 - 7.76}{65} \times 100 = \frac{57.24}{65} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{\text{exp}} \approx 88.06 \%}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner Note:** If you forget the $(1 - m_{\text{evap}})$ correction, you get $\eta = 87.69\%$, which is technically wrong on a *dry basis*. KTU's valuation key explicitly docks **1 mark** for this oversight in 14-mark questions. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydraulic Press Force Requirement ### Statement A batch hydraulic press uses a ram of diameter $D_r$ to apply pressure $P_r$ onto a cake of cross-section $A_c$. Derive the relationship between applied force, ram pressure, and cake pressure. ### Derivation **Step 1 — Pascal's law applied to the hydraulic fluid:** Pressure at the ram equals pressure throughout the incompressible fluid column: $$P_{\text{ram}} = \frac{F_{\text{ram}}}{A_{\text{ram}}}$$ **Step 2 — Mechanical advantage from the platen:** The platen (piston pressing on the cake) typically has the *same* area as the cake. By force balance on the rigid platen: $$F_{\text{cake}} = F_{\text{ram}} = P_{\text{ram}} \cdot A_{\text{ram}}$$ **Step 3 — Cake pressure (pressure delivered *into* the meal):** $$P_{\text{cake}} = \frac{F_{\text{cake}}}{A_{\text{cake}}}$$ **Step 4 — Combine Steps 2 and 3:** $$P_{\text{cake}} = P_{\text{ram}} \cdot \frac{A_{\text{ram}}}{A_{\text{cake}}}$$ In most plate-press designs, $A_{\text{ram}} = A_{\text{cake}}$, so $P_{\text{cake}} = P_{\text{ram}}$. The hydraulic system simply acts as a force multiplier because the *pump* side has a smaller piston, but the *working* cylinder matches the cake area. **Step 5 — Numerical check:** If $D_r = 0.20$ m and $P_r = 20$ MPa: $$A_{\text{ram}} = \frac{\pi D_r^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.20)^2}{4} = 0.0314 \text{ m}^2$$ $$F_{\text{ram}} = P_{\text{ram}} \cdot A_{\text{ram}} = 20 \times 10^6 \times 0.0314$$ $$F_{\text{ram}} = 6.28 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 628 \text{ kN}$$ This is roughly **64 tonnes-force** — typical of an industrial box press. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Screw Press Compression Ratio ### Statement A screw press has a feed-root diameter $D_f$ and a discharge-root diameter $D_d$ (after the choke). Show that for a constant-pitch worm, the volumetric compression ratio is: $$CR = \left(\frac{D_f}{D_d}\right)^2$$ ### Derivation **Step 1 — Volume per unit pitch at the feed zone:** For a screw with pitch $p$, the cross-sectional area of material carried per pitch is proportional to the square of the *root* diameter (the open volume inside the screw flight): $$V_f \propto \pi \left(\frac{D_f}{2}\right)^2 \cdot p = \frac{\pi D_f^2 p}{4}$$ **Step 2 — Same at the discharge zone:** $$V_d \propto \frac{\pi D_d^2 p}{4}$$ **Step 3 — Ratio (definition of compression ratio):** $$CR = \frac{V_f}{V_d} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi D_f^2 p}{4}}{\dfrac{\pi D_d^2 p}{4}}$$ **Step 4 — Cancel common factors $\pi$, $p$, and $4$:** $$\boxed{\;CR = \left(\frac{D_f}{D_d}\right)^2\;}$$ If $D_f = 0.15$ m and $D_d = 0.04$ m: $$CR = (0.15 / 0.04)^2 = (3.75)^2 = 14.06$$ This is squarely in the **typical industrial range of 6 – 25**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When To Use Which Press | Criterion | Hydraulic Press (Batch) | Screw Press (Continuous) | |---|---|---| | Throughput | **Low (50–500 kg/batch)** | **High (1–50 TPD)** | | Capital cost | Lower | Higher | | Labour intensity | Higher | Lower | | Cake residual oil | **4 – 7 %** | **6 – 15 %** | | Oil clarity | High (clarified by cloth) | Moderate (needs settling/filtering) | | Typical seeds | Coconut, palm kernel, olive | Soybean, sunflower, mustard, groundnut | | Working pressure | **14 – 28 MPa** | Built by screw geometry (no external gauge) | | Cold-pressing suitability | Excellent | Good (low-RPM variant) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Complete Oilseed Milling Process (Block Architecture) The following Mermaid block diagram shows the upstream-to-downstream architecture in which mechanical expression is embedded. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Raw Oilseeds] --> A2[Cleaning and Destoning] A2 --> A3[Dehulling] A3 --> A4[Cracking and Grinding] A4 --> A5[Flaking to 0.25 to 0.40 mm] A5 --> A6[Cooking and Conditioning 80 to 110 C] A6 --> A7[Mechanical Expression] A7 --> A8[Crude Oil] A7 --> A9[Press Cake] A9 --> A10[Solvent Extraction] A10 --> A11[Defatted Meal] A10 --> A12[Micella] A12 --> A13[Oil Refining] A8 --> A13 A13 --> A14[Refined Edible Oil] ``` --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Functional Block Architecture of a Screw Press (Internal Cross-Section Logic) The true geometry of a screw press is a tapered worm inside a slotted barrel. We render it as a block-level architecture to capture the *functional zones* of compression, drainage, and discharge. ```mermaid flowchart TB B1[Feed Hopper
Conditioned Flakes] --> B2[Feed Zone
Large Pitch Worm
Conveys Material] B2 --> B3[Compression Zone 1
Pitch Decreases
Pressure Rises] B3 --> B4[Compression Zone 2
Smaller Pitch
Oil Begins to Drain] B4 --> B5[Choke Zone
Discharge Restrictor
Back Pressure Peak] B5 --> B6[Discharge Cone
Cake Expelled] B4 --> B7[Barrel Slots
Oil Drainage] B3 --> B7 B7 --> B8[Crude Oil Collection Trough] B6 --> B9[Cake Breaker and Cooler] ``` > [!NOTE] > In a real screw press, **barrel slots are radial perforations along the barrel wall** in the compression zones. The choke (often a hydraulically adjustable cone) is what creates the back-pressure necessary for high expression efficiency. --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Hydraulic Box Press Functional Architecture A hydraulic press operates on a **batch basis**. The functional block diagram below shows the closed-loop force path. ```mermaid flowchart LR C1[Hydraulic Pump
High Pressure] --> C2[Directional Control Valve] C2 --> C3[Main Cylinder
Large Bore Piston] C3 --> C4[Pressing Platen] C4 --> C5[Stack of Cakes
Wrapped in Press Cloth] C5 --> C6[Steel Plates
and Drainage Frame] C6 --> C7[Static Base Platen] C5 --> C8[Oil Collection Channel] C8 --> C9[Crude Oil Tank] C1 --> C10[Pressure Relief Valve] C10 --> C1 ``` > [!TIP] > The **pressing cycle** of a box press is typically: *load → close → press at 14 MPa for 20 min → release → unload*. KTU problems often ask you to estimate **cycle time and throughput** from these parameters. --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Decision Tree: Selecting Mechanical Expression Method ```mermaid flowchart TD D1{Seed Type and Scale} --> D2[High Oil 45 percent plus
Soft Seed] D1 --> D3[Medium Oil 20 to 45 percent
Hard Seed] D1 --> D4[Low Oil below 20 percent] D2 --> E1[Coconut or Olive or Palm Kernel] E1 --> E2[Hydraulic Press Preferred] D3 --> E3[Soybean or Sunflower or Mustard] E3 --> E4[Screw Press Preferred] D4 --> E5[Most Economical Route] E5 --> E6[Skip Mechanical
Go Direct to Solvent] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > The following questions follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern**: Part A (2 marks each) and Part B (14 marks each with internal choice). Marks are tagged to specific **Course Outcomes (CO)** of PCFTT403. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 / 3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(3 Marks)** **Define mechanical expression of oilseeds. List the two principal methods used industrially.** **Model Answer:** Mechanical expression is the unit operation in which oil is physically separated from the oil-bearing cells of conditioned oilseed material by the application of mechanical pressure, without the use of chemical solvents. The two principal industrial methods are: 1. **Batch hydraulic pressing** — uses a hydraulic ram to apply static pressure (typically 14–28 MPa) to stacked cakes wrapped in cloth, in discrete cycles. 2. **Continuous screw pressing (expeller pressing)** — uses a rotating tapered worm inside a slotted barrel to develop progressive pressure and continuously expel oil and cake. --- ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(3 Marks)** **Why is cooking/conditioning of oilseed flakes an essential pre-treatment before mechanical expression?** **Model Answer:** Cooking/conditioning (moisture 8–10 %, temperature 80–110 °C, time 15–30 min) is essential because it: - **Denatures proteins** in the seed matrix, reducing their oil-binding capacity. - **Coagulates mucilaginous gums** that would otherwise block the drainage capillaries in the cake. - **Ruptures intact oil cells** through thermal expansion. - **Reduces oil viscosity**, improving flow through the porous cake. Without cooking, the press would experience **low oil yield** and severe **barrel choking**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > KTU 2024 ESE internal choice means: *within the same module, you may answer either Question A or Question B.* --- ### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **complete mechanical expression process** including upstream pre-treatment and downstream cake handling. Discuss the role of **flaking** and **cooking** in determining expression efficiency. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A continuous screw press handles **2000 kg/h of conditioned soybean** containing **20 % oil (w/w)**. The cake discharged contains **6 % oil (w/w)** and weighs **1650 kg/h**. Moisture lost in cooking is **2 %**. Calculate the **expression efficiency**, **oil recovered per hour**, and comment on whether this efficiency is acceptable for a single-pass screw press. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) The block diagram (Section 4.1) shows the complete chain: `Cleaning → Dehulling → Cracking → Flaking → Cooking → Mechanical Expression → Crude Oil + Press Cake` - **Flaking** rolls cracked grits into **0.25–0.40 mm flakes**, mechanically rupturing a large fraction of oil cells. A thinner flake means **shorter drainage paths** for oil, which directly raises efficiency. However, flakes thinner than **0.20 mm** generate excessive fines that clog the barrel slots. - **Cooking** then **thermally denatures proteins** and reduces oil viscosity, allowing the ruptured oil to flow out freely. - The combination is synergistic: *flaking provides the broken cells, cooking provides the free-flowing oil*. Together they push expression efficiency into the **75–85 %** range typical of single-pass screw pressing. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Oil input rate:** $$W_{\text{oil,in}} = 0.20 \times 2000 = 400 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Step 2 — Oil retained in cake:** $$W_{\text{oil,cake}} = 0.06 \times 1650 = 99 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Step 3 — Oil recovered:** $$W_{\text{oil,out}} = 400 - 99 = 301 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Step 4 — Moisture-loss factor:** $$1 - m_{\text{evap}} = 1 - 0.02 = 0.98$$ **Step 5 — Expression efficiency (dry-basis-corrected):** $$\eta_{\text{exp}} = \frac{20 - 6 \times 0.98}{20} \times 100 = \frac{20 - 5.88}{20} \times 100$$ $$\eta_{\text{exp}} = \frac{14.12}{20} \times 100 = 70.6\%$$ **Step 6 — Comment on acceptability:** A single-pass screw press efficiency of **70.6 %** is in the **lower end of the typical industrial range (65–85 %)**. It is **acceptable** for cold-pressing or premium-grade oil, where solvent extraction is intentionally avoided. For a commodity-grade operation, the cake (containing 6 % residual oil) would be sent to a **solvent extraction plant** to push total recovery beyond **99 %**. --- ### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Compare **hydraulic pressing** and **screw pressing** of oilseeds with respect to working principle, capacity, cake residual oil, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A batch hydraulic press has a **ram of diameter 25 cm**. The hydraulic pump delivers fluid at **20 MPa** to a working cylinder of the same diameter as the cake. The cake cross-section is **30 cm × 30 cm**. Calculate **(i)** the force exerted on the cake, **(ii)** the pressure transmitted into the cake, and **(iii)** the work done if the ram advances **12 cm** against the cake. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) | Parameter | Hydraulic Press | Screw Press | |---|---|---| | Principle | Static pressure from a hydraulic ram on stacked cakes | Progressive pressure from a rotating tapered worm | | Cycle | Batch (discrete cycles) | Continuous | | Capacity | 50–500 kg/batch | 1–50 TPD | | Cake residual oil | **4–7 %** (lower) | **6–15 %** (higher) | | Typical seeds | Coconut, palm kernel, olive | Soybean, sunflower, mustard, groundnut | | Capital | Lower | Higher | | Oil clarity | High (cloth filtration) | Moderate (needs settling) | | Cold-pressing | Excellent | Good (low-RPM) | #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Ram area:** $$A_{\text{ram}} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.25)^2}{4} = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Force on cake (i):** $$F = P_{\text{ram}} \times A_{\text{ram}} = 20 \times 10^6 \times 0.04909$$ $$\boxed{F \approx 9.82 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \;\;(\approx 100 \text{ tonnes-force})}$$ **Step 3 — Cake area:** $$A_{\text{cake}} = 0.30 \times 0.30 = 0.09 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4 — Pressure into cake (ii):** $$P_{\text{cake}} = \frac{F}{A_{\text{cake}}} = \frac{9.82 \times 10^5}{0.09}$$ $$\boxed{P_{\text{cake}} \approx 10.91 \text{ MPa}}$$ **Step 5 — Work done (iii) — Ram displacement 0.12 m:** $$W = F \times d = 9.82 \times 10^5 \times 0.12$$ $$\boxed{W \approx 1.18 \times 10^5 \text{ J} = 118 \text{ kJ}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks in this module:** > 1. **Forgetting the moisture-loss factor $(1 - m_{\text{evap}})$** → −1 mark per occurrence. > 2. **Mixing wet-basis and dry-basis oil percentages** without stating the assumption → −1 mark. > 3. **Unit confusion in pressure** (mixing MPa, bar, psi) → −0.5 to −1 mark. > 4. **Confusing expression efficiency with extraction efficiency** — they are *different* parameters; mechanical expression alone rarely exceeds 90 %. > 5. **Skipping the block diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts asking "with a neat diagram" → −2 marks. > 6. **Reporting residual oil as a *fraction* instead of % w/w** → −0.5 mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition (must memorize):** Mechanical expression = pressure-driven oil recovery without solvent. - **Two main types:** Hydraulic (batch) and Screw (continuous). - **Pre-treatment is non-negotiable:** Cleaning → Dehulling → Cracking → Flaking → Cooking. - **Cooking conditions to remember:** 8–10 % moisture, 80–110 °C, 15–30 min. - **Core formula #1:** $\eta_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{O_{\text{seed}} - O_{\text{cake}} (1 - m_{\text{evap}})}{O_{\text{seed}}} \times 100$. - **Core formula #2:** $\eta_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{oil,out}}}{W_{\text{oil,in}}} \times 100$. - **Core formula #3:** $P = F / A$ for hydraulic; $CR = (D_f / D_d)^2$ for screw. - **Typical single-pass efficiency:** 65 – 85 %. - **Typical residual oil in cake:** 5 – 15 %. - **Cold-pressing limit:** cake temperature < 60 °C. - **Industrial pressure:** 14 – 28 MPa for hydraulic press. - **Compression ratio range:** 1 : 6 to 1 : 25 for screw press. - **Byproduct:** Press cake (15–35 % protein, 5–15 % residual oil) → cattle feed / solvent extraction. - **Common exam trap:** Hydraulics → ram *force* is huge (hundreds of kN) but cake *pressure* depends on cake area, not ram area, when they differ. - **Engineering insight:** Expression efficiency plateaus at high pressure due to cake impermeability — this is why *combined mechanical + solvent* systems are standard in industry. - **Cold-pressed premium:** Solvent-free oils fetch 2×–5× the market price of refined solvent-extracted oils. - **Choking:** If pre-treatment is wrong, the screw press barrel blocks — a frequent viva question. > [!TIP] > **Last-minute revision strategy:** Memorize the **mass-balance derivation (Section 3.1)**, the **hydraulic force formula (Section 3.2)**, the **compression ratio formula (Section 3.3)**, and the **comparison matrix (Section 3.4)**. These four items alone account for **~10 of 14 marks** in any KTU Part B question on this topic.

topics.title: Screw Press
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT403 - PROCESSING OF CEREALS, PULSES AND OILSEEDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Screw Press

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Screw Press (Expeller)**: A continuous mechanical oil extraction device that uses a helical screw (worm) rotating inside a slotted cylindrical barrel (cage) to generate progressive compressive and shear forces on oil-bearing material, thereby rupturing oil cells and expelling liquid oil through the cage openings while discharging the de-oiled solid residue as a continuous cake through a variable-restriction choke cone.

The screw press is classified as a **mechanical expression device** and is the most widely employed continuous oil extraction equipment in the **small-to-medium scale vegetable oil industry**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCFTT403, the screw press is positioned as the principal industrial alternative to **solvent extraction** and **hydraulic pressing**, especially for high-oil-content seeds such as coconut, groundnut, sunflower, sesame, mustard, and soybean (after thermal conditioning).

### 1.1.1 Operating Principle (One-Line Summary)

The screw press converts **rotational mechanical energy** into **uniaxial compression and shear** along the axis of a tapered helical chamber, forcing oil out through longitudinal cage slots while compacting the residual meal into a cake.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Garlic Press / Sausage Stuffer Analogy**
> Imagine a garlic press or a traditional *murukku* / *sev* maker at home. You place crushed garlic or dough into a barrel, turn a handle attached to an Archimedes screw, and the material is pushed forward while being squeezed against a perforated cylinder. Juice (oil in our case) escapes through the small holes, and the dry pulp (cake) is expelled from the tip.
> 
> A screw press is the **industrial-scale, motorized, high-pressure version** of this exact concept — but instead of a few atmospheres, the screw press can generate internal pressures of **1,000 – 5,000 bar (≈ 100 – 500 MPa)** inside the cage.

### 1.2.1 Real-World Context

- **Coconut Oil Industry** in Kerala: Traditional *chekku* (oil ghani) is essentially a low-pressure cousin of the screw press; the modern **expeller** is its engineered successor.
- **Groundnut Oil Mills**: Continuous screw presses handle **5 – 50 tonnes/day** of seed, replacing the laborious batch hydraulic press.
- **Solvent Plants**: Screw pressing is used as a **pre-pressing stage** before solvent extraction to reduce residual oil in the cake to **15–18%**, lowering solvent load.

### 1.2.2 Standard Operating Metrics (Industry Benchmarks)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Pressure | **1,000 – 5,000 bar** | Inside the barrel |
| Screw Speed | **5 – 30 RPM** | Lower for hard seeds |
| Residual Oil in Cake | **5 – 8%** | Single-pass; pre-press |
| Residual Oil in Cake | **2 – 4%** | Pre-press + solvent |
| Capacity | **0.5 – 100 TPD** | Tonnes Per Day |
| Barrel Length-to-Diameter | **4 : 1 to 7 : 1** | Standard proportion |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024, students must remember the **barrel L/D ratio (4:1 to 7:1)** and **residual oil benchmark (5–8%)** as these are commonly asked in 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components and Constructional Details

A screw press consists of the following major subsystems:

### 2.1.1 Worm Shaft (Screw / Auger)
- A horizontal helical screw made of **hardened alloy steel**.
- The **pitch** (axial distance between consecutive flights) typically **decreases** from the feed end to the discharge end — this is the primary mechanism for **pressure build-up**.
- Often built in **2 to 4 sections** (called worms), mounted on a common shaft with intermediate **breaker / dam bars** to increase back-pressure and shear.

### 2.1.2 Barrel / Cage
- A horizontal cylindrical casing made of **vertical bars** (called cage bars or drainage bars) separated by narrow gaps (typically **0.4 – 0.8 mm**).
- Bars are held together by horizontal tie rods and spacer collars.
- The bar gaps allow oil to escape but retain the **solid meal cake**.
- Configurations:
  - **Full-Cage Barrel** – bars enclose the entire worm (highest pressure).
  - **Half-Cage Barrel** – bars enclose only the bottom half (used for high-viscosity oils like coconut).
  - **Open Barrel** – minimal cage, used as **pre-press** in two-stage plants.

### 2.1.3 Choke Mechanism (Cone / Cutter)
- A hydraulically or mechanically adjustable conical discharge at the end of the barrel.
- Increasing choke opening → lower pressure, wetter cake, higher throughput.
- Decreasing choke opening → higher pressure, drier cake, lower throughput (risk of overheating).
- **Knife attachments** at the cone cut the extruded cake into uniform **flakes/pellets**.

### 2.1.4 Feeding System
- **Hopper with feeder screw** (variable speed) ensures a constant, metered flow of conditioned seed.
- Critical to avoid choking or surging of the barrel.

### 2.1.5 Drive and Power Transmission
- **Electric motor** coupled via a **gear reducer** to bring RPM down from ~1,440 to 5–30.
- Modern presses use **VFD (Variable Frequency Drives)** for screw speed optimization.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The screw press operates on **four coupled physical phenomena**: Conveying → Compression → Shear → Filtration. KTU questions often test the *order* and the *role* of each.

1. **Conveying**: Rotating flights of the worm push the seed mass axially from the feed inlet toward the discharge cone.

2. **Compression**: As the pitch decreases or the root diameter increases, the **free volume per revolution decreases** → seed is squeezed into progressively smaller spaces → pressure rises exponentially.

3. **Shear**: The relative motion between the screw flights and the cage bars creates **inter-particle shear and frictional heating** (typically **50–90 °C**). Heat lowers oil viscosity (μₒᵢₗ ↓) and aids filtration.

4. **Filtration**: Liquid oil, under pressure, flows radially outward through the cage bar gaps. The de-oiled cake, now a **semi-plastic solid**, continues forward and exits through the choke as a continuous **extrudate**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **examination-critical** for Module 4. Memorize the variables and the limiting assumptions.

### Table 2.1 — Master Formula Sheet for Screw Press Design and Operation

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,(D_o^2 - D_s^2)\,P\,N$ | $Q_{th}$ = theoretical throughput (m³/min), $D_o$ = screw OD (m), $D_s$ = shaft dia (m), $P$ = pitch (m), $N$ = RPM | Volumetric displacement of the screw per minute |
| 2 | $Q_{a} = Q_{th} \cdot \phi \cdot \eta_f$ | $Q_a$ = actual throughput (kg/h), $\phi$ = bulk density (kg/m³), $\eta_f$ = fill factor (0.4–0.7) | Actual mass flow rate of material |
| 3 | $P(x) = P_0\, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\mu\,x}{h}\right)$ | $P(x)$ = pressure at distance $x$, $P_0$ = inlet pressure, $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $h$ = flight depth | Pressure rise along the barrel due to friction |
| 4 | $\dfrac{Q_o}{Q_s} = \dfrac{O_s - O_c}{100 - O_c}$ | $Q_o$ = oil flow rate, $Q_s$ = seed flow rate, $O_s$ = oil % in seed, $O_c$ = oil % in cake | Mass balance for oil–cake split |
| 5 | $\eta_{extr} = \dfrac{O_s - O_c}{O_s} \times 100$ | $\eta_{extr}$ = extraction efficiency (%), $O_s$, $O_c$ = oil content (%) | Fraction of original oil recovered |
| 6 | $N_s = \dfrac{Q_s \cdot \rho}{A \cdot \phi}$ | $N_s$ = screw speed (RPM), $A$ = cross-sectional area, $\rho$ = bulk density | Speed needed for a given throughput |
| 7 | $W_{req} = \dfrac{P_{avg} \cdot Q_s}{\eta_m}$ | $W_{req}$ = required power (W), $P_{avg}$ = average barrel pressure, $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency (0.4–0.6) | Motor power sizing |
| 8 | $\text{SLI} = \dfrac{L}{D_o}$ | SLI = Screw Length Index, $L$ = barrel length, $D_o$ = screw OD | Geometric ratio (typical 4–7) |
| 9 | $P_{mech} = \dfrac{2\pi\,N\,T}{60}$ | $P_{mech}$ = mechanical power (W), $N$ = RPM, $T$ = torque (N·m) | Power from torque-speed relation |
| 10 | $\Delta P = P_{choke} - P_{atm}$ | $\Delta P$ = back-pressure generated by choke | Pressure driving cake through discharge |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: In equations involving nested fractions, never use the vertical bar `\|` for absolute value inside markdown tables. Always write $\vert x \vert$ or use the word "magnitude of". This has caused table-rendering failures in past answer scripts compiled digitally.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility and Engineering Significance

The screw press is the **workhorse of the Indian and South-East Asian oil milling industry**. Its engineering relevance for KTU 2024 students includes:

- **Process Engineering**: Selection of screw geometry for different seeds (coconut vs. groundnut vs. soybean).
- **Energy Engineering**: Screw pressing consumes **~50–80 kWh/tonne** of seed — far less than solvent extraction's thermal equivalent.
- **Food Engineering**: Absence of solvent residues makes screw-pressed oil preferable for **cold-pressed / virgin / organic** certification (relevant to product development modules).
- **Environmental Engineering**: Lower effluent load (no miscella, no distillation residue) compared to solvent plants.

---

## 2.5 Effect of Operating Variables on Performance

| Variable | Effect on Throughput | Effect on Residual Oil | Effect on Cake Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Screw Speed | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases (wetter) | Crumbly, less compressed |
| ↓ Choke Opening | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases (drier) | Hard, glossy, thin flakes |
| ↑ Moisture Content (8–12%) | ↑ Initially, then ↓ | ↓ Decreases (plasticization) | Best at optimum moisture |
| ↑ Seed Temperature (50–90 °C) | ↑ Slightly | ↓ Decreases | Darker color (Maillard risk) |
| ↑ Barrel L/D Ratio | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases | Drier, harder cake |
| ↑ Bar Gap (0.4→0.8 mm) | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | Wetter cake, more fines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners **frequently test** the *inverse relationship between throughput and residual oil* and the *existence of an optimum seed moisture (typically 8–10%)* before pressing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below follow KTU 2024 Scheme's emphasis on **RBT Levels L3 (Apply) and L4 (Analyze)**. Each algebraic step is shown explicitly with explanatory annotations.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Throughput of a Screw Press

### 3.1.1 Statement
Derive the expression for the **theoretical volumetric throughput** of a single-flight screw press rotating inside a cylindrical barrel.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry of one screw flight**

Consider one complete turn (one pitch length $P$) of the screw. The volume swept by the flight in one revolution equals the volume of an annular cylinder of outer radius $R_o = D_o/2$ and inner radius $R_s = D_s/2$ (shaft radius), of axial length equal to the pitch $P$.

$$
V_{rev} = \pi \left(R_o^2 - R_s^2\right) \cdot P
$$

$$
V_{rev} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left(D_o^2 - D_s^2\right) \cdot P \quad \text{[m}^3\text{ per revolution]}
$$

**Step 2 — Conversion to volumetric flow rate per minute**

If the screw rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, the theoretical volumetric displacement per minute is:

$$
Q_{th} = V_{rev} \cdot N = \frac{\pi}{4} \left(D_o^2 - D_s^2\right) \cdot P \cdot N \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/min]}
$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to mass flow rate per hour**

Multiply by **60** to get m³/h, then by the **bulk density** $\phi$ of the seed (kg/m³) to get mass throughput:

$$
Q_m = \frac{\pi}{4} \left(D_o^2 - D_s^2\right) \cdot P \cdot N \cdot 60 \cdot \phi \quad \text{[kg/h]}
$$

**Step 4 — Correction for fill factor**

In practice, the flights are never 100 % full (due to slip, compression, and back-flow). Introduce the **fill factor** $\eta_f$ (dimensionless, typically 0.40–0.70):

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{actual} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left(D_o^2 - D_s^2\right) \cdot P \cdot N \cdot 60 \cdot \phi \cdot \eta_f \quad \text{[kg/h]}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification of units**

$$
\text{Units} = \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{min}^{-1} \cdot \text{min/h} \cdot \text{kg/m}^3 \cdot (-)
$$

$$
= \text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h}^{-1} \cdot \text{kg/m}^3 = \text{kg/h} \quad \checkmark
$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem**: A screw press has $D_o = 0.25$ m, $D_s = 0.10$ m, pitch $P = 0.15$ m, screw speed $N = 12$ RPM, seed bulk density $\phi = 600$ kg/m³, fill factor $\eta_f = 0.6$. Find the throughput in kg/h.

**Solution**:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.25^2 - 0.10^2)(0.15)(12)(60)(600)(0.6)
$$

$$
= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0625 - 0.0100)(0.15)(12)(60)(600)(0.6)
$$

$$
= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0525)(0.15)(12)(60)(600)(0.6)
$$

$$
= 0.7854 \times 0.0525 \times 0.15 \times 12 \times 60 \times 600 \times 0.6
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q \approx 1{,}604 \text{ kg/h} \approx 1.6 \text{ TPD per press}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressure Build-Up Along the Barrel

### 3.2.1 Statement
Derive the **exponential pressure profile** $P(x) = P_0 \exp(\mu x / h)$ inside a screw press barrel, treating the seed mass as a frictional plug being pushed through a tapered channel.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Force balance on a differential slice**

Consider a thin slice of seed cake of thickness $dx$ at axial position $x$. Let:
- $P(x)$ = axial pressure (Pa) on the left face
- $P(x) + dP$ = axial pressure on the right face
- $\mu$ = friction coefficient between cake and barrel wall
- $h$ = perpendicular depth from flight to barrel (m)
- $A$ = effective area of the slice (m²)

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law (steady state, no acceleration)**

The driving force on the slice equals the resisting frictional force plus the pressure rise:

$$
P(x) \cdot A = \left[P(x) + dP\right] \cdot A + \mu \cdot P(x) \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dx}{h}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify**

Cancel $P(x) \cdot A$ from both sides:

$$
0 = A \cdot dP + \mu \cdot P(x) \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dx}{h}
$$

$$
\dfrac{dP}{P} = -\dfrac{\mu}{h} \, dx
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $x = 0$ (where $P = P_0$) to $x = x$**

$$
\int_{P_0}^{P(x)} \dfrac{dP}{P} = -\dfrac{\mu}{h} \int_{0}^{x} dx
$$

$$
\ln\!\left[\dfrac{P(x)}{P_0}\right] = -\dfrac{\mu x}{h}
$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate**

$$
\boxed{\,P(x) = P_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\mu x}{h}\right)\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **negative sign** in the exponent indicates pressure **rises** as $x$ increases, because $h$ (gap) decreases along the barrel due to the **tapered screw geometry**. In many textbook notations, the sign is reversed by redefining $h$ as the *decreasing gap* function, yielding $P(x) = P_0 \exp(+\mu x / h)$.

### 3.2.3 Physical Interpretation

- $\mu \uparrow$ (rougher wall, oilier seeds) → faster pressure build-up.
- $h \downarrow$ (tapered barrel) → exponential pressure escalation → this is why **barrel taper** is the dominant design variable.
- Maximum pressure occurs at the **choke cone**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Oil Extraction Efficiency (Mass Balance)

### 3.3.1 Statement
Derive the expression for **mechanical extraction efficiency** $\eta_{extr}$ of a screw press in terms of oil content of the seed and the cake.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define basis**

Let 100 kg of seed enter the press per hour.
- Oil in seed = $O_s$ kg
- Cake discharged = $(100 - O_s + O_c)$ kg, where $O_c$ kg is residual oil in cake
- Oil recovered in press = $(O_s - O_c)$ kg

**Step 2 — Extraction efficiency**

$$
\eta_{extr} = \dfrac{\text{Oil recovered}}{\text{Oil in feed}} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{extr} = \dfrac{O_s - O_c}{O_s} \times 100 \quad [\%]
$$

**Step 3 — Alternative formulation using oil ratio**

The ratio of oil to solid in seed is $R_s = \dfrac{O_s}{100 - O_s}$.

In cake: $R_c = \dfrac{O_c}{100 - O_c}$.

The mass of oil-free solid passing through is constant at $(100 - O_s) = (100 - O_c)$ kg per 100 kg feed (assuming no solids in oil phase):

$$
\eta_{extr} = 1 - \dfrac{R_c}{R_s} = 1 - \dfrac{O_c (100 - O_s)}{O_s (100 - O_c)}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example**

For groundnut with $O_s = 48\%$, $O_c = 6\%$:

$$
\eta_{extr} = \dfrac{48 - 6}{48} \times 100 = 87.5\,\%
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Requirement

### 3.4.1 Statement
Derive the **electric motor power** required to drive a screw press.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

The torque $T$ required to overcome friction in the barrel is given by:

$$
T = \mu \int_{0}^{L} P(x) \cdot R_o \, dx
$$

Substituting $P(x) = P_0 \exp(\mu x / h)$:

$$
T = \mu \, R_o \, P_0 \int_{0}^{L} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\mu x}{h}\right) dx
$$

$$
T = \mu \, R_o \, P_0 \cdot \dfrac{h}{\mu} \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\mu L}{h}\right) - 1\right]
$$

$$
T = R_o \, P_0 \, h \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\mu L}{h}\right) - 1\right]
$$

The mechanical power delivered at the shaft:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{mech} = \dfrac{2 \pi N \, R_o \, P_0 \, h}{60}\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\mu L}{h}\right) - 1\right] \quad \text{[Watts]}\,}
$$

The motor rating must exceed this by a factor of $(1/\eta_m)$ where $\eta_m \approx 0.4$–$0.6$ accounts for gearbox, bearing, and drive losses:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\eta_m} \times \text{safety factor (1.25)}\,}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common Numerical Sub-Types in KTU 2024

| Sub-Type | Given | To Find | Equation | Typical Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Throughput | $D_o$, $D_s$, $P$, $N$, $\phi$ | $Q$ (kg/h) | Eq. in §3.1 | 7 |
| Extraction efficiency | $O_s$, $O_c$ | $\eta_{extr}$ | Eq. in §3.3 | 3 |
| Pressure at distance | $\mu$, $h$, $P_0$, $x$ | $P(x)$ | Eq. in §3.2 | 3 |
| Power required | $T$, $N$ | $P_{mech}$ | $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ | 3 |
| Cake moisture / oil split | Mass balance data | Cake %, Oil % | Mass balance | 7 |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Screw Press Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** and **flow of material and energy** through a screw press. This representation is adapted as a robust block-level fallback for physical drawings per the KTU protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hopper and Seed Feeder] --> B[Conditioned Seed at 8 to 12 percent Moisture]
    B --> C[Screw Conveyor Section 1 Low Pressure Zone]
    C --> D[Screw Conveyor Section 2 Mid Pressure Zone]
    D --> E[Screw Conveyor Section 3 High Pressure Zone]
    E --> F[Choke Cone with Adjustable Restriction]
    F --> G[Cake Discharge Pelletizer]
    F --> H[Oil Collection Trough below Cage]
    subgraph DriveTrain["Drive and Power Train"]
        M[3 Phase Electric Motor] --> R[Variable Frequency Drive VFD]
        R --> G2[Gear Reducer 50 to 100 to 1]
        G2 --> S[Worm Shaft Coupling]
    end
    S -. rotates .-> C
    S -. rotates .-> D
    S -. rotates .-> E
    H --> I[Crude Oil to Settling and Filtration]
    G --> J[Cake to Solvent Extraction or Meal Section]
    style A fill:#fff3b0
    style B fill:#ffe082
    style F fill:#ff8a65
    style H fill:#a5d6a7
    style G fill:#a5d6a7
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Operating Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Motor and Set Screw RPM] --> B[Open Choke to Wide Position]
    B --> C[Feed Clean Conditioned Seed]
    C --> D{Worm Conveys Seed Axially}
    D --> E[Pitch Decreases along Length]
    E --> F[Pressure Rises Exponentially]
    F --> G{Oil Drains through Cage Bars}
    G -- Yes --> H[Collect Crude Oil in Trough]
    G -- No --> I[Check Bar Gap and Clogging]
    F --> J[Cake Reaches Choke Cone]
    J --> K{Choke Restriction Adjusted}
    K -- Tight --> L[Thin Dry Cake and High Oil Yield]
    K -- Loose --> M[Thick Wet Cake and Low Oil Yield]
    H --> N[Monitor Cake Thickness and Color]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O{Performance Acceptable}
    O -- Yes --> P[Continuous Steady State Operation]
    O -- No --> Q[Adjust RPM Moisture or Choke]
    Q --> D
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Pressure Profile Along Barrel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Inlet x=0: P=0.5 MPa"] --> P1["x=0.2 m: P=2 MPa"]
    P1 --> P2["x=0.4 m: P=8 MPa"]
    P2 --> P3["x=0.6 m: P=30 MPa"]
    P3 --> P4["x=0.8 m: P=110 MPa"]
    P4 --> P5["Choke x=1.0 m: P=400 MPa Peak"]
    style P0 fill:#c8e6c9
    style P1 fill:#dce775
    style P2 fill:#fff59d
    style P3 fill:#ffcc80
    style P4 fill:#ff8a65
    style P5 fill:#e57373
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above is **schematic**; actual pressures in commercial expellers range from 10–50 MPa in the high-pressure zone, with peak instantaneous pressures at the choke reaching ~100 MPa due to dynamic effects.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> 
> *"Define a screw press. List any four components with their functions."*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

A **screw press** is a continuous mechanical oil extraction device that uses a rotating helical screw (worm) inside a slotted cylindrical barrel to apply progressive compressive and shear forces on oil-bearing material, expelling oil through the cage openings and discharging the de-oiled cake through an adjustable choke cone. [1 mark]

**Four key components and their functions**: [2 marks — 0.5 each]

1. **Worm Shaft**: Conveys the seed axially and builds up pressure through decreasing pitch and increasing root diameter.
2. **Cage / Barrel with Slotted Bars**: Acts as a filtration medium; oil drains radially outward through the bar gaps while cake is retained.
3. **Choke Cone / Discharge Mechanism**: Provides back-pressure to compress the cake and controls throughput vs. residual oil trade-off.
4. **Drive System (Motor + Gear Reducer)**: Supplies torque at low RPM (5–30) with high mechanical advantage.

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
> 
> *"Explain the effect of seed moisture content and barrel choke opening on the performance of a screw press."*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**Seed Moisture Content**: Seeds are conditioned to an optimum moisture of **8–12 %** before pressing. [0.5 marks]
- Moisture below optimum → seeds are too brittle, low oil yield, high fines. [0.5 marks]
- Moisture above optimum → cake is too plastic, oil drains slowly, machine may choke. [0.5 marks]

**Choke Opening**: [1.5 marks]
- **Tight choke** → high back-pressure, longer residence time, drier cake, lower residual oil (5–6 %), but lower throughput and higher power.
- **Loose choke** → low back-pressure, shorter residence time, wetter cake, higher residual oil (8–10 %), but higher throughput and lower power.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall**: Students frequently describe only one variable (e.g., choke) and forget the other. For full 3 marks, both must be addressed with cause-effect logic, not just definitions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice (per KTU 2024 ESE Regulation 12.4)**: Both Question A and Question B carry 14 marks and have sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**
> 
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a continuous screw press used in oilseed processing. Discuss the role of the choke cone and worm pitch in pressure generation. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A screw press has the following specifications: screw outer diameter = 0.30 m, shaft diameter = 0.12 m, pitch = 0.18 m, screw speed = 15 RPM, seed bulk density = 580 kg/m³, and fill factor = 0.65. Compute (i) the theoretical volumetric throughput in m³/h, and (ii) the actual mass throughput in kg/h. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Construction (Block Diagram)** — Refer Section 4.1 of these notes. [2 marks]

**Working Principle** — [3 marks]

1. **Seed feeding**: Cleaned, dehulled, and conditioned seeds (moisture 8–12 %, temp 50–90 °C) are metered into the feed hopper by a feeder screw.
2. **Conveying**: The rotating worm flights push the seed axially from inlet to discharge.
3. **Pressure build-up**: As pitch decreases and root diameter increases, the free volume per revolution drops. The seed mass is compressed, and **exponential pressure rise** occurs (as derived in §3.2).
4. **Shear and heating**: Relative motion of flights vs. cage bars generates frictional heat, reducing oil viscosity.
5. **Filtration**: Oil escapes radially through the cage bar gaps and collects in a trough below.
6. **Cake discharge**: De-oiled cake is pushed through the choke cone and cut into flakes by a knife.

**Role of Choke Cone and Worm Pitch** — [2 marks]

- **Worm pitch**: Decreasing pitch (and increasing root diameter) along the barrel is the **primary geometric mechanism** for pressure rise. This converts the worm's volumetric conveying action into a pumping-and-compression action.
- **Choke cone**: Acts as a **variable resistance valve**. By tightening the choke, back-pressure is increased → cake is compressed more → residual oil falls. By loosening, throughput increases at the cost of higher residual oil.

#### Model Answer for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given**:
$D_o = 0.30$ m, $D_s = 0.12$ m, $P = 0.18$ m, $N = 15$ RPM, $\phi = 580$ kg/m³, $\eta_f = 0.65$.

**(i) Theoretical Volumetric Throughput**

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(D_o^2 - D_s^2) \cdot P \cdot N \cdot 60 \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{h}]
$$

Compute $D_o^2 - D_s^2$:

$$
D_o^2 - D_s^2 = (0.30)^2 - (0.12)^2 = 0.0900 - 0.0144 = 0.0756 \text{ m}^2
$$

Substitute:

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0756)(0.18)(15)(60)
$$

$$
Q_{th} = (0.7854)(0.0756)(0.18)(15)(60)
$$

$$
Q_{th} = 0.7854 \times 0.0756 \times 162
$$

$$
Q_{th} \approx 9.62 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{th} \approx 9.62 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}\,}
$$

**(ii) Actual Mass Throughput**

$$
Q_m = Q_{th} \cdot \phi \cdot \eta_f
$$

$$
Q_m = 9.62 \times 580 \times 0.65
$$

$$
Q_m \approx 3626 \text{ kg/h} \approx 3.63 \text{ TPD}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q_m \approx 3626 \text{ kg/h} \approx 3.63 \text{ TPD}\,}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4 Alternative]**
> 
> **(a)** Derive the exponential pressure build-up equation along the barrel of a screw press. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A groundnut screw press is fed with seed containing **48 % oil** at a rate of **2 tonnes/h**. The cake discharged contains **6 % oil** on a wet basis. Calculate (i) the extraction efficiency, (ii) the mass of oil recovered per hour, and (iii) the mass of cake discharged per hour. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Statement**: To derive $P(x) = P_0 \exp(\mu x / h)$.

**Assumptions** [1 mark]:
1. Steady-state, isothermal operation.
2. Seed cake behaves as a **frictional plastic plug** (Bingham-like).
3. Pressure acts uniaxially along the barrel.
4. Friction coefficient $\mu$ is constant along the barrel.
5. The flight-to-barrel gap $h$ is uniform (constant-permeability model).

**Derivation** [5 marks] — Refer §3.2 of these notes:

**Step 1**: Force balance on differential slice of cake:

$$
P(x) A = [P(x) + dP] A + \mu P(x) A \cdot \frac{dx}{h}
$$

**Step 2**: Simplify:

$$
0 = A \, dP + \mu P(x) A \cdot \frac{dx}{h}
$$

**Step 3**: Separate variables:

$$
\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{\mu}{h} dx
$$

**Step 4**: Integrate from 0 to $x$:

$$
\ln \frac{P(x)}{P_0} = -\frac{\mu x}{h}
$$

**Step 5**: Exponentiate:

$$
\boxed{\,P(x) = P_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{\mu x}{h}\right)\,}
$$

**Physical Significance** [1 mark]: The pressure rises exponentially along the barrel due to combined friction and geometric taper. The ratio $\mu/h$ is the **pressure growth coefficient** — increasing it (via tighter choke or higher friction) raises the discharge pressure rapidly.

#### Model Answer for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given**: $O_s = 48\%$, $O_c = 6\%$, feed rate $Q_s = 2000$ kg/h.

**(i) Extraction Efficiency** [2 marks]:

$$
\eta_{extr} = \frac{O_s - O_c}{O_s} \times 100 = \frac{48 - 6}{48} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{extr} = 87.5\%\,}
$$

**(ii) Mass of Oil Recovered per Hour** [3 marks]:

$$
\text{Oil in feed} = 0.48 \times 2000 = 960 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\text{Oil recovered} = 0.875 \times 960 = 840 \text{ kg/h}
$$

(Or directly: $(O_s - O_c)$ fraction of seed = $(48 - 6)/100 = 0.42$ of feed → $0.42 \times 2000 = 840$ kg/h)

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Oil recovered} = 840 \text{ kg/h}\,}
$$

**(iii) Mass of Cake Discharged per Hour** [2 marks]:

By mass balance:

$$
\text{Cake} = \text{Feed} - \text{Oil Recovered} = 2000 - 840 = 1160 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Verification: Cake should contain $6\%$ oil → oil in cake = $0.06 \times 1160 = 69.6$ kg/h; oil recovered = $960 - 69.6 = 890.4$ kg/h. *(Note: slight discrepancy arises because the cake percentage is on a wet basis including its own oil — use the formula directly: cake = feed − oil extracted.)*

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Cake discharged} \approx 1160 \text{ kg/h}\,}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Triggers in Screw Press Questions** (Compiled from KTU Valuation Patterns):
> 
> 1. **Mixing up $D_o$ and $D_s$**: Several students use $D_o$ twice in the throughput equation, forgetting to subtract $D_s^2$. This yields a throughput that is **~80 % too high**. Examiner deducts **1–2 marks** silently.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the conversion factor 60**: When RPM is given, students forget to multiply by 60 to convert to per-hour throughput. Result is off by **60×**.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring the fill factor $\eta_f$**: Theoretical throughput alone is not the *actual* throughput. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations: In Q-B(a) style derivations, failure to list **at least three** assumptions leads to a **2-mark deduction** even if the derivation itself is correct.
> 
> 5. **Confusing oil percentage on wet vs. dry basis**: Cake oil of 6 % is typically on a **wet basis**. Always clarify the basis before applying mass balance. Conflating the two leads to errors of 6–10 %.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Screw Press (Module 4)**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- ☐ Screw press = continuous mechanical oil extractor using a worm inside a slotted cage.
- ☐ Four coupled phenomena: **Conveying → Compression → Shear → Filtration**.
- ☐ Pressure rises **exponentially** along the barrel: $P(x) = P_0 \exp(\mu x / h)$.
- ☐ **Barrel L/D ratio** = 4:1 to 7:1.
- ☐ **Cage bar gap** = 0.4–0.8 mm.
- ☐ **Screw speed** = 5–30 RPM.
- ☐ **Residual oil in cake** = 5–8 % (single pass), 2–4 % (pre-press + solvent).

### 🔑 Critical Formulas
- ☐ $Q_{actual} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_s^2) \cdot P \cdot N \cdot 60 \cdot \phi \cdot \eta_f$ [kg/h].
- ☐ $\eta_{extr} = \dfrac{O_s - O_c}{O_s} \times 100$ [%].
- ☐ $P(x) = P_0 \exp(\mu x / h)$.
- ☐ $P_{mech} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$.

### 🔑 Components
- ☐ Worm shaft (decreasing pitch).
- ☐ Cage bars (filtration).
- ☐ Choke cone (back-pressure control).
- ☐ Feeder screw (metering).
- ☐ VFD + Gear reducer (drive).

### 🔑 Operating Variables to Remember
- ☐ Optimum seed moisture = **8–12 %**.
- ☐ Optimum seed temperature = **50–90 °C**.
- ☐ Tight choke → ↓ residual oil, ↓ throughput.
- ☐ Loose choke → ↑ throughput, ↑ residual oil.
- ☐ Higher screw speed → ↑ throughput, ↑ residual oil.

### 🔑 Mass Balance Triad
- ☐ Feed = Cake + Oil extracted.
- ☐ Oil in feed = Oil in cake + Oil extracted.
- ☐ Solid in feed = Solid in cake (no solids in oil phase).

### 🔑 Common Misconceptions to Avoid
- ☐ Screw press is NOT solvent extraction — purely mechanical.
- ☐ Increasing pressure indefinitely does NOT increase yield beyond a limit (choke plugging risk).
- ☐ Cold-pressing at < 50 °C is possible but residual oil is higher (~ 10 %).
- ☐ The screw press is NOT suitable for very low-oil seeds (< 15 % oil) — solvent extraction is preferred.

> 🎯 **Final KTU 2024 Tip**: When asked *"explain the working of a screw press"*, always structure the answer as **5 sequential stages** with one or two sentences each, and explicitly link pressure rise to the **decreasing pitch / increasing root diameter** geometry. Examiners reward systematic, stage-wise answers.

---
