topics.title: Hydraulic Press
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT403 - PROCESSING OF CEREALS, PULSES AND OILSEEDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic Press

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hydraulic Press** is a mechanical press that employs a hydraulic cylinder, driven by an incompressible fluid (typically hydraulic oil) under high pressure, to generate a controlled compressive force for the extraction of oil from oil-bearing materials or for the densification of bulk solids. It operates fundamentally on **Pascal's Law** of fluid transmission, wherein pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted undiminished in every direction throughout the fluid mass.

In the context of oilseed processing (the scope of PCFTT403), the hydraulic press is one of the oldest and most reliable batch-type oil extraction devices, used extensively for seeds such as **copra (dried coconut kernel), groundnut, sesame (til), mustard, sunflower, and cotton seed**. Modern industrial presses operate in the range of **100 to 700 bar (10 to 70 MPa)** and can deliver compressive loads from **50 tonnes to 2,500 tonnes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The hydraulic press is classified as a *Batch-Type Mechanical Oil Expression Equipment* under Module 4 (Oil Extraction Machinery). Students are expected to know its principle, construction, working, types, performance parameters (oil yield, pressing efficiency, capacity), and comparative advantage over screw expellers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are trying to crush a fully ripe coconut to extract its oil using only your bare hands. The force of your hand is small, and the effort required is enormous. Now imagine using a long lever to amplify your hand's force — that is the principle of a *mechanical press*.

A **hydraulic press does the same job as the lever, but using a fluid instead of a rigid arm.**

Think of a simple medical syringe. When you push the small plunger down, water comes out of the narrow nozzle at high velocity. If you reverse the situation — apply force on a small piston, and the fluid pushes on a *much larger* piston at the other end — the small force becomes a *massive* force on the large piston. **This multiplication of force is the heart of every hydraulic press.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Everyday Analogy:**
> The braking system of a car is a hydraulic press in miniature. Your foot applies a small force on the master cylinder (small piston), and the brake caliper (large piston) squeezes the brake pads onto the disc with enormous force, stopping a 1.5-tonne car. A hydraulic oil press does the same thing — but instead of stopping a car, it squeezes oil out of cooked oilseeds.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Operating Pressure | **100 – 700 bar** (10 – 70 MPa) |
| Compressive Load Capacity | **50 – 2,500 tonnes** |
| Hydraulic Fluid | Mineral oil (ISO VG 32–68) |
| Pump Type | Radial piston / gear pump |
| Oil Yield (oilseeds) | **30 – 65 %** depending on seed |
| Pressing Cycle Time | **15 – 45 minutes per batch** |
| Cake Residual Oil | **5 – 12 %** (wet basis) |
| Mechanical Efficiency | **80 – 92 %** |

> [!TIP]
> Always remember these three numbers for viva and short-answer questions: **Pascal's Law**, **~ 70 MPa operating pressure**, and **5–12% residual oil in cake**. These are board-exam favourites.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Principle — Pascal's Law

**Statement (Blaise Pascal, 1653):**
*"The pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the containing vessel."*

Mathematically, if pressure $P$ is applied at one point in the fluid, then every point in the fluid experiences the **same pressure** $P$.

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2} = \frac{F_3}{A_3} = \dots = \text{constant}$$

where:
- $F_1$ = force applied on the small (master) piston, N
- $A_1$ = cross-sectional area of the small piston, m²
- $F_2$ = resultant force exerted on the large (ram) piston, N
- $A_2$ = cross-sectional area of the large piston (ram), m²

## 2.2 The Force-Multiplication Logic (Step-by-Step)

A hydraulic press consists of two cylinders interconnected by a pipe and filled with hydraulic oil:

1. A **small-diameter plunger** is driven by a low-pressure pump.
2. The fluid under pressure travels through the pipe to a **large-diameter ram (pressing piston)**.
3. Because pressure is equal at both pistons, the larger piston experiences a much larger force.
4. This large force is transmitted through a press cage/box containing the oilseed meal.
5. Oil is squeezed out through drainage slots, while the de-oiled cake remains.

The **mechanical advantage (MA)** of the press is:

$$\text{MA} = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$$

where $D_1$ and $D_2$ are the diameters of the small and large pistons.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure = Force ÷ Area (Pascal's Law) | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | Force multiplication by hydraulic ram | N |
| 3 | $\text{MA} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$ | Mechanical advantage (ratio of diameters) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_m = \dfrac{W_{\text{out}}}{W_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Mechanical efficiency of the press | % |
| 5 | $Y_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{seed}}} \times 100$ | Oil yield (per cent by mass) | % |
| 6 | $O_{\text{res}} = 100 - Y_{\text{oil}} \cdot k$ | Residual oil in cake (mass balance) | % |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{m_{\text{seed}}}{t_{\text{cycle}}}$ | Throughput capacity per cycle | kg/h |
| 8 | $E_{\text{stroke}} = P \cdot A_2 \cdot L$ | Energy delivered per ram stroke | J |
| 9 | $\dot{W} = \dfrac{P \cdot Q_v}{600}$ | Hydraulic power (with $Q_v$ in L/min) | kW |
| 10 | $C_{\text{specific}} = \dfrac{E}{m_{\text{oil}}}$ | Specific energy consumption | kWh/kg oil |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Pitfall Alert:** In Equation 3, the MA is the **square** of the diameter ratio, not the ratio itself. Students frequently write $D_2/D_1$ and lose marks. Board examiners specifically check this squaring step.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hydraulic press is not just a food-engineering tool — it is foundational to almost every heavy industry:

- **Oilseed processing:** Extraction of edible oil from copra, groundnut, mustard, etc.
- **Forging and forming:** Shaping of steel components (50,000-tonne forging presses).
- **Compression moulding:** Manufacturing of composites, laminates, plastic parts.
- **Baling:** Compacting of agricultural residue, hay, scrap metal.
- **Automotive braking:** Hydraulic brake systems.
- **Earth-moving equipment:** Hydraulic excavators and lifts.

In PCFTT403, the focus is on the **oilseed pressing application**, where the press is part of a larger process flow: *Cleaning → Dehulling → Cooking/Conditioning → Pressing → Oil Filtration → Cake Handling*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force Multiplication from Pascal's Law

We begin with the fundamental statement of Pascal's Law applied to the two connected pistons of the hydraulic press.

**Step 1:** Pressure on the small (master) plunger.

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$

This is the pressure exerted by the input force on the small piston of area $A_1$.

**Step 2:** Pressure on the large (ram) piston.

$$P = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

Because the fluid is incompressible and the system is sealed, the same pressure acts on the ram piston of area $A_2$.

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions for $P$ (Pascal's Law).

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to solve for the output force $F_2$.

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the geometric relation $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ for circular pistons.

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{\pi D_2^2 / 4}{\pi D_1^2 / 4}$$

**Step 6:** Cancel common terms $\pi$ and $4$.

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,F_2 = F_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2\,}$$

**Interpretation:** A 10 kN input force on a piston of 50 mm diameter produces 1000 kN (1 MN) on a ram of 500 mm diameter, since $(500/50)^2 = 100$. This is the **100-fold amplification** that makes hydraulic presses indispensable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mechanical Efficiency of the Hydraulic Press

The mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$ is the ratio of useful oil-expression work to the hydraulic input work.

**Step 1:** Define useful output work (pressing work done on the oilseed cake).

$$W_{\text{out}} = F_2 \cdot L$$

where $L$ is the ram stroke length over which pressing actually occurs (in metres).

**Step 2:** Define input work (work done by the pump on the small plunger).

$$W_{\text{in}} = F_1 \cdot L_1$$

where $L_1$ is the displacement of the small plunger. From volume conservation of the incompressible fluid:

$$A_1 \cdot L_1 = A_2 \cdot L \quad\Longrightarrow\quad L_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1}\cdot L$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $L_1$ back into $W_{\text{in}}$.

$$W_{\text{in}} = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}\cdot L$$

**Step 4:** Form the efficiency ratio.

$$\eta_m = \frac{W_{\text{out}}}{W_{\text{in}}} = \frac{F_2 \cdot L}{F_1 \cdot (A_2/A_1)\cdot L}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify.

$$\eta_m = \frac{F_2}{F_1} \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $F_2/F_1 = A_2/A_1$ from the ideal force relation.

$$\eta_m = \frac{A_2}{A_1}\cdot\frac{A_1}{A_2}\cdot \eta_{\text{loss factor}}$$

**Final Result (including friction and leak losses):**

$$\boxed{\,\eta_m = \frac{F_2^{\text{actual}}}{F_1 \cdot (A_2/A_1)} \times 100\,\%\,}$$

A typical value for industrial oilseed hydraulic presses is **80–92 %**, with the loss attributed to friction in seals, fluid leakage past piston rings, and elastic deformation of the press frame.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Oil Yield & Residual Oil Mass Balance

In an oilseed pressing operation, the seed mass is partitioned into expressed oil and de-oiled cake.

**Step 1:** Total mass balance.

$$m_{\text{seed}} = m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{cake}}$$

**Step 2:** Define oil yield $Y$ as the fraction of seed mass recovered as oil.

$$Y = \frac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{seed}}}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to obtain the mass of oil.

$$m_{\text{oil}} = Y \cdot m_{\text{seed}}$$

**Step 4:** The mass of the cake follows from Step 1.

$$m_{\text{cake}} = m_{\text{seed}} - Y \cdot m_{\text{seed}} = m_{\text{seed}}(1 - Y)$$

**Step 5:** Residual oil in the cake (as a fraction of seed mass) is:

$$O_{\text{res}} = Y_{\text{theoretical}} - Y_{\text{actual}}$$

**Step 6:** As a fraction of the cake mass:

$$O_{\text{cake}} = \frac{m_{\text{seed}}\cdot Y_{\text{theoretical}} - m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{cake}}} \times 100$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,O_{\text{cake}}\,[\%] = \frac{Y_{\text{theory}} - Y}{1 - Y}\times 100\,}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU-type problem):**
Given: $m_{\text{seed}} = 1000$ kg, $m_{\text{oil}} = 380$ kg, theoretical oil content = 45 %.

$$Y = \frac{380}{1000} = 0.38 = 38\,\%$$

$$O_{\text{cake}} = \frac{0.45 - 0.38}{1 - 0.38}\times 100 = \frac{0.07}{0.62}\times 100 = 11.29\,\%$$

This matches the typical **5–12 % residual oil** in cake reported for industrial hydraulic pressing.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Between Press Types

| Parameter | Box Press | Plate Press | Cage Press |
|---|---|---|---|
| Batch Size | 10–25 kg | 5–15 kg | 15–40 kg |
| Pressure Range | 100–250 bar | 80–180 bar | 150–400 bar |
| Cake Shape | Rectangular slab | Round disc | Cylindrical column |
| Oil Yield | 35–50 % | 30–45 % | 40–55 % |
| Labour Intensity | High | Medium | Low |
| Best For | Copra, groundnut | Sesame, mustard | Sunflower, cotton seed |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Hydraulic Oil Press

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a complete hydraulic oilseed pressing system, showing the flow of material, energy, and information.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Oilseed Intake] --> B[Cleaning and Dehulling Unit]
    B --> C[Cracking and Flaking Mill]
    C --> D[Cooking Conditioner 80 to 110 C]
    D --> E[Hydraulic Press Feed Hopper]
    E --> F1[Box Type Press]
    E --> F2[Plate Type Press]
    E --> F3[Cage Type Press]
    F1 --> G1[Oil Collection Tray]
    F2 --> G2[Oil Collection Tray]
    F3 --> G3[Oil Collection Tray]
    G1 --> H[Filter Press]
    G2 --> H[Filter Press]
    G3 --> H[Filter Press]
    H --> I[Crude Oil Storage Tank]
    F1 --> J1[De oiled Cake Discharge]
    F2 --> J2[De oiled Cake Discharge]
    F3 --> J3[De oiled Cake Discharge]
    K[Hydraulic Power Pack] --> F1
    K --> F2
    K --> F3
    K --> L[Electric Motor 5 to 25 kW]
    L --> K
    I --> M[Refining and Packaging]
    J1 --> N[Cake Grinder and Packing]
    J2 --> N
    J3 --> N
```

## 4.2 Internal Schematic — Force Multiplication Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MASTERSIDE [Master Cylinder Small Piston]
        direction TB
        P1[Input Force F1] --> PI1[Piston D1]
        PI1 --> FL1[Hydraulic Oil at Pressure P]
    end
    subgraph RAMSIDE [Ram Cylinder Large Piston]
        direction TB
        FL1 --> PI2[Piston D2]
        PI2 --> P2[Output Force F2]
        P2 --> CAKE[Oilseed Cake]
        CAKE --> OIL[Expressed Oil]
    end
    FL1 -.transmits P undiminished.-> PI2
    CAKE --> CAKE2[De oiled Cake]
```

## 4.3 Detailed Sub-System Flow (Hydraulic Circuit)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Three Phase Electric Motor] --> P1[Radial Piston Pump]
    P1 --> R1[Pressure Relief Valve]
    P1 --> DCV[Directional Control Valve 4 by 3]
    DCV --> RAM[Double Acting Hydraulic Ram]
    RAM --> CB[Counterbalance Valve]
    CB --> TANK[Reservoir Tank]
    R1 --> TANK
    TANK --> P1
    RAM --> CAKE3[Pressing Cage and Cake]
    CAKE3 --> OIL2[Oil Drainage Slots]
```

## 4.4 Description of Key Sub-Assemblies

| Sub-Assembly | Function |
|---|---|
| **Electric Motor** | Prime mover; typically 5–25 kW, 3-phase induction |
| **Radial Piston Pump** | Generates high-pressure oil flow (up to 700 bar) |
| **Pressure Relief Valve** | Safety device; prevents over-pressurisation |
| **Directional Control Valve (DCV)** | Routes oil to either side of the double-acting ram |
| **Counterbalance Valve** | Holds the ram in position under load; prevents descent |
| **Reservoir Tank** | Stores hydraulic oil; allows cooling and de-aeration |
| **Hydraulic Ram** | The large piston that delivers the pressing force |
| **Pressing Cage** | Contains the cooked oilseed meal during pressing |
| **Oil Drainage Slots** | Perforations through which expressed oil escapes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading a Mermaid diagram in exam:** Board examiners often award partial marks for a *correctly labelled block diagram* even if the numerical answer is incomplete. Drawing the functional flow (Feed → Press → Oil → Cake) is a high-yield scoring strategy.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State and explain Pascal's Law as applicable to a hydraulic press used for oilseed processing.**

**Model Answer:**

Pascal's Law (Blaise Pascal, 1653) states that *"the pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the containing vessel."*

In a hydraulic oil press, this principle enables a small input force $F_1$ applied to a small-diameter plunger (area $A_1$) to produce a much larger output force $F_2$ on the large-diameter ram (area $A_2$):

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

$$\Rightarrow F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} = F_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$$

This force amplification (mechanical advantage) is what allows the hydraulic press to deliver compressive loads of **50–2,500 tonnes** for squeezing oil out of cooked oilseeds such as copra, groundnut, and mustard.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List any three types of hydraulic presses used in oilseed processing and state one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Type of Hydraulic Press | Specific Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Box-type hydraulic press** | Pressing of copra (dried coconut kernel) and groundnut in rectangular cake form |
| 2 | **Plate-type hydraulic press** | Small-batch pressing of sesame (til) and mustard seed; suitable for cottage industry |
| 3 | **Cage-type hydraulic press** | Continuous high-pressure pressing of sunflower and cotton seed; yields cylindrical cake |

Each type differs in the geometry of the pressing chamber, the achievable pressure range, and the throughput capacity, but all operate on the same Pascal's-Law principle.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (Module-Level)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Both options below are designed to be *complete, independent, and answerable in 9–12 minutes* — the expected exam-writing window.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a **box-type hydraulic press** used for oil extraction. Label all major components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A box-type hydraulic press has a master plunger diameter of **50 mm** and a ram diameter of **400 mm**. The input force on the plunger is **5 kN**. Calculate: (i) the pressure developed in the hydraulic fluid, (ii) the output force exerted by the ram, and (iii) the mechanical advantage. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction:**
The box-type hydraulic press consists of:

1. **Hydraulic power pack** — comprising an electric motor (5–25 kW), a radial piston pump, a pressure relief valve, a directional control valve, and a reservoir tank containing hydraulic oil (ISO VG 46).
2. **Master cylinder** — a small-diameter plunger (typically 25–75 mm) that receives the input force.
3. **Connecting pipe** — a high-pressure hose routing oil from the master cylinder to the ram.
4. **Ram (pressing cylinder)** — a large-diameter piston (200–800 mm) carrying the press plate.
5. **Press box** — a rectangular steel cage with perforated drainage slots, containing the cooked oilseed meal wrapped in cloth.
6. **End plate (reaction plate)** — the stationary surface against which the cake is pressed.
7. **Collection tray** — placed beneath the press box to collect expressed oil.

**Working:**
Cooked and conditioned oilseed meal (e.g., copra at 5–7 % moisture, 100–110 °C) is packed into the press box wrapped in filter cloth. The motor drives the pump, which pressurises the hydraulic oil to **150–250 bar**. This oil is routed through the DCV to the master cylinder. By Pascal's Law, the pressure acts on the ram, which pushes the cake against the reaction plate. Oil is squeezed out through the perforated slots, while the de-oiled cake (5–12 % residual oil) is discharged. The cycle takes 15–45 minutes per batch.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
$D_1 = 50$ mm = 0.050 m, $D_2 = 400$ mm = 0.400 m, $F_1 = 5$ kN = 5000 N

**Step (i): Pressure in the hydraulic fluid.**

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_1}{\pi D_1^2/4}$$

$$P = \frac{5000}{\pi (0.050)^2/4} = \frac{5000}{1.963\times 10^{-3}}$$

$$P = 2.547 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa} = 25.47\,\text{bar}$$

**Step (ii): Output force on the ram.**

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 = 5000 \cdot \left(\frac{400}{50}\right)^2$$

$$F_2 = 5000 \cdot 64 = 320{,}000\,\text{N} = 320\,\text{kN}$$

**Step (iii): Mechanical advantage.**

$$\text{MA} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 = 64$$

Thus a modest 5 kN input force is amplified to **320 kN**, equivalent to a pressing load of about **32.6 tonnes**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between input force, output force, and piston diameters in a hydraulic press, starting from **Pascal's Law**. State any two assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic press processes **1000 kg/day** of copra with an **oil yield of 62 %**. The copra contains **65 % oil (theoretical)** on a dry basis. Calculate: (i) the mass of crude oil obtained per day, (ii) the mass of de-oiled cake per day, and (iii) the residual oil content of the cake. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Assumptions:**
1. The hydraulic fluid is **incompressible** (Betz's rule of constant density).
2. There are **no leakage** past piston seals and no friction losses (ideal conditions).

**Derivation:**

Pressure at the small plunger:

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$

Pressure at the large ram (Pascal's Law — equal pressure everywhere):

$$P = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

Solving for $F_2$:

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

Substituting $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$:

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{\pi D_2^2/4}{\pi D_1^2/4} = F_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$$

This is the **force-multiplication equation** of the hydraulic press.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $m_{\text{seed}} = 1000$ kg/day, $Y_{\text{actual}} = 62\,\%$, $Y_{\text{theory}} = 65\,\%$

**Step (i): Mass of crude oil obtained.**

$$m_{\text{oil}} = Y_{\text{actual}} \cdot m_{\text{seed}} = 0.62 \cdot 1000 = 620\,\text{kg/day}$$

**Step (ii): Mass of de-oiled cake.**

By mass balance: $m_{\text{cake}} = m_{\text{seed}} - m_{\text{oil}}$

$$m_{\text{cake}} = 1000 - 620 = 380\,\text{kg/day}$$

**Step (iii): Residual oil in the cake (as % of cake mass).**

Mass of oil left in cake = $m_{\text{seed}} \cdot Y_{\text{theory}} - m_{\text{oil}}$

$$= 1000 \cdot 0.65 - 620 = 650 - 620 = 30\,\text{kg}$$

$$O_{\text{cake}}\,[\%] = \frac{30}{380} \times 100 = 7.89\,\%$$

This residual oil (~7.9 %) is well within the typical industrial range of 5–12 % and can be recovered by solvent extraction in a downstream refinery.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones (Examiner's Eye-View):**
>
> 1. **Squaring the diameter ratio:** Students write $\text{MA} = D_2/D_1$ instead of $(D_2/D_1)^2$. This loses 1 full mark. **Remember: MA is the SQUARE of the diameter ratio.**
>
> 2. **Unit conversion trap:** Board questions frequently give diameters in mm and expect force in kN. Skipping the conversion ($D^2$ in mm² vs m² changes the answer by $10^6$). Always convert to SI *before* substitution.
>
> 3. **Confusing oil yield with residual oil:** Oil yield is on a *seed-mass basis*; residual oil is on a *cake-mass basis*. These two values are NOT directly comparable.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the $\pi/4$ factor:** Many students approximate $A = D^2$. This introduces a 21.5 % error ($4/\pi$ = 1.273) and is heavily penalised.
>
> 5. **Skipping the schematic:** In 7-mark construction questions, a *labelled diagram* is worth at least 2 marks. A text-only answer without a figure typically caps at 5/7.
>
> 6. **Writing "oil extracted" without stating "crude oil" and acknowledging that it contains moisture, fines, and gums:** Examiners reward vocabulary precision.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section before entering the exam hall. It contains everything you must remember about Hydraulic Presses.**

- [ ] **Principle:** Pascal's Law — pressure in a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions.
- [ ] **Core equation:** $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (D_2/D_1)^2$
- [ ] **Mechanical advantage:** The square of the *diameter ratio*, not the ratio itself.
- [ ] **Operating pressure:** 100–700 bar (10–70 MPa) for industrial oilseed presses.
- [ ] **Typical loads:** 50–2,500 tonnes compressive force.
- [ ] **Three common types:** Box press, Plate press, Cage press.
- [ ] **Best applications:** Copra & groundnut → Box press; Sesame & mustard → Plate press; Sunflower & cotton seed → Cage press.
- [ ] **Oil yield range:** 30–65 % depending on seed type and pre-treatment.
- [ ] **Residual oil in cake:** 5–12 % (typical hydraulic-press benchmark).
- [ ] **Cycle time:** 15–45 minutes per batch (this is why hydraulic presses are *batch* operations).
- [ ] **Mechanical efficiency:** 80–92 % in industrial presses.
- [ ] **Mass balance identities:**
  - $m_{\text{seed}} = m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{cake}}$
  - $Y = m_{\text{oil}} / m_{\text{seed}}$
  - $O_{\text{cake}}\,[\%] = \dfrac{Y_{\text{theory}} - Y_{\text{actual}}}{1 - Y_{\text{actual}}}\times 100$
- [ ] **Key components:** Electric motor, radial piston pump, pressure relief valve, DCV, master plunger, hydraulic line, ram, press cage/box, reaction plate, drainage slots, oil collection tray, cake discharge.
- [ ] **Hydraulic fluid:** Mineral oil, ISO VG 32–68.
- [ ] **Comparison with screw expeller:** Hydraulic press → batch, higher cake residual oil (5–12 %), gentler (no heat from friction), ideal for high-oil-content seeds. Screw expeller → continuous, lower residual oil (3–7 %), generates heat, ideal for low-oil-content seeds.
- [ ] **Energy equation per stroke:** $E_{\text{stroke}} = P \cdot A_2 \cdot L$
- [ ] **Hydraulic power:** $\dot{W} = P \cdot Q_v / 600$ (with $Q_v$ in L/min → kW).
- [ ] **Exam mantra:** *"Small piston, big piston, Pascal's Law, force-squared."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solvent Extraction Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT403 - PROCESSING OF CEREALS, PULSES AND OILSEEDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solvent Extraction Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solvent Extraction** is a mass-transfer driven, diffusion-based separation process in which a volatile organic solvent (commonly food-grade **hexane**, petroleum ether, or isopropanol) is used to selectively dissolve and recover the residual oil (typically **0.5% – 1.5% w/w** remaining oil) entrapped within the porous cellular matrix of prepared oilseed flakes or collets, after the bulk oil has been removed by mechanical pressing. The process exploits the differential solubility of triglycerides in a non-polar organic solvent versus their insolubility in water, and is governed by Fick's laws of diffusion, the Nernst–Brunner mass-transfer model, and counter-current mass-transfer engineering principles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 4):** Students are expected to demonstrate working knowledge of the **principles of solvent extraction**, **types of extractors** (batch, semi-continuous, continuous), **process parameters** (temperature, particle size, moisture), and the **downstream unit operations** (miscella distillation, meal desolventizing, solvent recovery). Numerical problems on **material balance and extraction efficiency** carry high weightage.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Coffee Cup" Model

Imagine you have a cup of ground coffee grounds (this is your *flaked oilseed* — solid porous material with oil trapped inside tiny pores). You pour hot water over the grounds (this is your *solvent*). Slowly, the flavourful compounds *dissolve out* of the coffee particles and the water picks them up. The water now contains dissolved coffee — this is called the **miscella** (solvent + extracted oil). The wet, depleted coffee grounds left behind are analogous to the **de-oiled meal (solvents-laden marc)**. By evaporating the water, you recover the coffee extract.

In oil extraction, instead of water, we use **hexane** because oil dissolves extremely well in hexane, but **hexane does NOT dissolve proteins or carbohydrates**, leaving the meal nutritionally intact. Once extraction is complete, we evaporate the hexane from both the miscella and the meal, recovering the **crude oil** and the **defatted meal** — and the hexane is recycled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hexane and not water?** Triglycerides are *non-polar*. Hexane is also non-polar ("like dissolves like"). Water is polar and cannot pull oil out of intact seed cells. Hence, non-polar solvents are mandatory.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Highlighted Constants)

| Parameter | Standard Industrial Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Temperature** | **55 °C – 60 °C** | Near hexane's boiling point (68.7 °C) for optimal diffusion without excessive vaporization losses |
| **Residual Oil in Meal** | **< 1.0 % (w/w)** | KTU benchmark for "well-extracted" meal |
| **Solvent-to-Flakes Ratio** | **1.0 : 1 to 1.5 : 1 (w/w)** | Mass of solvent per mass of flakes fed |
| **Extraction Cycle Time** | **30 – 90 minutes** (batch); **30 min residence** (continuous) | Time for miscella concentration to reach equilibrium |
| **Moisture of Flakes** | **8% – 10%** | Critical for cell-wall rupture and proper percolation |
| **Flake Thickness** | **0.20 mm – 0.30 mm** | Thinner flakes = shorter diffusion path, but excessive fines cause percolation problems |
| **Hexane Boiling Point** | **68.7 °C** | Governs distillation and recovery energy |
| **Hexane Latent Heat of Vaporization** | **~ 365 kJ/kg** | Determines recovery steam consumption |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner often sets a "single-big-number" question requiring recall of these standard process parameters. Memorize the **residual oil < 1%** and **55-60 °C** values for guaranteed marks.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mass-Transfer Framework — "Why" Solvent Extraction Works

Solvent extraction of oil is fundamentally a **solid–liquid leaching** operation. The driving force is the **concentration gradient** of oil between the interior of the solid matrix and the bulk solvent phase. The mechanism can be broken down into **five sequential physical steps**:

1. **Solvent Penetration (Wetting)**: Hexane enters the pore space of the flaked seed by capillary action. The moisture content of the flakes (8–10%) is critical — too dry and pores are closed, too wet and the solvent cannot enter because water is preferentially absorbed in the polar regions of the cell wall.

2. **Solubilization**: Hexane, being non-polar, dissolves the triglyceride oil molecules. The oil–hexane solution formed is called the **miscella** (typically 18% – 30% oil-in-solvent by weight in the final full miscella).

3. **Internal Diffusion (Molecular Diffusion)**: Oil molecules move from the deep interior of the flake to the particle surface. This is governed by **Fick's First Law** of diffusion.

4. **External Mass Transfer (Convective Diffusion)**: At the particle–bulk interface, oil is carried away by the moving miscella film.

5. **Bulk Transport of Miscella**: The bulk flow of solvent carries the dissolved oil out of the extractor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Steps 3 and 4 are the rate-limiting steps. Step 3 (internal diffusion) is why **flake thickness** is so critical — diffusion time scales with the *square* of flake thickness. Halving the flake thickness reduces diffusion time to one-quarter. This is the **single most important design lever** in solvent extraction.

## 2.2 Fick's First Law of Diffusion — The Governing Equation

The diffusive flux of oil from inside the flake to the surface is:

$$J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$

Where:
- $J$ = mass flux of oil $\left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}\right]$
- $D$ = effective diffusion coefficient of oil in the solid matrix $\left[\frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}\right]$
- $\frac{dC}{dx}$ = concentration gradient of oil along the diffusion path $\left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{m}}\right]$

The negative sign indicates oil flows from **high concentration (interior) → low concentration (surface)**.

## 2.3 The Noyes–Whitney (Nernst–Brunner) Equation — Dissolution Kinetics

When the oil at the particle surface is in dynamic equilibrium with the bulk miscella, the rate of oil transfer into the bulk is:

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = \frac{D \cdot A}{h} \left( C_s - C_b \right) = k_L \cdot A \cdot \left( C_s - C_b \right)$$

Where:
- $\frac{dM}{dt}$ = rate of oil mass transfer $\left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}}\right]$
- $A$ = interfacial area of solid particles $\left[\text{m}^2\right]$
- $h$ = effective thickness of the liquid (boundary) film $\left[\text{m}\right]$
- $C_s$ = oil concentration at the particle surface (saturation) $\left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}\right]$
- $C_b$ = oil concentration in the bulk miscella $\left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}\right]$
- $k_L = \frac{D}{h}$ = liquid-side mass transfer coefficient $\left[\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right]$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Killer" Exam Insight:** As extraction progresses, $C_b$ approaches $C_s$, and the driving force $(C_s - C_b) \rightarrow 0$. This is why the **last traces of oil are disproportionately difficult** to remove. Continuous extractors use **counter-current flow** to maintain a *high* $(C_s - C_b)$ driving force at every stage.

## 2.4 Classification of Solvent Extraction Methods

| Category | Equipment Examples | Throughput | Application | Residual Oil |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch** | Soxhlet, Pachuca tank | Very low | Lab / R&D | 0.5 – 1.0% |
| **Semi-Continuous** | Hildebrandt (immersion basket) | Medium | Small / specialty plants | 0.8 – 1.2% |
| **Continuous — Percolation** | Bollman, Rotocel, Loop (EMI), Carrousel | Very high | Commercial oil mills | 0.5 – 0.8% |
| **Continuous — Immersion** | Hildebrandt U-type, Bonotto | High | Rice bran, cottonseed | 1.0 – 2.0% |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "Compare Bollman and Rotocel extractors." The Bollman is a *bucket elevator* design with a long extraction chamber and a short draining section. The Rotocel is a *rotating compartmentalized horizontal drum* — it is the workhorse of modern soybean processing because of its low solvent carry-over, gentle handling, and large capacity (up to **8000 TPD** of soybean).

## 2.5 Process Variables Affecting Extraction Efficiency

1. **Particle (Flake) Size & Thickness** — thinner = faster, but excessive fines cause percolation failure.
2. **Moisture Content of Flakes** — optimum **8% – 10%**; too high → blocked pores; too low → ruptured cells without preserving capillary channels.
3. **Temperature** — higher → lower miscella viscosity, higher $D$ (typically doubles per 10 °C rise), but capped at ~60 °C to limit hexane vaporization.
4. **Solvent-to-Solids Ratio** — 1.0:1 to 1.5:1 for continuous; higher ratios improve driving force but waste solvent.
5. **Number of Extraction Stages / Counter-Current Stages** — more stages → lower residual oil.
6. **Extraction Time** — diminishing returns after ~60 min.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fick's First Law | $J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$ | kg/(m²·s) |
| 2 | Noyes–Whitney | $\frac{dM}{dt} = k_L \cdot A \cdot (C_s - C_b)$ | kg/s |
| 3 | **Extraction Efficiency** | $\eta_{ext} = \dfrac{O_{in} - O_{out}}{O_{in}} \times 100$ | **%** (Target: ≥ 95%) |
| 4 | Residual Oil in Meal (ROM) | $ROM = \dfrac{O_{out}}{M_{out}} \times 100$ | **% w/w** (Target: < 1%) |
| 5 | Miscella Concentration | $C_{miscella} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{oil} + m_{hexane}} \times 100$ | **% w/w** (18–30%) |
| 6 | Solvent-to-Flakes Ratio (S:F) | $S/F = \dfrac{m_{hexane}}{m_{flakes}}$ | dimensionless (1.0–1.5) |
| 7 | Oil Density (soybean) | $\rho_{oil} \approx 920$ | kg/m³ |
| 8 | Hexane Boiling Point | $T_b$ | **68.7 °C** |
| 9 | Hexane Latent Heat | $\lambda_{hexane}$ | **~365 kJ/kg** |
| 10 | Material Balance — Oil | $M_{in} \cdot O_{in} = M_{out} \cdot O_{out} + V_{miscella} \cdot C_{miscella}$ | kg basis |
| 11 | Mass-Transfer Coefficient Correlation | $Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \, Re^{1/2} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Diffusion Time Scaling | $t_{diff} \propto L^2 / D$ | seconds |

> [!CAUTION]
> When writing the modulus $\vert x \vert$ inside any table cell of a KTU answer script rendered in markdown, use the LaTeX command $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — never the pipe character `|` — to avoid breaking the table row syntax. This is a **direct examiner-compliance** instruction.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Solvent extraction is the **backbone of the global vegetable-oil industry**. The world's largest soybean plants process **up to 10,000 tonnes of soybean per day**, recovering >99% of the oil. Solvent extraction is preferred over *only* mechanical pressing because mechanical screw presses leave **6% – 12% residual oil** in the cake, whereas solvent extraction reduces this to **< 1%**. The recovered defatted meal becomes high-value animal feed (e.g., soybean meal has ~48% crude protein). Solvent recovery systems achieve >99.8% hexane recovery, and the energy required is roughly **80 – 120 kg of steam per ton of meal processed**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time Required for Oil Diffusion in a Flake (Fickian Model)

We model the flake as a *thin slab* of half-thickness $L$, initially containing uniform oil concentration $C_0$. At time $t=0$, both faces are exposed to a fresh solvent (concentration $\approx 0$ at the surface). The problem reduces to non-steady-state diffusion in a slab, governed by Fick's Second Law:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Boundary & Initial Conditions:**

$$C(x, 0) = C_0 \quad \text{for} \quad -L \le x \le +L$$

$$C(\pm L, t) = C_s \approx 0 \quad \text{(assuming perfect miscella renewal at the surface)}$$

**Step 1 — Non-dimensionalization:**
Let $\theta = \dfrac{C - C_s}{C_0 - C_s}$ and $\xi = \dfrac{x}{L}$, $\tau = \dfrac{D t}{L^2}$.

The PDE becomes the canonical heat-diffusion equation:

$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial \tau} = \frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial \xi^2}$$

**Step 2 — Separation of variables:** Assume $\theta(\xi, \tau) = X(\xi) \cdot T(\tau)$. Substituting and dividing by $XT$:

$$\frac{1}{T}\frac{dT}{d\tau} = \frac{1}{X}\frac{d^2 X}{d\xi^2} = -\lambda^2$$

(negative constant because $\theta$ decays with time).

**Step 3 — Solve the spatial ODE:**

$$\frac{d^2 X}{d\xi^2} + \lambda^2 X = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_n(\xi) = \cos\left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi \xi}{2}\right)$$

(odd-numbered eigenfunctions satisfy the symmetric boundary conditions $\theta(\pm 1, \tau) = 0$).

**Step 4 — Solve the temporal ODE:**

$$\frac{dT}{d\tau} + \lambda^2 T = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_n(\tau) = \exp\left(- \lambda_n^2 \tau\right)$$

**Step 5 — Superposition (Fourier series):**

$$\theta(\xi, \tau) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} A_n \cos\left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi \xi}{2}\right) \exp\left(- \lambda_n^2 \tau\right)$$

The coefficients $A_n$ are found from the initial condition $\theta(\xi, 0) = 1$ (uniform initial oil). Using orthogonality of cosines on $[-1, +1]$:

$$A_n = \frac{2}{(2n+1)\pi} \cdot (-1)^n$$

**Step 6 — Final solution (Carleman / Crank's slab solution):**

$$\theta(\xi, \tau) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{4}{(2n+1)\pi} (-1)^n \cos\left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi \xi}{2}\right) \exp\left(- \left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi}{2}\right)^2 \tau\right)$$

**Step 7 — Average oil concentration in the flake** (the measurable quantity — oil remaining in the meal): integrate over $\xi$ from $-1$ to $+1$:

$$\bar{\theta}(\tau) = \frac{1}{2} \int_{-1}^{+1} \theta(\xi, \tau) \, d\xi = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{8}{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2} \exp\left(- \left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi}{2}\right)^2 \tau\right)$$

Retaining only the first term ($n=0$) — valid for $\tau \ge 0.2$:

$$\bar{\theta}(\tau) \approx \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\left(- \frac{\pi^2}{4} \tau\right) = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\left(- \frac{\pi^2 D t}{4 L^2}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Result:** The time to reduce the *average* oil concentration by a factor $1/e$ (i.e., to 36.8% of original) is the **characteristic diffusion time**:

$$t_{char} = \frac{4 L^2}{\pi^2 D}$$

This is the **fundamental scaling law** of solvent extraction. Reducing flake thickness $L$ from 0.30 mm to 0.20 mm reduces $t_{char}$ by a factor of $(0.30/0.20)^2 = 2.25$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Material Balance Across a Counter-Current Extractor

Consider a continuous counter-current extractor with:
- $F$ = mass flow rate of *full miscella* leaving (kg/h), containing $F_o$ kg/h oil and $F_s$ kg/h hexane.
- $B$ = mass flow rate of *spent flakes* leaving (kg/h), containing $B_o$ kg/h residual oil and $B_s$ kg/h hexane (carry-over).
- $L$ = mass flow rate of *fresh solvent* entering (kg/h) ≈ pure hexane.
- $V$ = mass flow rate of *fresh flakes* entering (kg/h), containing $V_o$ kg/h oil.

**Overall mass balance (steady state):**

$$V + L = B + F$$

**Oil component balance (what we are tracking):**

$$V_o = B_o + F_o$$

**Solvent balance (hexane conservation):**

$$L + V_w = F_s + B_s$$

Where $V_w$ is the moisture originally in the flakes. For simplicity, assuming flakes enter dry:

$$L = F_s + B_s$$

**Extraction Efficiency (mass basis):**

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{F_o}{V_o} \times 100 = \frac{V_o - B_o}{V_o} \times 100$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A continuous extractor processes **500 kg/h** of soybean flakes containing **18% oil (w/w)** using **550 kg/h** of fresh hexane. The spent flakes leaving carry **0.7% oil** and **25% hexane** (both w/w, wet basis). Find:
1. Mass flow of spent flakes.
2. Mass flow of full miscella and its oil concentration.
3. Extraction efficiency.

**Given:**
- $V = 500$ kg/h
- $V_o = 0.18 \times 500 = 90$ kg/h (oil entering in flakes)
- $L = 550$ kg/h (fresh hexane)
- $B_o / B = 0.007$ (residual oil in spent flakes)
- $B_s / B = 0.25$ (hexane carry-over in spent flakes)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Let $B$ be the spent flake mass flow.** Mass fraction of solids in spent flakes:

$$x_{solids} = 1 - 0.007 - 0.25 = 0.743$$

Mass of *solids* (non-oil, non-hexane) entering flakes = $500 \times (1 - 0.18) = 410$ kg/h. This equals the mass of solids leaving in spent flakes (conservation):

$$0.743 \cdot B = 410 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = \frac{410}{0.743} = 551.82 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Oil leaving in spent flakes:**

$$B_o = 0.007 \times 551.82 = 3.86 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Miscella oil and solvent masses:**

$$F_o = V_o - B_o = 90 - 3.86 = 86.14 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$B_s = 0.25 \times 551.82 = 137.96 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Miscella flow rate (overall mass balance):**

$$F = (V + L) - B = (500 + 550) - 551.82 = 498.18 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Miscella composition:**

$$C_{miscella} = \frac{F_o}{F} \times 100 = \frac{86.14}{498.18} \times 100 = 17.29 \% \text{ oil (w/w)}$$

**Step 6 — Extraction efficiency:**

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{V_o - B_o}{V_o} \times 100 = \frac{90 - 3.86}{90} \times 100 = 95.7\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** The examiner awards **1 mark** each for: (i) correct identification of all 5 unknowns, (ii) correct setup of mass balance equations, (iii) correct arithmetic. Always show the *unit* on every numerical result.

## 3.3 Derivation: Hexane Recovery Steam Requirement

The full miscella ($F = 498.18$ kg/h) must be distilled to separate oil from hexane. Assume:
- Miscella is preheated to hexane boiling point (68.7 °C) in a heat exchanger (no steam cost).
- Hexane is vaporized at atmospheric pressure using steam (latent heat $\lambda_{hexane} = 365$ kJ/kg).
- Steam supplied at 3 bar has latent heat $\lambda_{steam} = 2137$ kJ/kg.

**Step 1 — Mass of hexane in miscella to vaporize:**

$$m_{hexane} = F - F_o = 498.18 - 86.14 = 412.04 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Heat required (assuming no sensible heat losses):**

$$Q = m_{hexane} \cdot \lambda_{hexane} = 412.04 \times 365 = 150{,}395 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of steam required:**

$$m_{steam} = \frac{Q}{\lambda_{steam}} = \frac{150{,}395}{2137} = 70.4 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Steam-to-meal ratio:**

$$\frac{m_{steam}}{m_{meal\_out}} = \frac{70.4}{551.82} = 0.128 = 12.8\% \text{ (w/w)}$$

This matches the industry benchmark of **80–120 kg steam per ton of meal processed** (i.e., 8%–12% on a per-meal basis). ✔

## 3.4 Stage Efficiency of Multi-Stage Counter-Current Extraction (Number of Ideal Stages)

Using the **Kremser equation** for solid–liquid leaching in $N$ counter-current ideal stages:

$$\frac{B_o - \text{(solids oil at equilibrium)}}{V_o - \text{(solids oil at equilibrium)}} = \frac{1}{E^N}$$

Where $E$ is the **extraction factor** per stage:

$$E = \frac{L}{V \cdot m_{distribution}}$$

For a dilute miscella with a distribution coefficient $m_{distribution} \approx 0.05$ (oil strongly prefers the solid phase at equilibrium) and $L/V = 1.1$:

$$E = \frac{1.1}{0.05} = 22$$

After $N = 4$ ideal stages:

$$E^N = 22^4 = 234{,}256 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Residual oil fraction} \approx 4.3 \times 10^{-6} \rightarrow \text{effectively zero}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why commercial extractors need only **5–7 stages** to achieve <1% residual oil — the extraction factor is highly favorable when hexane strongly dissolves oil.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Solvent Extraction Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flakes In] --> B[Pre-Preparation Section]
    B --> B1[Flaking Rolls]
    B1 --> B2[Cooker-Conditioner]
    B2 --> C[Extractor Vessel]
    L[Hexane Storage Tank] --> M[Solvent Heater]
    M --> C
    C --> D1[Full Miscella Out]
    C --> D2[Spent Flakes Out]
    D1 --> E[Miscella Pre-Evaporator]
    E --> F[Miscella Final Stripper]
    F --> G[Crude Oil to Refining]
    F --> F1[Hexane Vapor Recycle]
    D2 --> H[Desolventizer-Toaster DTDC]
    H --> I[Defatted Meal to Cooler]
    H --> H1[Hexane Vapor Recycle]
    F1 --> J[Solvent Vapor Condenser]
    H1 --> J
    J --> K[Solvent-Water Separator]
    K --> L
    K --> K1[Waste Water Treatment]
    I --> Z[Bagged Meal Dispatch]
```

**Reading Guide:**
- `A`–`C`: upstream flake preparation and main extraction.
- `C`–`G`: miscella distillation to recover crude oil.
- `C`–`I`: meal desolventizing to recover defatted meal.
- `F1`, `H1`, `J`, `K`: closed-loop solvent recovery (achieves > 99.8% hexane recovery).

## 4.2 Operational Sequence Inside a Continuous Rotocel Extractor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1: Fresh Flakes In] --> P2[Stage 2: Counter-Current Miscella]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3: Higher Strength Miscella]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4: Strongest Miscella Full Miscella Out]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5: Drainage Zone]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6: Final Drain and Spent Flakes Out]
    S1[Fresh Hexane In] --> S6
    S6[Hexane Enters at Discharge End] --> S5
    S5 --> S4
    S4 --> S3
    S3 --> S2
    S2 --> S1
```

**Reading Guide:** In a Rotocel, fresh hexane enters at the spent-flakes discharge end and *flows counter* to the rotating cells. This maintains a *high concentration gradient* at every stage, driving residual oil below **1%** within 5–7 stages.

flowchart TD

    Q2 --> Q3[Particle Surface Boundary]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**"What is solvent extraction? Mention any two advantages of solvent extraction over mechanical pressing for oil recovery from oilseeds."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Solvent extraction is a solid–liquid mass-transfer separation process in which a volatile organic solvent (typically food-grade **hexane**) is used to selectively dissolve and recover oil entrapped within the porous matrix of flaked oilseeds, after the bulk oil has been removed by mechanical pressing.

**Advantages over mechanical pressing (any 2):**
1. **Lower residual oil in meal (< 1%)** versus 6% – 12% in expeller cake → higher oil yield and better meal economics.
2. **Better for low-oil-content seeds** (e.g., soybean at ~18% oil) where pressing alone is uneconomical.
3. **Higher extraction efficiency (> 95%)** with relatively low energy input per kg of oil recovered.

> [!TIP]
> **Word-count Strategy:** A 3-mark answer should be **80–120 words** with **2 distinct numbered points** plus a definition. Examiners reject answers that are *only* a definition.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**"List the desirable properties of a solvent used for extraction of edible oils. Why is hexane the most widely used solvent despite its flammability?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Desirable properties (any 3):**
- High solvency power for triglycerides (high oil-holding capacity)
- Low boiling point for easy recovery (e.g., hexane 68.7 °C)
- Chemical inertness — must not react with oil or meal
- Non-toxic, non-corrosive, low cost, high purity
- Low latent heat of vaporization (lower energy cost)
- Selective — should NOT dissolve proteins, gums, or phosphatides

**Why hexane despite flammability:**
Hexane offers the *best combination* of (a) high oil solvency, (b) low boiling point enabling easy recovery, (c) chemical inertness, and (d) low cost. Its flammability is mitigated by **closed-loop systems, explosion-proof motors, inert-gas blanketing, and >99.8% solvent recovery**, making it the industry standard.

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Process & Material Balance Track*

**(a)** Describe the **principle of solvent extraction** for oilseeds. With a neat labeled diagram, explain the working of a **Bollman extractor**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (3 marks):** Solvent extraction is a solid–liquid leaching process where a non-polar solvent (hexane) selectively dissolves triglycerides from flaked oilseeds. The driving force is the concentration gradient between oil inside the porous seed matrix and oil in the surrounding miscella. Mass transfer occurs in three sequential steps: (i) solvent penetration into pores, (ii) solubilization of oil, and (iii) diffusion of miscella out of the particle.

**Bollman Extractor (4 marks):**
- **Type:** Continuous, percolation-type extractor with a *bucket-elevator* design.
- **Construction:** A long vertical chamber divided into two sections — an *upper extraction section* (where buckets filled with flakes descend through falling miscella) and a *lower drainage section* (where buckets rise through draining miscella).
- **Operation:** Fresh flakes are fed into the bucket at the top of the extraction section. As the buckets slowly descend, fresh hexane is sprayed at the bottom of the extraction section and flows *downward* (co-currently) through the descending buckets, dissolving oil. The full miscella is collected at the bottom. The buckets then enter the drainage section, where they rise and drain, with the drained miscella re-entering the extraction section as a counter-current. Spent flakes are discharged at the top.
- **Limitation:** Some co-current flow in the extraction section reduces overall efficiency compared to fully counter-current extractors (Rotocel, Loop).

**(b)** A continuous solvent extraction plant processes **1000 kg/h** of soybean flakes containing **20% oil (w/w)**. Fresh hexane flow rate is **1100 kg/h**. The spent flakes leaving contain **0.8% oil** and **28% hexane** (both w/w, wet basis). Calculate:
1. Mass flow rate of spent flakes.
2. Mass flow rate of full miscella.
3. Oil concentration in the full miscella.
4. Extraction efficiency. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $V = 1000$ kg/h, $V_o = 0.20 \times 1000 = 200$ kg/h, $V_s = 800$ kg/h (solids)
- $L = 1100$ kg/h
- $B_o / B = 0.008$, $B_s / B = 0.28$
- Solid mass leaving = 800 kg/h (conservation, assuming no soluble solids lost).

**Step 1 — Spent flake composition:**

$$x_{solids} = 1 - 0.008 - 0.28 = 0.712$$

$$B = \frac{800}{0.712} = 1123.6 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Oil in spent flakes:**

$$B_o = 0.008 \times 1123.6 = 8.99 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Miscella flow rate (overall mass balance):**

$$F = (V + L) - B = (1000 + 1100) - 1123.6 = 976.4 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Oil in miscella:**

$$F_o = V_o - B_o = 200 - 8.99 = 191.01 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Oil concentration in miscella:**

$$C_{miscella} = \frac{F_o}{F} \times 100 = \frac{191.01}{976.4} \times 100 = 19.56 \% \text{ (w/w)}$$

**Step 6 — Extraction efficiency:**

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{V_o - B_o}{V_o} \times 100 = \frac{200 - 8.99}{200} \times 100 = 95.5\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiner Warning):** Students often forget that the *solid mass* (non-oil, non-solvent) is conserved across the extractor. The solids in fresh flakes = 800 kg/h = solids in spent flakes. Solving without this *conservation-of-solids* equation leads to an *under-determined* system. The examiner will deduct **2 full marks** for this omission.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Equipment & Downstream Processing Track*

**(a)** Compare the construction, working, and applications of **Rotocel** and **Hildebrandt** extractors. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Rotocel | Hildebrandt (U-type) |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Continuous, percolation, horizontal rotating drum | Continuous, immersion-type, U-shaped conveyor |
| **Construction** | Cylindrical drum with 16–24 wedge-shaped cells rotating slowly above a fixed miscella reservoir | Two vertical legs connected by a horizontal conveyor; flakes immersed in miscella bath |
| **Working** | Each cell is filled with flakes at the top, then *rotates* through 5–7 miscella zones (counter-current). Spent flakes are dumped out at the end. | Flakes are conveyed by an internal screw and *immersed* in a flowing miscella; counter-current arrangement. |
| **Solvent Carry-Over** | Low (good drainage) | Higher (immersion) |
| **Residual Oil** | < 1% | 1–2% |
| **Application** | Soybean, sunflower, rapeseed (the industry workhorse) | Rice bran, cottonseed, low-oil or fine-textured seeds |
| **Capacity** | Up to 8000 TPD (tons per day) | Up to 1500 TPD |

**Key Differences:**
- Rotocel uses *percolation* (solvent drains through stationary flake bed) → better for hard, flaked seeds.
- Hildebrandt uses *immersion* (flakes submerged in solvent) → better for fragile, powdery, or pre-pressed material.

**(b)** Explain the working of a **Desolventizer-Toaster (DT)** with a neat flow diagram. How is hexane recovered from the spent meal in a **DTDC** (Desolventizer-Toaster-Dryer-Cooler)? *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Desolventizer-Toaster (DT) — Purpose:** The spent meal leaving the extractor carries 25% – 35% hexane (w/w) and ~25% moisture. The DT removes both hexane and moisture *simultaneously* by **indirect steam heating** and **direct steam sparging**, while *toasting* the meal to denature anti-nutritional factors (e.g., trypsin inhibitors in soybean).

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
- The meal enters the top of a vertical, multi-tray vessel.
- Each tray is steam-heated from below (indirect heating) and live steam is sparged from below (direct contact).
- As the meal is agitated and moved downward by sweep arms on each tray, the temperature rises progressively from ~60 °C at the top to ~105 °C at the bottom.
- Hexane + water vapor exits from the top of the DT and goes to a **condenser → separator** for hexane recovery.
- The "toasting" at 100–105 °C for 15–20 min denatures trypsin inhibitors and improves protein digestibility.
- The DTDC adds two further sections: a **Dryer** (reduces moisture from ~18% to ~12%) and a **Cooler** (reduces temperature to < 40 °C for safe bagging).

**Flow Diagram (Block form):**
`Spent Meal In → Top Tray (60°C) → Middle Trays (80–95°C, toasting) → Bottom Tray (105°C) → Discharge (~12% moisture) → Cooler → Bagged Meal`

**Hexane Recovery Loop (3 marks):**
1. Vapor from DT top → **Condenser** (cooled by cooling water) → liquid mixture of hexane + water.
2. Liquid → **Separator** (decantation tank) — hexane floats on water due to lower density (hexane $\rho \approx 655$ kg/m³, water 1000 kg/m³). Hexane is decanted and recycled to the solvent tank; the aqueous layer is sent to **waste-water treatment** (stripper to recover dissolved hexane).
3. Overall solvent recovery efficiency: **> 99.8%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write *only* "hexane is evaporated and condensed" — this is worth just **1 mark**. The examiner expects explicit mention of (i) indirect + direct steam heating, (ii) multi-tray design with progressive temperature rise, (iii) condenser–separator–recycle loop, and (iv) the *toasting* function for protein quality. Omitting any one of these costs **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Definition:** Solvent extraction = solid–liquid leaching using a non-polar organic solvent (hexane) to recover residual oil from oilseed flakes.
- [ ] **Why hexane?** High oil solvency, low BP (68.7 °C), chemically inert, low cost, easy recovery.
- [ ] **Governing equation:** Fick's First Law $J = -D \cdot dC/dx$ and Noyes–Whitney $\frac{dM}{dt} = k_L A (C_s - C_b)$.
- [ ] **Diffusion time scaling:** $t_{diff} \propto L^2 / D$ → thinner flakes = faster extraction (quadratic benefit).
- [ ] **Key process parameters:** 55–60 °C, 8–10% moisture, 0.20–0.30 mm flake thickness, S:F = 1.0–1.5, < 1% residual oil.
- [ ] **Equipment hierarchy:** Batch (Soxhlet) < Semi-continuous (Hildebrandt) < Continuous (Bollman / Rotocel / Loop).
- [ ] **Rotocel** = workhorse; **Hildebrandt** = best for rice bran / fine seeds; **Bollman** = co-current (less efficient).
- [ ] **Extraction efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{V_o - B_o}{V_o} \times 100$; target **≥ 95%**.
- [ ] **Miscella definition:** mixture of oil + hexane; concentration typically **18–30% oil (w/w)**.
- [ ] **Material balance pillars:** Total mass, oil component, solids (conserved!), solvent component — 4 equations for 4 unknowns.
- [ ] **Desolventizer-Toaster (DT):** indirect + direct steam, multi-tray, 100–105 °C toasting for 15–20 min.
- [ ] **DTDC** = DT + Dryer (→ 12% moisture) + Cooler (→ < 40 °C).
- [ ] **Solvent recovery loop:** Vapor → Condenser → Separator (decantation, $\rho_{hexane} < \rho_{water}$) → Recycle to solvent tank.
- [ ] **Industry benchmark:** 80–120 kg steam per ton of meal processed; > 99.8% hexane recovery.
- [ ] **Counter-current flow** maintains a high $(C_s - C_b)$ driving force at every stage → only 5–7 stages needed.
- [ ] **Noyes–Whitney insight:** as $C_b \to C_s$, driving force vanishes → last traces of oil are *disproportionately* hard to remove.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Day-Before-Exam Quick-Recall Mnemonic — "HOLD-ME-SAFE":**
> **H**exane 68.7 °C | **O**il in meal < 1% | **L**imit by internal diffusion | **D**iffusion time ∝ $L^2/D$ | **M**iscella 18–30% oil | **E**xtraction efficiency ≥ 95% | **S**team 80–120 kg/ton meal | **A**nti-nutritional factors destroyed in DT | **F**ick + Noyes–Whitney govern | **E**xtractor = Rotocel (workhorse).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preconditioning of Oilseeds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT403 - PROCESSING OF CEREALS, PULSES AND OILSEEDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Preconditioning of Oilseeds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Preconditioning of Oilseeds** refers to a series of preparatory unit operations performed on harvested oil-bearing seeds **prior to mechanical expelling or solvent extraction**, with the objective of altering the cellular, thermal, and moisture equilibrium of the seed matrix to maximize oil recovery, improve pressed cake quality, reduce wear on extraction equipment, and enhance downstream refining efficiency.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCFTT403, Module 4), the preconditioning chain typically comprises the following sequential stages:

1. **Storage and Drying** (moisture reduction to safe levels — generally **<10% w.b.**)
2. **Cleaning and Grading** (removal of foreign matter, stones, ferrous contaminants)
3. **Dehulling / Decortication** (removal of outer fibrous husk for selected seeds)
4. **Size Reduction** (crushing, grinding, rolling)
5. **Flaking** (lamination of crushed seed into thin flakes ~0.2–0.4 mm)
6. **Cooking / Conditioning** (thermal-moisture treatment at 80–110 °C with controlled moisture 8–12%)
7. **Extrusion / Expanders** (high-shear thermomechanical pre-treatment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> Module 4 of PCFTT403 specifically targets *Preconditioning of Oilseeds* as a standalone unit worth **15–20%** of the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). Mastery of the *cooking curve*, *flake thickness*, *moisture-temperature interaction*, and *extruder operating principles* is mandatory.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍽️ Real-World Analogy — "Making Tea from Herbs":**
> Imagine you want to extract maximum flavor from dried ginger or tea leaves. If you throw whole, dry pieces into hot water, the extraction is *slow and incomplete* — the cell walls are intact and the moisture inside is locked. However, if you first **crack** the ginger, **moisten** it slightly, and **warm** it gently, the cells swell, rupture, and release their inner contents. When you then steep it, you get a richer, faster extraction.
> Preconditioning of oilseeds is the **industrial equivalent** of this "swelling + rupture + softening" trick — but applied to soybean, groundnut, sunflower, mustard, or rapeseed before oil pressing.

In every oilseed, oil exists inside microscopic **spherosomes/oil bodies** wrapped in cell walls made of cellulose, hemicellulose, and pectin. Preconditioning **destroys these walls**, **lowers oil viscosity**, **coagulates proteins** (preventing emulsification), and **drives moisture to optimal levels** so that the oil flows out cleanly under pressure.

---

### 1.3 Significance Metrics (Industry Standards)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Safe storage moisture | **<10% w.b.** | FAO Codex standards |
| Flake thickness (solvent extraction) | **0.20–0.40 mm** | Bailey's Industrial Oil & Fat Products |
| Cooking temperature | **80–110 °C** | AOCS Process Standards |
| Cooking moisture | **8–12%** | KTU/Industry standard |
| Residual moisture before extraction | **2–3% (solvent)** | Solvent extraction economics |
| Foots/foaming (over-cooked) | **>5%** | Indicates protein denaturation |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stage-by-Stage Logic Breakdown

#### Stage 1: Storage and Drying
- **Why?** Freshly harvested seeds have moisture **15–30% w.b.** — favorable for mold, lipase activity (free fatty acid rise), and self-heating.
- **How?** Hot-air drying, often in **deep-bed dryers** or **rotary dryers**, reduces moisture to **<10% w.b.** for safe storage.
- **Engineering logic:** Drying follows a *falling-rate* period after an initial constant-rate period. Bound water in hygroscopic seeds is governed by sorption isotherms.

#### Stage 2: Cleaning and Grading
- **Why?** Stones, sticks, metallic fragments damage expellers; weed seeds lower oil quality.
- **How?** Combination of **scalper reels** (large impurities), **aspirators** (light impurities), **magnetic separators** (Fe), and **gravity tables / destoners** (density-based).
- **Key metric:** Cleaning efficiency $\geq 99\%$ removal of foreign matter.

#### Stage 3: Dehulling / Decortication
- **Why?** Hulls contain waxes, pigments, and crude fiber that reduce oil quality, increase refining losses, and absorb oil in cake.
- **How?** Mechanical impact (e.g., **palm kernel cracker**, **soybean cracker-aspirator**), pneumatic separation of husk from kernel using specific gravity or aerodynamic differences.
- **Application-specific:** Applied to **groundnut, sunflower, soybean, cottonseed**. Not used for **mustard, sesame (whole)**.

#### Stage 4: Size Reduction (Crushing & Grinding)
- **Why?** Reduces particle size to expose oil cells and prepare material for flaking.
- **How?** **Hammer mills**, **roller mills**, or **cracking rolls** with fluted rolls.

#### Stage 5: Flaking (Roller Milling into Flakes)
- **Why?** Flakes rupture **lipid-bearing cells**, increasing oil drainage surface area.
- **How?** **Smooth roller mill** compresses seed into flakes of thickness **0.2–0.40 mm** for solvent extraction. For mechanical pressing, thicker flakes (**0.5–1.0 mm**) are preferred.
- **Key formula:** Flake thickness $t \propto \dfrac{\text{gap between rolls}}{\text{compression ratio}}$.

#### Stage 6: Cooking / Conditioning
- **Why?** This is the *heart* of preconditioning. Cooking achieves:
  1. **Protein coagulation** → prevents emulsification during pressing.
  2. **Cell wall rupture** → releases oil.
  3. **Moisture equilibration** → reduces oil viscosity.
  4. **Membrane lysis** → disrupts oleosin-bound oil bodies.
- **How?** **Stack cookers** (vertical, multi-tray steam-jacketed vessels) or **horizontal rotary steam-tube cookers**. Seeds are heated to **80–110 °C** with moisture **8–12%** for **15–30 minutes**.
- **The "Cooking Curve":** A graphical plot of moisture × temperature × time. Each seed has an optimum.

#### Stage 7: Extrusion (Expanders)
- **Why?** Expander-extruder forms a *porous collared plug* that:
  - Ruptures remaining oil cells.
  - Reduces solvent retention in extractor.
  - Increases bulk density for percolation.
- **How?** Screw extruder with steam injection; mechanical shear raises temp to **130–150 °C** briefly.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $M_w = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % w.b. | Storage drying target |
| 2 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $M_d = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | % d.b. | Equilibrium humidity |
| 3 | Equilibrium moisture content | Henderson equation: $1 - M_e = \left[-\dfrac{K \cdot T \cdot \ln(\phi)}{A}\right]^{1/B}$ | dimensionless | Drying design |
| 4 | Flake thickness (typical) | $t_f = 0.20 - 0.40$ | mm | Solvent extraction |
| 5 | Cooking temperature (range) | $T_c = 80 - 110$ | °C | Stack cooker |
| 6 | Cooking moisture | $M_c = 8 - 12$ | % | Optimum range |
| 7 | Oil yield efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{seed} \cdot O_c} \times 100$ | % | Press performance |
| 8 | Residual oil in cake (solvent) | $R = 0.5 - 1.5$ | % | After extraction |
| 9 | Protein coagulation temperature | $T_p = 65 - 75$ | °C | Cooking rationale |
| 10 | Free fatty acid (FFA) increase (overcooked) | $\Delta FFA = k \cdot t \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | % | Quality indicator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The cooking curve is the single most-asked concept in KTU exams for this module. A **poorly cooked seed** has high residual oil in cake (loss), while an **over-cooked seed** shows dark color, high FFA, and increased refining losses. Always plot the *bell-shaped* cooking response curve.

### 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Soybean processing plants** in India (e.g., Adani Wilmar, Cargill) precondition 5,000–10,000 tonnes/day. Each 1% loss in extraction efficiency translates to crores of revenue loss.
- **Cold-pressed oil** units deliberately **skip the cooking stage** to retain natural antioxidants (tocopherol) — this is a *consumer-driven* deviation from industrial standard.
- **Solvent extraction plants** require flake porosity $\geq 0.4$ to allow hexane percolation; expanders guarantee this.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Drying Rate and Equilibrium Moisture

The moisture content of oilseeds during drying follows the **thin-layer drying equation**:

$$\dfrac{dM}{dt} = -k (M - M_e)$$

**Step 1:** Separate variables:

$$\dfrac{dM}{M - M_e} = -k \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Integrate from initial moisture $M_0$ at $t = 0$ to moisture $M$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{M_0}^{M} \dfrac{dM}{M - M_e} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral:

$$\ln \left( \dfrac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} \right) = -k t$$

**Step 4:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$M(t) = M_e + (M_0 - M_e) \, e^{-k t}$$

**Step 5:** Engineering interpretation:

At $t \to \infty$, $M \to M_e$ (the seed equilibrates with drying air humidity). The drying constant $k$ depends on air temperature, velocity, and seed type.

**Step 6:** Numerical example — Soybean drying:

Given: $M_0 = 18\%$ (w.b.), $M_e = 6\%$ (w.b.), $k = 0.25$ per hour. Find moisture after $t = 4$ hours.

$$M(4) = 6 + (18 - 6) \cdot e^{-0.25 \times 4}$$

$$M(4) = 6 + 12 \cdot e^{-1.0}$$

$$M(4) = 6 + 12 \cdot 0.3679$$

$$M(4) = 6 + 4.41 = 10.41\% \text{ w.b.}$$

**Conclusion:** After 4 hours of drying, the soybean moisture has reduced from 18% to ~10.4% — close to safe-storage level.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cooking Curve — Optimal Moisture × Temperature

The **effective oil yield** $\eta_o$ after cooking follows a **parabolic relationship** with moisture and temperature:

$$\eta_o (M_c, T_c) = \eta_{max} - a (M_c - M_{c,opt})^2 - b (T_c - T_{c,opt})^2$$

**Step 1:** Identify typical optimums for soybean:
- $M_{c,opt} = 10.5\%$
- $T_{c,opt} = 95 \, °C$
- $\eta_{max} = 96\%$

**Step 2:** Substituting into the expression:

$$\eta_o = 96 - a (M_c - 10.5)^2 - b (T_c - 95)^2$$

Let $a = 0.6$ and $b = 0.02$ (industry-derived penalty constants).

**Step 3:** Evaluate for typical cooking condition $M_c = 11\%$, $T_c = 100 \, °C$:

$$\eta_o = 96 - 0.6 \times (0.5)^2 - 0.02 \times (5)^2$$

$$\eta_o = 96 - 0.6 \times 0.25 - 0.02 \times 25$$

$$\eta_o = 96 - 0.15 - 0.50$$

$$\eta_o = 95.35\%$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate for over-cooked condition $M_c = 14\%$, $T_c = 120 \, °C$:

$$\eta_o = 96 - 0.6 \times (3.5)^2 - 0.02 \times (25)^2$$

$$\eta_o = 96 - 7.35 - 12.50$$

$$\eta_o = 76.15\%$$

**Conclusion:** Over-cooking causes a **20% drop** in oil yield efficiency due to protein degradation, oil darkening, and emulsion formation — this is why KTU examiners frequently test the "cooking curve bell shape."

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Preconditioning Stage

| Stage | Critical Variable | Effect of Too Low | Effect of Too High | Engineering Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Drying | Moisture <10% | Brittleness, dust, fire risk | Mold, FFA rise | Rotary/dryer controller |
| Cleaning | Foreign matter <1% | Equipment damage | Contaminated oil | Aspirator + magnetic separator |
| Dehulling | Hull removal >90% | Fiber in oil | Kernel loss, oil loss | Air classification |
| Flaking | Thickness 0.2–0.4 mm | Poor percolation | Crumbly, oil drainage poor | Roller gap control |
| Cooking | Moisture 8–12% & T 80–110 °C | Hard to press, high residual oil | Dark oil, FFA up, emulsion | Stack cooker trays |
| Extrusion | Bulk density 0.45–0.55 g/cc | Loose percolation | Compact, slow solvent flow | Die & screw RPM |

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Free Fatty Acid (FFA) Kinetics During Improper Storage

FFA formation follows **first-order kinetics**:

$$\dfrac{d[FFA]}{dt} = k_{lipase} \cdot [TG]$$

**Step 1:** Integrate with triglyceride $[TG] \approx$ constant at early stages:

$$[FFA](t) = [FFA]_0 + k_{lipase} \cdot [TG] \cdot t$$

**Step 2:** Using Arrhenius temperature dependence:

$$k_{lipase} = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

**Step 3:** Combining:

$$[FFA](t) = [FFA]_0 + A \cdot [TG] \cdot t \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

**Step 4:** Quantitative impact:

At $T = 30 \, °C = 303 \, K$, FFA doubles in ~7 days for damaged seeds.
At $T = 10 \, °C = 283 \, K$, FFA doubling takes ~30 days — confirming the necessity of drying + low-temperature storage.

This is why **moisture control** is the *primary* preconditioning step — it suppresses lipase activity, not just mold.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Preconditioning Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Oilseed Harvest 15-30% moisture] --> B[Receiving and Weighing]
    B --> C[Drying Stage]
    C --> D[Moisture Conditioning to 10% wb]
    D --> E[Cleaning and Grading]
    E --> F[Scalper Reel Removal]
    E --> G[Aspirator Light Impurities]
    E --> H[Magnetic Separator Fe Removal]
    E --> I[Destoner Stones]
    F --> J[Optional Dehulling Decortication]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Cracking Rolls]
    K --> L[Flaking Smooth Roller Mill 0.2-0.4 mm]
    L --> M[Cooking Conditioning Stack Cooker]
    M --> N[Temperature 80-110 deg C]
    M --> O[Moisture 8-12%]
    N --> P[Optional Extrusion Expanders]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Ready for Mechanical Pressing or Solvent Extraction]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Cooking Process Detail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Crushed Seed Input] --> S2[Steam-Jacketed Tray 1]
    S2 --> S3[Tray 2 Heating]
    S3 --> S4[Tray 3 Moisture Equilibration]
    S4 --> S5[Tray 4 Cooking 95 deg C]
    S5 --> S6[Output Flakes to Expeller or Extractor]
    S6 --> S7{Check FFA and Color}
    S7 -->|OK| S8[Proceed to Extraction]
    S7 -->|Over-cooked| S9[Reject and Cool for Downgrade]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Cooking Curve (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time and Temperature] --> B[Cell Wall Rupture Begins]
    B --> C[Optimum Yield Region Plateau]
    C --> D[Over-cooking Onset]
    D --> E[FFA Rises, Color Darkens]
    E --> F[Yield Drops Sharply]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Preconditioning Selection by Seed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P{Which Oilseed?}
    P --> S1[Soybean]
    P --> S2[Groundnut]
    P --> S3[Sunflower]
    P --> S4[Mustard or Sesame]

    S1 --> R1[Dehull YES, Crack YES, Flake YES, Cook YES]
    S2 --> R2[Dehull YES, Crack YES, Flake YES, Cook YES]
    S3 --> R3[Dehull YES, Crack YES, Flake YES, Cook YES]
    S4 --> R4[Dehull NO, Flake YES, Cook YES]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Always emphasize that **mustard** and **sesame** seeds have *very small* kernels and are typically not dehulled — only cleaned, flaked lightly, and cooked gently. This is a frequent 3-mark short-answer question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model QP]**
**Define preconditioning of oilseeds. List any four operations involved in it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Preconditioning refers to the series of preparatory unit operations carried out on oilseeds before oil extraction, in order to improve oil yield and quality.

Four operations are:
1. Cleaning and grading
2. Drying / moisture conditioning
3. Dehulling / decortication
4. Flaking and cooking / conditioning

*(1 mark for definition, 0.5 mark each for listing = 3 marks total)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model QP]**
**What is the optimum cooking temperature and moisture for soybean? Why is cooking essential?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- Optimum cooking temperature: **95 °C** (range 80–110 °C) — *1 mark*
- Optimum cooking moisture: **10–11%** — *1 mark*
- Cooking is essential to:
  - Coagulate proteins (prevent emulsification)
  - Rupture cell walls (release oil)
  - Reduce oil viscosity
  - Inactivate enzymes (lipase, lipoxygenase)
  - *1 mark for any two reasons*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **flaking operation** in oilseed preconditioning. Describe the equipment used, operating parameters, and the role of flake thickness in solvent extraction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **cooking/conditioning process** with reference to stack cookers. Draw and explain the cooking curve. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Flaking Operation (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Flaking is the process of converting crushed/cracked oilseeds into thin, uniform flakes using smooth roller mills, as a critical step before cooking or solvent extraction.

**Equipment (2 marks):**
- **Smooth roller mill** (5-roll or 7-roll stand).
- Rolls rotate at differential speeds to shear material.
- Hydraulic gap adjustment for thickness control.
- Feeding via vibratory feeder for uniform distribution.

**Operating parameters (2 marks):**
- Flake thickness: **0.20–0.40 mm** for solvent extraction; **0.5–1.0 mm** for mechanical pressing.
- Moisture before flaking: **10–12%** (too dry → flaky, too wet → sticky rolls).
- Roll surface speed: 200–400 m/min.
- Capacity: 50–500 tonnes/day per mill stand.

**Role of flake thickness in solvent extraction (2 marks):**
- Thinner flakes → greater surface area → faster oil dissolution by hexane.
- Too thin → flakes crumble → poor percolation bed → channeling.
- Too thick → solvent penetration inadequate → high residual oil in marc.
- Optimum balances drainage and oil extraction kinetics.

**(b) Cooking / Conditioning Process (7 marks):**

**Equipment — Stack Cooker (2 marks):**
- Vertical stack of 5–7 horizontal steam-jacketed trays.
- Each tray has a sweep arm / agitator to move material.
- Steam pressure typically **4–6 bar**, jacket temperature 110–130 °C.
- Residence time per tray: 5–8 minutes; total 30–45 minutes.
- Material flows top → bottom by gravity.

**Process Description (2 marks):**
1. Crushed/cleaned seeds enter top tray.
2. Steam heats material to **80–110 °C**.
3. Moisture injected/regulated to **8–12%**.
4. Proteins coagulate, cells rupture, oil viscosity drops.
5. Material discharged at bottom ready for pressing/extraction.

**Cooking Curve Explanation (3 marks):**
The cooking curve plots oil yield vs. cooking time/temperature.

- **Stage 1 (Initial):** Yield rises sharply as cells rupture and moisture equilibrates.
- **Stage 2 (Optimum plateau):** Maximum yield achieved at ~10% moisture and ~95 °C.
- **Stage 3 (Over-cooking):** Yield drops due to:
  - Protein charring (dark color)
  - FFA increase from thermal hydrolysis
  - Emulsion re-formation
  - Oil oxidation

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students often draw the cooking curve with a *linear* increase, but it is **bell-shaped (parabolic)**. Awarding full marks (3/3) requires showing both the **rising limb**, the **plateau**, and the **falling limb** clearly. Skipping the falling limb loses 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**(a)** Describe the **storage and drying** of oilseeds. Why is moisture control critical for storage stability? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle and operation of **expander-extruders** in modern oilseed processing. What advantages do they offer over conventional cooking? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Storage and Drying (7 marks):**

**Storage Principles (2 marks):**
- Oilseeds stored in silos, warehouses, or flat godowns.
- Maintain moisture <10% (w.b.) to prevent mold and lipase activity.
- Aeration prevents hot spots.
- Fumigation against insects (Sitophilus, Tribolium).
