**Drying Methods (2 marks):**
- **Hot-air drying** in deep-bed dryers (batch or continuous).
- **Rotary drum dryers** for high-capacity plants.
- **Solar drying** for small-scale rural storage.

**Drying Kinetics (1 mark):**

$$M(t) = M_e + (M_0 - M_e) \, e^{-k t}$$

**Why Moisture Control is Critical (2 marks):**
- **Lipase activation:** Above 12% moisture, lipase hydrolyzes triglycerides → FFA rises → oil quality drops.
- **Mold growth:** Aspergillus, Penicillium proliferate, producing **aflatoxins** (carcinogenic).
- **Self-heating:** Biological respiration + metabolic heat → fire risk in silos.
- **Respiration losses:** Dry matter loss via $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\text{O}_2 \to 6\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

**(b) Expander-Extruders (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
- Screw-type extruder with steam injection and die restriction.
- Mechanical shear + steam → temperature **130–150 °C** for ~30 seconds.
- Material exits die as **porous collared plug** with bulk density **0.45–0.55 g/cc**.

**Operation (2 marks):**
1. Cooked flakes fed into extruder hopper.
2. Steam injected at 2–4 bar along barrel.
3. Screw rotates at 200–400 rpm; material compresses.
4. Die at outlet forms collared pellets or expanded cake.

**Advantages over Conventional Cooking (3 marks):**
1. **High bulk density** → greater extractor capacity, less solvent volume.
2. **Ruptures all remaining cells** → residual oil drops below 1%.
3. **Pre-drains oil** in mechanical expellers following expanders.
4. **Better solvent percolation** in extractors.
5. **Energy efficient** — short residence time reduces thermal degradation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute prep)**

- ✅ Preconditioning = Drying → Cleaning → Dehulling → Cracking → Flaking → Cooking → Extrusion.
- ✅ Storage moisture target: **<10% w.b.**; Flake thickness: **0.2–0.4 mm**.
- ✅ Cooking temperature: **80–110 °C**; Moisture: **8–12%**.
- ✅ Cooking curve is **bell-shaped (parabolic)** — has rising limb, optimum plateau, falling limb.
- ✅ **Protein coagulation** (~65–75 °C) is the key event in cooking — prevents emulsification.
- ✅ **Lipase activity** is the primary driver of FFA rise in poorly stored seeds.
- ✅ **Mustard & sesame** are generally NOT dehulled (tiny kernels).
- ✅ **Soybean, groundnut, sunflower** are dehulled before extraction.
- ✅ **Expanders** increase bulk density and reduce residual oil below 1%.
- ✅ **Cold-pressed oils** deliberately skip cooking to retain natural antioxidants.
- ✅ Henderson equation governs equilibrium moisture content in drying.
- ✅ FFA kinetics follow first-order Arrhenius behavior.
- ✅ Remember the formula $M(t) = M_e + (M_0 - M_e) e^{-k t}$ — frequently tested in KTU derivations.
- ✅ Always show the **over-cooking penalty** (FFA rise + dark color) in cooking curve diagrams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refining of Oil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT403 - PROCESSING OF CEREALS, PULSES AND OILSEEDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Refining of Oil

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Oil Refining

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refining of Oil** is defined as a series of purification processes applied to crude vegetable oil obtained after extraction (mechanical pressing and/or solvent extraction) to remove undesirable non-glyceride impurities such as gums, free fatty acids (FFA), pigments, waxes, trace metals, and odoriferous compounds, yielding an edible-grade oil with improved stability, appearance, flavor, and shelf-life that conforms to **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** and **Codex Alimentarius** standards.

The crude oil as extracted from oilseeds (soybean, sunflower, groundnut, mustard, coconut, palm, rice bran, sesame, etc.) is essentially a **triacylglycerol (TAG)** matrix contaminated with a wide spectrum of minor constituents. Refining is **NOT** a single step; it is a **multi-stage unit operation train** that sequentially targets different classes of impurities.

## 1.2 Classification of Impurities in Crude Oil

| Impurity Class | Chemical Nature | Effect on Oil | Removed By |
|----------------|-----------------|---------------|------------|
| Phosphatides (Gums) | Phospholipids (Lecithin, Cephalin) | Foaming, dark colour, sludge during frying | **Degumming** |
| Free Fatty Acids (FFA) | Carboxylic acids (R-COOH) | Off-flavour, increased acidity, smoke | **Neutralization (Alkali Refining)** |
| Pigments | Carotenoids, Chlorophyll, Gossypol | Undesirable dark colour | **Bleaching** |
| Waxes | Long-chain esters, hydrocarbons | Cloudiness at low temperatures | **Winterization / Dewaxing** |
| Odour compounds | Aldehydes, Ketones, Hydrocarbons | Unpleasant smell & taste | **Deodorization** |
| Trace metals | Fe, Cu, Ni (from seeds/processing) | Catalyse oxidation, reduce shelf-life | **Bleaching & Refining** |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Dirty Water to Clean Water" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you have muddy, oily water from a puddle. To make it drinkable, you would (1) let the heavy mud settle, (2) strain through cloth, (3) add alum to coagulate fine particles, (4) filter through sand/charcoal, and (5) boil to remove odour. **Oil refining works exactly the same way** — it is a sequential cleaning train where each station removes a specific category of "dirt" from the oil:
> - **Degumming = Settling/Mud removal** (removes heavy phospholipids)
> - **Neutralization = Alum treatment** (neutralizes acidic impurities)
> - **Bleaching = Sand/charcoal filter** (absorbs colour bodies)
> - **Deodorization = Boiling/steam treatment** (drives off volatile odours)

## 1.4 Objectives of Refining

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The five major objectives, in KTU examination order:
> 1. **Removal of non-glyceride impurities** for stability and safety
> 2. **Improvement of organoleptic properties** (colour, flavour, odour)
> 3. **Enhancement of oxidative stability** and shelf-life
> 4. **Reduction of FFA** below permissible limits (**< 0.25%** for refined oil as per FSSAI)
> 4. **Production of by-products** (soap stock, gums, spent earth, deodorizer distillate) of commercial value

## 1.5 Standard Quality Metrics for Refined Oil

The following **FSSAI / Codex** limits are critical for KTU numerical and short-answer questions:

- **Free Fatty Acid (as oleic):** $\leq 3.0\%$ for crude, $\leq 0.25\%$ for refined
- **Peroxide Value (PV):** $\leq 10$ meq O$_2$/kg oil
- **Iodine Value (IV):** Varies by oil (sunflower: 100–145, coconut: 7–12)
- **Saponification Value (SV):** Coconut ~ 255, Palm ~ 200, Soybean ~ 190
- **Moisture & Volatile Matter:** $\leq 0.1\%$
- **Colour (Lovibond):** Refined oil typically 1.5–3.0 Red (1 inch cell)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Refining Sequence — Operational Breakdown

### Step 1: DEGUMMING (Removal of Phosphatides)

**Why:** Phosphatides are surface-active agents that cause emulsification with water, leading to **foaming, dark colour development, and increased refining loss** during alkali treatment.

**Principle:** Phospholipids are **hydratable** when mixed with water — they swell, become insoluble, and can be separated by centrifugation.

**Process parameters:**
- **Oil temperature:** $60 - 80^\circ C$
- **Water/Citric acid addition:** 1–3% of oil weight
- **Citric acid concentration:** 30–50% (for non-hydratable gums)
- **Reaction time:** 15–30 minutes with slow agitation
- **Separation:** Centrifugation at $5000–7000$ rpm
- **By-product:** **Lecithin (crude/bleached)** — commercial emulsifier

> [!NOTE]
> **Water Degumming vs. Acid Degumming vs. Enzymatic Degumming:**
> - *Water degumming* removes only hydratable phosphatides ($\sim 80\%$ of total)
> - *Acid degumming* (using **phosphoric acid** at 0.05–0.1% or **citric acid**) converts non-hydratable phosphatides (calcium/magnesium salts) into hydratable form
> - *Enzymatic degumming* (e.g., **Lecitase Ultra, Rohalase**) uses phospholipase enzymes; offers the lowest phosphorus content (< 5 ppm) and minimal loss

### Step 2: NEUTRALIZATION / ALKALI REFINING (Removal of FFA)

**Why:** FFA is corrosive, causes soapy off-flavour, and decreases smoke point. The reaction with alkali converts FFA into **soap (sodium salt)** that is water-insoluble and separable.

**Core chemical reaction:**

$$\text{R-COOH} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{R-COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{Free Fatty Acid} + \text{Sodium Hydroxide} \longrightarrow \text{Soap} + \text{Water}$$

**Process parameters:**
- **Lye concentration:** **8–14 °Bé (Baumé)** for most oils; **12–18 °Bé** for high-FFA oils
- **Lye temperature:** $60-75^\circ C$
- **Oil temperature:** $60-75^\circ C$
- **Excess alkali:** 0.1–0.5% over theoretical (termed **'treat'** or **'caustic excess'**)
- **Centrifugation:** $5000–7000$ rpm
- **Washing:** $1-2$ hot water washes ($90^\circ C$) to remove residual soap
- **Drying:** Vacuum drying at $90^\circ C$, 50–100 mbar

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soap-stock** is a major by-product; it is acidulated with sulfuric acid to produce **acid oil** used in soap industry, animal feed, and oleochemistry.

### Step 3: BLEACHING (Removal of Pigments & Trace Metals)

**Why:** Carotenoids (yellow/orange), chlorophyll (green), gossypol (yellow-brown in cottonseed oil) must be removed for visual appeal and oxidative stability.

**Principle:** Physical adsorption onto a porous, activated adsorbent — typically **Natural Earth (NF — Neutral Fuller's Earth)**, **Activated Earth (A-NF — Acid-Activated)**, or **Activated Carbon**.

**Process parameters:**
- **Bleaching earth dosage:** 0.5–2.5% w/w of oil
- **Temperature:** $90-110^\circ C$ (under vacuum: 50–100 mbar)
- **Time:** 15–30 minutes
- **Agitation:** Slow, to prevent adsorbent fracturing
- **Filtration:** **Niagara filter** or **leaf filter** (precoat with diatomaceous earth)

**By-product:** Spent bleaching earth (15–25% oil) — recovered by solvent extraction.

### Step 4: DEODORIZATION (Removal of Volatiles & Off-Flavours)

**Why:** Removes aldehydes, ketones, hydrocarbons, and any residual free fatty acids imparting odour and flavour. It is essentially a **high-vacuum, high-temperature steam distillation** of the oil.

**Process parameters:**
- **Temperature:** $220-260^\circ C$
- **Pressure:** $1-5$ mbar (high vacuum)
- **Steam injection:** 1–3% of oil weight (sparging steam)
- **Time:** 60–120 minutes
- **Equipment:** **Batch Deodorizer, Continuous Semi-Continuous Deodorizer, Packed Column Deodorizer**

> [!NOTE]
> **Deodorizer Distillate (DD)** is a valuable by-product rich in tocopherols (Vitamin E), sterols, squalene, and fatty acid distillates — used in nutraceuticals and cosmetics.

### Step 5: WINTERIZATION / DEWAXING (Optional — for Sunflower, Rice Bran, Corn Oils)

**Why:** Waxes and high-melting TAGs cause **cloudiness** when oil is refrigerated.

**Principle:** Slow cooling causes waxes to crystallize, then separation by **filtration** or **centrifugation**.

**Process parameters:**
- **Cooling rate:** $0.5-1^\circ C$/hour
- **Final temperature:** $5-10^\circ C$
- **Holding time:** 12–24 hours
- **Crystal modifier:** Use of seeding crystals (e.g., dewaxing aids like **Talyte**)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Formula / Expression | Symbols Explained | Unit | Use |
|------|----------------------|--------------------|------|-----|
| 1 | $\text{Theoretical Alkali} = 0.142 \times \text{FFA\%} \times \frac{V_o}{W_o}$ | $V_o$ = oil volume, $W_o$ = oil weight | L, kg | Lye volume for FFA neutralization |
| 2 | $\text{Total Lye} = \text{Theoretical} + \text{Excess (0.1–0.5%)}$ | Excess compensates neutral oil loss | mL | Total caustic volume |
| 3 | $\text{Refining Loss \%} = \dfrac{W_{soap} + W_{gum} + W_{bleached}}{W_{crude}} \times 100$ | $W$ = weights of each stream | % | Material balance in refinery |
| 4 | $\text{FFA \%} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W_{sample}}$ | $V$ = titrant, $N$ = normality, $W$ = sample | % by wt | Acid value determination |
| 5 | $\text{Acid Value (AV)} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W_{sample}}$ | KOH basis for AV | mg KOH/g | Standard oil quality test |
| 6 | $\text{FFA (as oleic)} = \dfrac{\text{AV}}{2}$ | Conversion factor | % | Quick AV-to-FFA conversion |
| 7 | $\text{SV} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 56.1}{W_{sample}}$ | $V_b$ = blank, $V_s$ = sample | mg KOH/g | Saponification value |
| 8 | $\text{IV} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 12.69}{W_{sample}}$ | Wij's method for iodine | g I$_2$/100g | Unsaturation degree |
| 9 | $\text{PV (meq O}_2\text{/kg)} = \dfrac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 1000}{W_{sample}}$ | Iodometric titration | meq/kg | Oxidative rancidity index |
| 10 | $\text{Peroxide Index} = \text{PV after aging} - \text{PV before}$ | Difference method | meq/kg | Shelf-life prediction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Constants to Memorize:**
> - **$56.1$** = Molecular weight of KOH (g/mol)
> - **$28.2$** = Equivalent weight of oleic acid ÷ 10 (used for FFA)
> - **$0.142$** = Empirical constant relating FFA% to NaOH (in some forms using **40°Bé lye** = 18.7% NaOH, this factor derives from molar ratios)
> - **12.69** = Wijs iodine constant for I$_2$ per 100 g sample

## 2.3 Physical vs. Chemical Refining — Comparison

| Parameter | Chemical Refining | Physical Refining |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Order | Degumming → Neutralization → Bleaching → Deodorization | Degumming (acid) → Bleaching → Deodorization (with FFA stripping) |
| FFA removal | Via alkali → soap | Via high-temperature vacuum steam distillation |
| Suitable for | Low-to-medium FFA oils (< 5%) | High-FFA oils (palm, rice bran > 5%) |
| Loss | Higher (1.5–2.5× FFA) | Lower (~1.0–1.2× FFA) |
| Product | Light colour, neutral flavour | Slightly darker, requires high-grade bleaching |
| By-product | Soap stock | Deodorizer distillate (high-value) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Alkali Requirement for FFA Neutralization

**Problem Context:** Given an oil with **FFA = 4.0% (as oleic)** and batch size **$W_o = 1000$ kg**, calculate the theoretical volume of **20°Bé NaOH solution** required.

**Step 1 — Identify molar relationships:**

The reaction stoichiometry is:

$$\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{33}\text{COOH} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{33}\text{COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Molecular weight of oleic acid:**

$$M_{oleic} = 282.46 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalents of FFA in 1000 kg oil:**

$$\text{FFA mass} = 0.04 \times 1000 = 40 \text{ kg} = 40000 \text{ g}$$

$$\text{Moles of FFA} = \frac{40000}{282.46} = 141.62 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric NaOH needed (1:1 molar ratio):**

$$\text{Moles of NaOH} = 141.62 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mass of pure NaOH ($M_{NaOH} = 40$ g/mol):**

$$\text{Mass NaOH} = 141.62 \times 40 = 5664.8 \text{ g} = 5.665 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — Account for NaOH purity and lye concentration:**

For **20°Bé lye** (specific gravity 1.16, NaOH content ~12% by weight):

$$\text{Volume of 20°Bé lye} = \frac{5.665}{0.12 \times 1.16} = \frac{5.665}{0.1392}$$

$$\text{Volume} = 40.69 \text{ L}$$

**Step 7 — Add 0.3% excess (typical industrial practice):**

$$V_{total} = 40.69 \times 1.003 = 40.81 \text{ L}$$

**Final Answer:** $\approx$ **40.8 L of 20°Bé NaOH solution** is required to neutralize 1000 kg of oil containing 4.0% FFA.

## 3.2 Derivation: Refining Loss Calculation (Material Balance)

**Problem:** Process **10,000 kg crude oil** with **FFA = 5%**. After refining:
- Soap stock formed = **650 kg**
- Gums removed (degumming) = **120 kg**
- Spent earth (recovered oil) = **80 kg**
- Bleached/dried oil loss = **50 kg**

**Step 1 — Total mass of impurities removed:**

$$W_{removed} = W_{soap} + W_{gum} + W_{bleached}$$

$$W_{removed} = 650 + 120 + 50 = 820 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Refining Loss Percentage:**

$$\text{Refining Loss \%} = \frac{W_{removed}}{W_{crude}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Refining Loss \%} = \frac{820}{10000} \times 100 = 8.2\%$$

**Step 3 — Compare with theoretical expectation (rule of thumb: Refining Loss = 1.5–2× FFA%):**

$$\text{Expected Loss (lower)} = 1.5 \times 5 = 7.5\%$$

$$\text{Expected Loss (upper)} = 2.0 \times 5 = 10.0\%$$

**Conclusion:** The actual 8.2% falls within the expected range, indicating a well-controlled refinery operation.

## 3.3 Derivation: Acid Value from FFA and Vice Versa

**Given:** FFA (as oleic) = 2.5%

**Step 1 — Recall that FFA% directly equals acid value ÷ 2 (in oleic acid basis):**

$$\text{FFA (\% oleic)} = \frac{\text{Acid Value}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for Acid Value:**

$$\text{Acid Value} = 2 \times \text{FFA\%} = 2 \times 2.5 = 5.0 \text{ mg KOH/g}$$

**Step 3 — Verification using molecular weight ratio:**

$$\text{AV} = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$$

The relationship between FFA (as oleic, eq. wt = 282) and KOH (eq. wt = 56.1):

$$\frac{\text{eq. wt KOH}}{\text{eq. wt oleic}/10} = \frac{56.1}{28.2} = 1.989 \approx 2$$

This confirms the **2× conversion factor** used industrially.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Refining Stage on Oil Properties

| Property | Crude Oil | After Degumming | After Neutralization | After Bleaching | After Deodorization |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| FFA (% oleic) | 3.0 | 3.0 | **0.05** | 0.05 | **0.03** |
| Phosphorus (ppm) | 800 | **20** | 5 | 1 | 1 |
| Colour (Lovibond Red) | 12 | 12 | 10 | **2.0** | 1.8 |
| Peroxide Value (meq/kg) | 8 | 8 | 8 | 7 | **3** |
| Odour | Strong | Strong | Strong | Strong | **Neutral** |
| Tocopherol loss (%) | 100% (start) | 98% | 85% | 75% | **60%** |
| Loss of oil (%) | 0 | 1.5% | + 2.5% | + 0.5% | + 0.3% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Notice that the **maximum loss of tocopherols (Vitamin E)** occurs at the **deodorization** stage due to high-temperature stripping. This is why physical refining (which skips neutralization but requires harsh deodorization) tends to lose more natural antioxidants than chemical refining.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Determination of Iodine Value

**Sample weight = 0.25 g; Blank titre = 47.5 mL; Sample titre = 23.8 mL; Normality of Na$_2$S$_2$O$_3$ = 0.1 N**

**Step 1 — Difference in volume:**

$$V_b - V_s = 47.5 - 23.8 = 23.7 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Wijs formula:**

$$\text{IV} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 12.69}{W_{sample}}$$

$$\text{IV} = \frac{23.7 \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.25}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$23.7 \times 0.1 \times 12.69 = 30.075$$

**Step 4 — Divide by sample weight:**

$$\text{IV} = \frac{30.075}{0.25} = 120.3 \text{ g I}_2/\text{100g oil}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

An IV of 120 indicates a **semi-drying oil** (likely soybean, sunflower, or safflower).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATS

## 4.1 Complete Block Flow Diagram — Chemical Refining Train

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Oil Storage Tank] --> B[Pre-heater 60-80C]
    B --> C[Degumming Vessel with Water and Citric Acid]
    C --> D[Centrifuge 1 - Gum Separation]
    D --> E[Lecithin Recovery Tank]
    D --> F[Degummed Oil Holding Tank]
    F --> G[Alkali Neutralizer with NaOH 12-18Be]
    G --> H[Centrifuge 2 - Soap Stock Removal]
    H --> I[Soap Stock Acidulation Unit]
    H --> J[Oil Washing Column with Hot Water 90C]
    J --> K[Centrifuge 3 - Wash Water Removal]
    K --> L[Vacuum Dryer 90C, 50-100 mbar]
    L --> M[Bleacher with NF Earth 90-110C, Vacuum]
    M --> N[Niagara Filter for Spent Earth]
    N --> O[Bleached Oil Storage Tank]
    O --> P[Deodorizer 220-260C, 1-5 mbar, Steam]
    P --> Q[Deodorizer Distillate Collection]
    P --> R[Cooling and Polishing Filter]
    R --> S[Refined Oil Storage Tank]
    S --> T[Quality Lab QC Analysis]
    T --> U[Packing and Dispatch]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Physical Refining (Alternative Train)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Crude Oil] --> A2[Acid Degumming with Phosphoric Acid 0.1%]
    A2 --> A3[Centrifuge for Gum Removal]
    A3 --> A4[Bleaching with Activated Earth 0.5-1.5%]
    A4 --> A5[Niagara Filter]
    A5 --> A6[High-Vacuum Deodorizer 240-260C, 1-3 mbar]
    A6 --> A7[FFA Stripped to < 0.1%]
    A6 --> A8[Deodorizer Distillate - Tocopherols, Sterols]
    A7 --> A9[Cooling and Polishing]
    A9 --> A10[Refined Oil]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Quality Control Interconnections

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUTS[INPUT QUALITY CONTROL]
        QA1[Crude Oil Sampling at Unloading]
        QA2[FFA, PV, IV, Moisture, Colour, Phosphorus]
    end

    subgraph INPROCESS[IN-PROCESS QUALITY CHECKS]
        QB1[Post-Degumming P Test]
        QB2[Post-Neutralization FFA and Soap Test]
        QB3[Post-Bleaching Colour and Residual Earth]
        QB4[Post-Deodorization FFA, Flavour, PV]
    end

    subgraph PRODUCT[FINAL PRODUCT QC]
        QC1[FSSAI Compliance Check]
        QC2[Shelf-life Accelerated Test]
        QC3[Microbiological Analysis]
    end

    QA1 --> QA2
    QA2 --> QB1
    QB1 --> QB2
    QB2 --> QB3
    QB3 --> QB4
    QB4 --> QC1
    QC1 --> QC2
    QC2 --> QC3
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic — Choosing Chemical vs. Physical Refining

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Oil Analysis] --> B{FFA Content}
    B -->|Less than 3%| C[Chemical Refining Preferred]
    B -->|3 to 5%| D{Desired Product Quality}
    B -->|Greater than 5%| E[Physical Refining Recommended]
    D -->|High-grade neutral oil| C
    D -->|Cost-effective high volume| E
    C --> F[Degumming, NaOH, Bleaching, Deodorization]
    E --> G[Acid Degumming, Bleaching, High-Temp Deodorization with FFA Stripping]
    F --> H[Soap Stock By-product]
    G --> I[Deodorizer Distillate By-product]
```

## 4.5 Equipment Architecture — Continuous Deodorizer Cross-Sectional View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DEOD[CONTINUOUS DEODORIZER VESSEL]
        D1[Oil Inlet at Top - Tray 1] --> D2[Tray 2 - Steam Sparger]
        D2 --> D3[Tray 3 - Falling Film]
        D3 --> D4[Tray 4 - Final Stripping]
        D4 --> D5[Oil Outlet at Bottom - Polished]
        S1[Steam Inlet - 1 to 3 percent of Oil Weight] --> D2
        S1 --> D3
        S1 --> D4
        V1[Vacuum System 1-5 mbar] --> DEOD
        DD[Deodorizer Distillate Vapor] --> V1
    end
    DEOD --> COOL[Cooling Section 50C]
    COOL --> POL[Polishing Filter 1-3 micron]
    POL --> STORE[Refined Oil Storage]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Degumming. State its objectives. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Degumming** is the first step in oil refining, wherein **phosphatides (gums)** are removed from crude oil by hydration with water, acid, or enzymes.

**Objectives (3 points expected):**
1. **Removal of hydratable and non-hydratable phosphatides** (mainly lecithin and cephalin) to prevent emulsification and dark colour development during subsequent processing.
2. **Reduction of phosphorus content** to below **20 ppm**, which is essential to prevent catalyst poisoning in hydrogenation and to improve oil stability.
3. **Recovery of valuable lecithin** as a commercial by-product used as an emulsifier in food, pharmaceutical, and cosmetic industries.
4. **Minimization of refining loss** in subsequent alkali refining stage.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Chemical Refining and Physical Refining. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (in tabulated form):**

| Parameter | Chemical Refining | Physical Refining |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Sequence | Degumming → Neutralization → Bleaching → Deodorization | Acid Degumming → Bleaching → Steam Deodorization |
| FFA Removal | By alkali → soap | By high-temperature vacuum steam distillation |
| Suitable for | Low-FFA oils (< 5%) | High-FFA oils (> 5%) e.g. palm, rice bran |
| Refining Loss | Higher (1.5–2.5× FFA) | Lower (1.0–1.2× FFA) |
| By-products | Soap stock → acid oil | Deodorizer distillate → tocopherols |
| Colour quality | Excellent (lighter) | Slightly darker |
| Investment | Moderate | Higher (vacuum equipment) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern: Internal Choice between Q-A and Q-B, each 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks]**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam — July 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Describe the **Alkali Refining (Neutralization) process** of vegetable oil in detail. Include the chemical reaction, process parameters, and the principle of separation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Definition and Purpose:** Alkali refining is the chemical treatment of crude/degummed oil with a **caustic alkali (NaOH)** solution to **neutralize free fatty acids (FFA)** present in the oil, converting them into **soap** which is water-insoluble and easily separable by centrifugation.

**(ii) Core Chemical Reaction:**

$$\text{R-COOH} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{R-COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

(Free Fatty Acid + Sodium Hydroxide → Soap + Water)

**(iii) Process Parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Lye concentration | 8–18 °Bé |
| Lye temperature | 60–75°C |
| Oil temperature | 60–75°C |
| Excess alkali | 0.1–0.5% over theoretical |
| Agitation | Slow, 30–60 rpm |
| Reaction time | 10–20 minutes |
| Soap separation | Centrifuge at 5000–7000 rpm |
| Washing | 1–2 washes with hot water (90°C, 10–15% of oil) |
| Drying | Vacuum drying at 90°C, 50–100 mbar |

**(iv) Principle of Separation:** Soap formed has higher density than oil and is insoluble. Centrifugal force (G-force ~ 5000–7000×g) causes the heavy soap phase to migrate outward, while the lighter refined oil is discharged from the centre. Wash water removes trace residual soap from the oil.

**(v) By-products:** **Soap stock** — further acidulated with H$_2$SO$_4$ to produce **acid oil** for soap industry.

**(vi) Final Quality:** FFA reduced to **< 0.1%**, phosphorus < 5 ppm, soap < 50 ppm in washed oil.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **Deodorization process** of refined oil. What are the conditions and why is high vacuum essential? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Definition:** Deodorization is the **final steam-distillation step** in oil refining where volatile compounds responsible for **off-flavours and odours** (aldehydes, ketones, hydrocarbons, residual FFA) are removed from the bleached oil.

**(ii) Principle:** At high temperature and low pressure, the **partial pressure of volatile components is reduced**, allowing them to be stripped from the oil by sparging steam. The vapour–liquid equilibrium favours the transfer of low-molecular-weight compounds from the liquid oil phase to the steam phase.

**(iii) Process Conditions:**

- **Temperature:** $220 - 260^\circ C$
- **Pressure:** $1 - 5$ mbar
- **Steam injection:** $1 - 3\%$ of oil weight (food-grade dry steam, free of O$_2$)
- **Time:** $60 - 120$ minutes (batch); 15–30 min (continuous)
- **Equipment types:** Batch, semi-continuous, continuous (packed column)

**(iv) Why High Vacuum is Essential — Four Reasons:**

1. **Prevents thermal oxidation/polymerization** of oil at high temperatures. Without vacuum, oil would oxidize rapidly and form dimers/polymers.
2. **Reduces the boiling point of FFA and odour compounds** — at 3 mbar, FFA boils near $180^\circ C$ instead of $>300^\circ C$ at atmospheric pressure.
3. **Enables use of lower operating temperatures** than theoretical boiling points, preserving **nutrients (tocopherols, sterols)** and **preventing trans-fatty acid formation**.
4. **Reduces steam requirement** for effective stripping by maintaining steep vapour-pressure gradient.

**(v) By-product — Deodorizer Distillate (DD):** A high-value mixture of tocopherols, sterols, squalene, and fatty acid distillates — used in nutraceuticals.

**(vi) Final Quality:** Oil becomes **odourless, tasteless, and bland** — suitable for use as a cooking medium or salad oil.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE:** `[KTU University Exam — July 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Describe the **Bleaching process** of refined oil. Discuss the role of bleaching earth, process conditions, and types of equipment used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Purpose:** Bleaching is the **adsorptive removal of colour pigments** (carotenoids, chlorophyll, xanthophylls, gossypol) and **trace metal contaminants** (Fe, Cu, Ni) from neutralized oil using **adsorbent materials**.

**(ii) Adsorbents Used:**

| Type | Composition | Use |
|------|-------------|-----|
| Natural Earth (NF) | Calcium montmorillonite | Mild bleaching |
| Acid-Activated Earth (A-NF) | Bentonite activated with H$_2$SO$_4$ | Most commonly used |
| Activated Carbon | From coconut, wood | Strong decolourization, removes polycyclic aromatics |
| Silica (synthetic) | Amorphous SiO$_2$ | Removes soap, gums, trace metals |

**(iii) Process Conditions:**

- **Bleaching earth dosage:** $0.5 - 2.5\%$ w/w of oil
- **Temperature:** $90 - 110^\circ C$
- **Vacuum:** $50 - 100$ mbar (to prevent oxidation)
- **Time:** $15 - 30$ minutes
- **Agitation:** Slow, $30–60$ rpm (to avoid fracturing adsorbent)

**(iv) Equipment:**
- **Open/atmospheric bleacher** — used for non-sensitive oils
- **Vacuum bleacher** — most common; with closed vessel, steam heating, mechanical agitator
- **Niagara filter / leaf filter** — for separating spent earth
- **Pressure leaf filter** — closed system, prevents oil exposure to air

**(v) Mechanism:** Pigment molecules are **adsorbed** onto the large surface area (200–500 m²/g) of the activated clay. Acid activation creates micropores and increases surface acidity, improving adsorption capacity for basic pigments like chlorophyll.

**(vi) By-product:** Spent earth (containing 15–25% retained oil) — recovered by solvent (hexane) extraction.

---

**Q. (b)** Calculate the **theoretical and actual volume of 16°Bé NaOH solution** required to neutralize **5000 kg of degummed soybean oil** with **FFA = 2.5% (as oleic)**. Assume **0.2% excess** alkali is used. The **16°Bé lye** contains **10.06% w/w NaOH** with specific gravity **1.125**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate mass of FFA in oil:**

$$\text{Mass of FFA} = 0.025 \times 5000 = 125 \text{ kg} = 125000 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Molecular weight of oleic acid (C$_{17}$H$_{33}$COOH):**

$$M_{oleic} = 282.46 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Moles of FFA:**

$$n_{FFA} = \frac{125000}{282.46} = 442.5 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric moles of NaOH (1:1 ratio):**

$$n_{NaOH} = 442.5 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of pure NaOH ($M_{NaOH} = 40$ g/mol):**

$$W_{NaOH} = 442.5 \times 40 = 17700 \text{ g} = 17.7 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of 16°Bé lye required (10.06% NaOH):**

$$W_{lye} = \frac{17.7}{0.1006} = 175.94 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7 — Volume of 16°Bé lye (SG = 1.125):**

$$V_{lye} = \frac{175.94}{1.125} = 156.4 \text{ L}$$

**Step 8 — Add 0.2% excess:**

$$V_{actual} = 156.4 \times 1.002 = 156.71 \text{ L}$$

**Final Answer:** $\approx$ **156.7 L of 16°Bé NaOH solution** is required to neutralize 5000 kg of oil at 2.5% FFA with 0.2% excess.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Lead to Mark Deductions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing FFA% with Acid Value:** Students often forget the factor of 2 conversion. **Acid Value = 2 × FFA (as oleic)**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to add the excess alkali** in numerical problems — this is a **mandatory industrial practice** to compensate for neutral oil entrapment in soap stock. Examiner will deduct 1–2 marks if missed.
> 
> 3. **Not specifying the lye °Bé or NaOH concentration** in the problem — many students assume 1:1 stoichiometry directly, ignoring that industrial lye is a **dilute solution (8–18°Bé)**.
> 
> 4. **Misidentifying by-products:** In deodorization, by-product is **deodorizer distillate (DD)** rich in tocopherols, NOT soap stock. Soap stock comes from **neutralization**.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the role of vacuum** in deodorization — without specifying high vacuum (1–5 mbar), the explanation is incomplete and loses 2 marks.
> 
> 6. **Sequencing errors:** Students sometimes bleach before neutralizing — wrong order! The correct sequence is **Degumming → Neutralization → Bleaching → Deodorization**.
> 
> 7. **Confusing Winterization with Bleaching** — Winterization removes **waxes** (cloudiness), not colour.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST:**

- ☐ **Refining** is a multi-step purification of crude oil — NOT a single operation.
- ☐ The four major steps: **Degumming → Neutralization → Bleaching → Deodorization** (Mnemonic: **"D-N-B-D"** or **"Do Not Bleach Deodorized"**)
- ☐ **Degumming** uses water/acid to remove phosphatides; by-product is **lecithin**.
- ☐ **Neutralization (Alkali Refining)** uses **NaOH** to convert FFA → **soap**; by-product is **soap stock / acid oil**.
- ☐ **Bleaching** uses **activated earth (NF, A-NF)** and **activated carbon**; by-product is **spent earth**.
- ☐ **Deodorization** uses **high-temperature vacuum steam stripping** ($220–260^\circ C$, $1–5$ mbar); by-product is **deodorizer distillate (tocopherols)**.
- ☐ **Winterization/Dewaxing** is optional, for wax-containing oils (sunflower, rice bran).
- ☐ **Chemical refining** is preferred for low-FFA oils; **Physical refining** is preferred for high-FFA oils (palm, rice bran).
- ☐ **Theoretical alkali = 0.142 × FFA%** (empirical formula, depends on °Bé and excess).
- ☐ **FSSAI limit for FFA in refined oil ≤ 0.25%**; **PV ≤ 10 meq/kg**.
- ☐ **Acid Value (mg KOH/g) = 2 × FFA (% as oleic)**.
- ☐ **Iodine Value** measures unsaturation; **Saponification Value** measures average molecular weight.
- ☐ **Peroxide Value** is the indicator of oxidative rancidity.
- ☐ Refining loss typically = **1.5–2.5 × FFA%** in chemical refining.
- ☐ High vacuum in deodorization **prevents oil oxidation** and **lowers boiling point** of volatiles.
- ☐ The sequence **must be followed strictly** to avoid emulsion formation, dark colour entrapment, and excessive oil loss.

---

> **End of Notes — Refining of Oil | Module 4 | PCFTT403**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Examination Standards*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stabilization of Rice Bran, Processing of Soya Bean, Storage Structure, Bag Storage
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT403 - PROCESSING OF CEREALS, PULSES AND OILSEEDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stabilization of Rice Bran, Processing of Soya Bean, Storage Structure, Bag Storage

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stabilization of Rice Bran

### Formal Definition
**Rice Bran Stabilization** is the postharvest processing technique used to **inactivate the native lipase enzyme (and to a lesser extent, lipoxygenase and peroxidise)** in freshly milled rice bran, thereby arresting the rapid hydrolysis of the bran oil into **Free Fatty Acids (FFA)** and **glycerol**, which causes rancidity and makes the bran unsuitable for edible oil extraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The rice bran is the most unstable by-product in the rice milling industry. Within **2–4 hours** of milling, FFA can rise from **2–3%** to **5–7%**, and within weeks, it may exceed **60–70%** if untreated. Stabilization is, therefore, a *time-critical* process step.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of freshly cut rice bran like a **cut apple exposed to air**. Just as the apple's flesh turns brown within minutes due to enzymatic oxidation, rice bran becomes rancid within hours because of enzymatic lipolysis. The lipase enzyme is like a tiny pair of molecular scissors that snip the oil (triglyceride) molecules into smaller, smelly fragments. **Stabilization is essentially "blanching" or "cooking" the bran** to denature these scissors before they cause damage — analogous to how we freeze or heat-treat food to preserve freshness.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Moisture content of fresh bran:** typically **10–14%** (wet basis).
- **Critical moisture for safe storage:** below **4%** for long-term stability.
- **FFA threshold for refining feasibility:** generally below **5%** (some refineries accept up to **10%**).
- **Time window for stabilization:** ideally within **30–60 minutes** of bran generation.
- **Operating temperature range for thermal stabilization:** **100–130 °C** for short durations.
- **Standard enzyme inactivation kinetic constant (k):** depends on temperature, typically follows **first-order Arrhenius kinetics**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why stabilization is mandatory (KTU 2024 CO Mapping):**
> - CO2: Understand the role of enzymes in post-harvest losses.
> - CO3: Apply stabilization techniques to extend shelf life and improve oil recovery economics.

---

## 1.2 Processing of Soya Bean

### Formal Definition
**Soya bean processing** refers to the **sequence of unit operations** — cleaning, drying, cracking, dehulling, conditioning, flaking, and oil extraction (mechanical and/or solvent) — used to convert raw soybeans into **refined edible oil, defatted meal, protein concentrates, isolates, and various food-grade products** such as soymilk, tofu, and textured vegetable protein (TVP).

### Conceptual Analogy
Processing soya bean is like running a **multi-stage kitchen recipe** to extract the best ingredients from a legume. Imagine the soybean as a "nutty capsule" wrapped in a bitter, fibrous husk (hull), inside which lies a cotyledon rich in oil and protein. Each processing step is like peeling, soaking, mashing, and pressing in a home kitchen — but done at industrial scale with precision.

### Standard Quality Metrics for Soya Bean
- **Oil content:** **18–22%** (w/w)
- **Protein content:** **36–42%** (w/w)
- **Moisture (incoming):** **12–14%**
- **Moisture after conditioning:** **10–11%** for flaking.
- **Urease activity (trypsin inhibitor test):** should be reduced to **≤ 0.05–0.3 mg N/g** after cooking.
- **Beany flavor compound (hexanal):** eliminated by proper heat treatment.

---

## 1.3 Storage Structure (Grain Storage Structures)

### Formal Definition
**Storage structures** are the **engineered physical facilities** — ranging from traditional mud bins to modern steel silos — designed to **preserve the quality, quantity, and germinability** of harvested grains, oilseeds, and pulses by providing controlled environments against **moisture, insects, rodents, microorganisms, and temperature fluctuations**.

### Conceptual Analogy
A storage structure is to a grain what a **bank locker is to jewellery** — a secure, climate-controlled vault that protects your valuables from theft, decay, and damage over long periods.

### Standard Storage Targets
- **Safe moisture content for cereal grains:** **≤ 12–14%** (depending on grain type).
- **Safe storage temperature:** ideally **< 25 °C** with daily fluctuation < **3 °C**.
- **Hermetic storage O₂ threshold:** maintained below **2–5%** to kill insects.
- **Standard storage capacity units:** **MT (Metric Tonnes)**, **bags of 50/75/100 kg**.

---

## 1.4 Bag Storage

### Formal Definition
**Bag storage** is the **traditional and widely used method** of grain storage wherein cleaned, dried grains are packed into **burlap (jute), polypropylene (PP), or polyethylene-lined bags** and stacked systematically inside a warehouse, godown, or shed, usually in palletized or block-stack arrangements.

### Conceptual Analogy
Bag storage is like **organising books on a library shelf** — each book (bag) is an independent unit, easy to identify, count, and move, but the system depends on the quality of the individual unit and the discipline of stacking.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module Mapping:** Bag storage is part of the **traditional storage system** in Indian context. The Food Corporation of India (FCI) and Central Warehousing Corporation (CWC) use a mix of bag storage (≈ **65%**) and bulk storage (≈ **35%**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Methods of Rice Bran Stabilization

Stabilization methods are broadly classified into **three categories** based on the principle of enzyme inactivation:

### A. Thermal Stabilization (Most Common)

1. **Dry Heat Treatment (DHT)**
   - **Principle:** Hot air or steam indirectly contacts moist bran to denature lipase.
   - **Operating conditions:** **100–130 °C** for **15 seconds to 30 minutes** depending on moisture.
   - **Equipment:** Rotary drum heater, fluidized bed dryer, scraped-surface heat exchanger (ECOTRON).
   - **Advantages:** Continuous, scalable, high throughput.
   - **Disadvantage:** Risk of nutrient (tocopherol, oryzanol) degradation at high temperatures.

2. **Wet Heat Treatment (Steam Treatment)**
   - **Principle:** Direct steam injection raises moisture and temperature simultaneously, denaturing enzymes.
   - **Conditions:** **100 °C saturated steam for 3–15 minutes**.
   - **Equipment:** Steam-jacketed conveyor, autoclave-type batch stabilizers.
   - **Effect:** Reduces FFA to < 3% within 30 days of storage.

3. **Microwave / Infrared / Radio-Frequency Heating**
   - **Principle:** Volumetric heating — molecular dipole rotation.
   - **Conditions:** **2450 MHz, 600–1200 W** for **2–5 minutes**.
   - **Advantages:** Uniform heating, rapid, energy efficient.
   - **Disadvantage:** High capital cost; limited to high-value bran.

4. **Extrusion Cooking**
   - **Principle:** Combination of high temperature (**130–150 °C**), pressure (**20–40 bar**), and shear.
   - **Effect:** Lipase is fully inactivated; starch gelatinized; bran is partially cooked.
   - **Use:** Produces stabilized bran for direct food applications.

### B. Chemical Stabilization
- Use of antioxidants or enzyme inhibitors such as **BHA, BHT, TBHQ, citric acid, ascorbic acid, oryzanol, tocopherols**.
- Disadvantage: regulatory restrictions, consumer preference for "clean label" foods.

### C. Physical / Non-Thermal Methods
- **Drying to low moisture (< 4%)** — lipase is inactive in dry state.
- **High-Pressure Processing (HPP)** — 400–600 MPa.
- **Irradiation** — gamma rays at 5–10 kGy.

### D. Combination Methods (Modern Industry)
- **Heating + Moisture Adjustment + Cooling** — three-stage process:
  1. **Stage 1:** Heat bran to **100 °C** (enzyme denaturation).
  2. **Stage 2:** Reduce moisture to **5–7%**.
  3. **Stage 3:** Cool to **< 40 °C** to prevent reactivation and self-heating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 'How' and 'Why' this works:**
> - The lipase enzyme is a **globular protein** whose 3D structure (tertiary conformation) is essential for its catalytic activity.
> - Heating disrupts hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and disulphide bridges, causing **irreversible denaturation**.
> - Once denatured, the active site (catalytic triad Ser-Asp-His) cannot bind triglycerides, so lipolysis ceases.

### E. Kinetic Model of Lipase Inactivation
The inactivation of lipase follows **first-order kinetics**:

$$ \frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-k \cdot t} $$

Where:
- $N$ = residual enzyme activity at time $t$ (% of initial)
- $N_0$ = initial enzyme activity (100%)
- $k$ = inactivation rate constant (min⁻¹)
- $t$ = treatment time (minutes)

The temperature dependence of $k$ follows the **Arrhenius equation**:

$$ k = A \cdot e^{-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T}} $$

Where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor
- $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant (**8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

For rice bran lipase: $E_a \approx 80–120 \text{ kJ/mol}$.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Processing Steps of Soya Bean

The flowchart below summarizes the modern soya bean processing sequence:

| **Step No.** | **Unit Operation** | **Purpose** | **Key Parameters** |
|:------------:|:------------------|:------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | **Receiving & Storage** | Temporary bulk storage | Moisture < 12% |
| 2 | **Cleaning** | Remove foreign matter (stones, straw, dust) | Aspirator + magnetic separator |
| 3 | **Drying** | Reduce moisture to safe level | < 10% moisture |
| 4 | **Cracking / Dehulling** | Break cotyledons; remove hulls (≈ 8% of seed) | Roller cracker; aspirator |
| 5 | **Conditioning** | Adjust moisture/temperature for plasticity | Moisture 10–11%, 60–70 °C |
| 6 | **Flaking** | Convert cracked beans to thin flakes (0.25–0.4 mm) | Roller flaker |
| 7 | **Cooking** | Denature anti-nutritional factors | 100–110 °C, 15–20 min |
| 8 | **Mechanical Expelling** | Pre-press to remove bulk oil | Screw press, residual oil 15–18% |
| 9 | **Solvent Extraction** | Remove residual oil using hexane | Miscella 18–25%, full miscella; miscella distillation |
| 10 | **Desolventizing & Toasting** | Recover solvent, improve meal quality | DT + Desolventizer-Toaster (Schumacher type) |
| 11 | **Oil Refining** | Degumming → Neutralization → Bleaching → Deodorization | Standard edible oil refining |
| 12 | **Meal Processing** | Grinding, classification (Hi-Pro, Low-Pro) | Particle size control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-nutritional factors in soya bean that must be inactivated:**
> - **Trypsin inhibitors (Kunitz & Bowman-Birk)** — reduce protein digestibility.
> - **Lectins (soybean agglutinin)** — cause RBC agglutination.
> - **Urease** — hydrolyses urea to ammonia.
> - **Phytic acid** — binds minerals (Zn, Ca, Fe).
> - **Lipoxygenase** — causes "beany" off-flavor.
> Proper cooking/inactivation: Trypsin inhibitor reduction ≥ **80%**; Urease activity < **0.3 pH unit rise**.

---

## 2.3 Types of Storage Structures (Comprehensive)

### A. Traditional / Indigenous Structures (Rural Level)

| **Structure** | **Region** | **Material** | **Capacity** |
|:--------------|:-----------|:-------------|:-------------|
| **Morai / Puhar** | Tamil Nadu | Bamboo/straw + clay | 1–5 tonnes |
| **Kothar / Kuthla** | UP, Bihar | Mud + straw | 5–20 tonnes |
| **Khattal / Kharik** | Rajasthan | Stone slab + mud | 2–10 tonnes |
| **Bukhari** | Punjab, Haryana | Bamboo + mud | 1–2 tonnes |
| **Thekka / Hagevu** | Karnataka | Mud + stone | 5–15 tonnes |
| **Pathisa** | North India | Underground pit | 5–30 tonnes |

### B. Improved / Scientific Structures

1. **Covered Pusa Bin** (IARI design)
   - Galvanized iron sheet cylindrical bin, 3–6 tonnes capacity.
   - Fitted with **perforated floor aeration**.
   - Suitable for small farmers.

2. **PAU Bin (Punjab Agricultural University)**
   - Steel/iron construction, 1.5–5 tonnes.
   - Includes **moisture meter** and aeration ducts.

3. **Silo / Bulk Storage**
   - Cylindrical steel bins, capacities **100 to 10,000+ tonnes**.
   - Used by FCI, CWC, and modern flour mills.
   - Equipped with **aeration, temperature, and moisture sensors**.

4. **Warehouses / Godowns**
   - Rectangular, brick/steel construction, bag storage system.
   - Capacity 500–10,000 tonnes.

5. **Hermetic Storage Systems (Cocoons, Purdue Improved Crop Storage — PICS bags)**
   - Triple-layer hermetic bags for small-holder farmers.
   - Oxygen depletion (< 5%) kills insects without chemicals.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Be able to differentiate between **traditional, improved, and modern storage** structures and list their **relative advantages and disadvantages**.

---

## 2.4 Bag Storage — Principles and Practices

### Definition Refined
Bag storage involves packing dried grains in standard bags of **50 kg (public distribution), 75 kg (FCI norm for wheat), or 100 kg (private trade)** and arranging them in warehouse stacks following scientifically designed **stack patterns**.

### Stacking Patterns in Bag Storage

| **Pattern** | **Description** | **Stability** |
|:------------|:----------------|:-------------|
| **Pyramid / Dutch Cross** | Bags lean against each other, narrow at top | Moderate |
| **Block Stack** | Rectangular blocks of bags, all aligned | High stability, best for palletized |
| **Pinch Stack** | Alternate rows overlap, no wooden supports | Good for jute bags |
| **Silo-bag (in-bag storage)** | Long polyethylene tubes filled with grain | Newer, used in Argentina, Brazil |

### Bag Types and Their Properties

| **Bag Type** | **Material** | **Moisture Barrier** | **Reuse** | **Cost (₹/bag, approx.)** |
|:-------------|:-------------|:---------------------|:----------|:--------------------------|
| **Jute (Burlap)** | Jute fibre | Low — breathable | 3–5 times | ₹ 30–50 |
| **PP Woven** | Polypropylene | Low — breathable | 2–3 times | ₹ 20–35 |
| **HDPE Laminated** | Woven + film lining | Moderate | 2–3 times | ₹ 35–50 |
| **PICS Hermetic** | Triple-layer PE | Very high (gas-tight) | Multi-season | ₹ 100–150 |
| **Super Bag (FIBC)** | Woven PP, 1–1.5 tonne | Variable | 50+ times | ₹ 800–1500 |

### Standard Stack Specifications (FCI/CWC Guidelines)
- **Stack width:** **5–6 m**
- **Stack length:** **15–20 m** (depending on warehouse length)
- **Stack height:** **4.5 m (≈ 15 layers for 75 kg bags)**
- **Aisle width:** **0.6 m (main), 1.0 m (cross aisle)**
- **Wall clearance:** **0.6 m** minimum
- **Floor clearance:** **palettes 10–15 cm above floor** to prevent moisture wicking.

### Godown Sanitation and Storage Hygiene (Godown Hygiene Index)
The **Food Corporation of India** recommends a strict protocol:
- **Fumigation:** Aluminium phosphide tablets at **3 g/tonne** for 7-day exposure.
- **Inspection frequency:** Weekly during storage, daily during monsoon.
- **Pest control:** Prophylactic spray with **malathion 50% EC @ 1:100** on stack surfaces.
- **Rodent control:** Traps, bait stations, and weatherseal on entry points.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Equation / Expression** | **Variables / Units** | **Use Case** |
|:------|:---------------------|:-------------------------|:----------------------|:-------------|
| 1 | Lipase first-order inactivation | $N = N_0 e^{-k t}$ | $N$ (%), $k$ (min⁻¹), $t$ (min) | Stabilization design |
| 2 | Arrhenius dependence of $k$ | $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $E_a$ (J/mol), $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ | Predicting inactivation at $T$ |
| 3 | D-value (decimal reduction time) | $D = \ln(10)/k = 2.303/k$ | $D$ (min) | Time to reduce enzyme to 10% |
| 4 | Z-value (temperature for 10-fold D change) | $\log(D_2/D_1) = (T_1 - T_2)/Z$ | $Z$ (°C) | Thermal process design |
| 5 | FFA % (acid value calculation) | $\text{FFA as oleic} = \frac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}$ | $V$ = titrant vol (mL), $N$ = normality, $W$ = sample wt (g) | Quality of bran oil |
| 6 | Grain equilibrium moisture content (EMC) | $\text{EMC} = f(\text{RH}, T)$ via Henderson or Chung-Pfost eqn. | RH (%), T (°C) | Safe storage MC prediction |
| 7 | Stack volume calculation | $V_s = n \times (L \times W \times H)_{\text{bag}}$ | Bags $n$, dimensions (m) | Warehouse capacity |
| 8 | Bag stack capacity | $Q = \frac{V_s \times \rho_{\text{grain}}}{\text{bag mass}}$ | $Q$ (no. of bags), $\rho$ (kg/m³) | Godown planning |
| 9 | Aeration air volume | $Q_{air} = m_{grain} \times q_{spec}$ | $q_{spec} = 0.05–0.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{min/tonne}$ | Silo aeration design |
| 10 | Hermetic O₂ depletion rate | $\frac{dO_2}{dt} = -k_{resp} \cdot O_2$ | $k_{resp}$ (day⁻¹) | Insect kill time prediction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip (2024 Scheme):** The Henderson equation is a *favourite* KTU 2024 question. Be sure to write:
> $$ 1 - RH = \exp\left[-A (T + C) M^n\right] $$
> where $M$ = moisture content (% d.b.), $T$ = temperature (°C), $A$, $C$, $n$ are empirical constants for the grain.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rice Bran:** India is the world's **2nd largest rice producer** (130+ MT/year), producing ≈ **10 million tonnes of bran/year**. Even a **2% oil recovery improvement** translates to ₹ **200+ crores/year**.
- **Soya Bean:** India processes ~**10–12 million tonnes of soybean/year**, predominantly in Madhya Pradesh. The defatted soybean meal is the **backbone of India's poultry feed industry**.
- **Storage:** A **1% post-harvest loss in India** equals **₹ 50,000+ crores/year** — proper storage is a national economic priority.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time Required for Lipase Inactivation

**Given:** A rice bran sample has initial lipase activity $N_0 = 100\%$. The inactivation rate constant at the operating temperature is $k = 0.35 \text{ min}^{-1}$.

**Required:** Time $t$ to reduce activity to **5%** of original.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** State the first-order inactivation equation (assumed earlier).

$$ N = N_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t} $$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known values. The residual activity fraction is $N/N_0 = 0.05$.

$$ 0.05 = e^{-0.35 \, t} $$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides.

$$ \ln(0.05) = -0.35 \, t $$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the left-hand side using $\ln(0.05) = -2.9957$.

$$ -2.9957 = -0.35 \, t $$

**Step 5:** Solve for $t$.

$$ t = \frac{-2.9957}{-0.35} $$

$$ t = 8.56 \text{ minutes} $$

**Step 6 (verification):** Compute $N$ at $t = 8.56$ min.

$$ N = 100 \cdot e^{-0.35 \times 8.56} = 100 \cdot e^{-2.996} = 100 \cdot 0.0501 = 5.01\% \quad \checkmark $$

> **Result:** Approximately **8.6 minutes** at the chosen temperature are required to reduce lipase activity to **5%** of the initial value, which is generally considered acceptable for commercial stabilization.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: FFA Content (Acid Value Method)

**Given:** A 5 g sample of rice bran oil is titrated with **0.1 N KOH**. The volume of KOH consumed is **3.2 mL**.

**Required:** Calculate the **Free Fatty Acid (% as oleic acid)** and decide if the bran requires further stabilization.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Recall the formula:

$$ \text{FFA (\% as oleic)} = \frac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W} $$

**Step 2:** Identify the variables:
- $V = 3.2 \text{ mL}$
- $N = 0.1$
- $W = 5 \text{ g}$
- Molecular weight of oleic acid = **282 g/mol** → factor $28.2/10 = 2.82 \times 10$ corresponds to expressing as a percentage.

**Step 3:** Substitute the values.

$$ \text{FFA} = \frac{3.2 \times 0.1 \times 28.2}{5} $$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator.

$$ 3.2 \times 0.1 = 0.32 $$
$$ 0.32 \times 28.2 = 9.024 $$

**Step 5:** Divide by $W$.

$$ \text{FFA} = \frac{9.024}{5} = 1.8048 \% $$

**Step 6 (rounded):**

$$ \text{FFA} \approx 1.81\% $$

**Step 7 (interpretation):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Verdict:** FFA < **3%** indicates **fresh, well-stabilized bran** suitable for refining. FFA between 3–5% is acceptable; FFA > **5%** suggests inadequate stabilization, and FFA > **10%** requires chemical refining (high refining loss).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: D-Value and Z-Value Computation

**Given:** A pilot study shows that at $T_1 = 100 \text{ °C}$, the time to reduce lipase to 10% of original is $D_1 = 4.0 \text{ min}$. At $T_2 = 110 \text{ °C}$, $D_2 = 1.5 \text{ min}$.

**Required:** Calculate the **Z-value** for the inactivation process.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** State the Z-value definition: the temperature change required to alter the D-value by a factor of 10.

$$ \log_{10}\left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right) = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{Z} $$

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio of D-values.

$$ \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{1.5}{4.0} = 0.375 $$

**Step 3:** Take the log₁₀ of the ratio.

$$ \log_{10}(0.375) = -0.4260 $$

**Step 4:** Compute the temperature difference.

$$ T_1 - T_2 = 100 - 110 = -10 \text{ °C} $$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the Z-value equation.

$$ -0.4260 = \frac{-10}{Z} $$

**Step 6:** Solve for $Z$.

$$ Z = \frac{-10}{-0.4260} = 23.47 \text{ °C} $$

**Step 7 (interpretation):**

$$ Z \approx 23.5 \text{ °C} $$

> **Engineering Meaning:** A rise of **23.5 °C** in the stabilization temperature reduces the D-value (i.e., the time required for 90% inactivation) by a factor of 10. This is a typical Z-value range for enzymatic/biological inactivation in food systems.
